entities.summary: Caesar's eight-month campaign at Alexandria in pursuit of Pompey's killers (autumn 48 to spring 47 BC), with the dynastic intervention on behalf of Cleopatra against her brother Ptolemy XIII. Recorded in the Caesarian Bellum Alexandrinum.

The Roman defeat by the Gauls on the river Allia (traditionally 390/387 BC), which opened the way to the sack of Rome; the dies Alliensis remained a black day in the calendar. Cicero invokes it as a disaster more mournful than the capture of the City that followed from it.
The Theban victory over Sparta in 371 BC, by which Epaminondas broke Spartan hegemony; Cicero jokingly dates this letter '765 days after the battle of Leuctra' in mock-antiquarian style.
The Athenian victory over the invading Persians in 490 BC. Cicero recalls that Hippias, son of Pisistratus, fell there bearing arms against his own fatherland.
Caesar's conquest of Gaul (58-50 BC), in whose winter campaigns of 54-53 BC Trebatius served on Caesar's staff. Cicero asks Trebatius to keep him informed of its progress (Fam. 7.18.1).

The plot of L. Sergius Catilina against the consular government in 63 BC, exposed and crushed during Cicero's consulship. In Fam 5.6 Cicero calls himself "the avenger of conspiracy" (vindex coniurationis).

15 March 44 BC, the day Julius Caesar was assassinated in the Senate by Marcus Brutus, Cassius, and the other Liberators. Celebrated by Cicero as the restoration of liberty; in Fam. 9.14 he says his love for Brutus surged on that day.

Lists of citizens declared outlaw under Sulla in 82–81 BC; their lives forfeit and their property auctioned to the dictator's followers. Pro Roscio Amerino is the first oblique attack on the proscriptions in surviving Latin literature.

The festival of Ceres, 12-19 April, with games in the Circus (ludi Ceriales) climaxing on 19 April. Cicero dates his chance meeting with Curio on the Appian Way and the writing of the letter to the Cerialia (Att 2.12).

A moveable Roman festival of the crossroads-gods (Lares Compitales), held in early January; the occasion of Cicero's proposed walks with Atticus. Later notorious as the festival Clodius's henchman revived in 58 BC.

The Roman festival of the dead on 21 February, the closing day of the Parentalia, on which offerings were made at family tombs. Cicero uses it as a calendar marker for the day in February 49 BC on which the armies broke camp (Att. 8.14).
The ancient movable festival of the Latin League on the Alban Mount, presided over by the consuls; until it was held no major public business could go forward.

entities.canonical_name: Pompey's Games (dedication of his theatre, 55 BC)
entities.summary: The lavish games marking the dedication of Pompey's stone theatre in the Campus Martius, late September 55 BC: stage plays under Sp. Maecius Tarpa's curation, gladiators, two five-day venationes, ending with the famous slaughter of elephants whose distress drew the crowd's pity.
The festival of Liber (Bacchus) held on 17 March, on which Roman boys traditionally assumed the toga virilis.
Annual games in honour of Apollo, held in July with theatrical performances. At the games of 59 BC the actor Diphilus's lines against Pompey provoked a pointed popular demonstration.
The great Roman public games of September, in honour of Jupiter Optimus Maximus. The setting of the De Oratore conversation, in 91 BC.

Festival celebrated at Syracuse in honour of the Claudii Marcelli, hereditary patrons of the city since its capture by M. Claudius Marcellus in 212 BC. Verres abolished the Marcellia and instituted in its place the self-glorifying Verria (In Verrem 2.2).

Roman festival of the Matronalia, celebrated on the Kalends (1st) of March in honour of Juno Lucina, when husbands gave presents to wives and matrons. Cicero's closing 'an honour, then, to the Kalends of March!' in Fam. 9.22 alludes to it.

The festival of the Magna Mater (Cybele), held 4-10 April, presided over by the curule aediles; it featured ludi scaenici (theatrical performances) and was one of the most prestigious of the Roman public games. Cicero dates Fam. 2.11 to the first day of the Megalesia (4 April 50 BC).

Festival celebrated at the cities of Sicily in honour of Q. Mucius Scaevola, the conspicuously upright governor of Asia (94 BC), whose name became proverbial for proper provincial administration. Cicero invokes the Mucia as the standard against which Verres's conduct is measured (In Verrem 2.2).
21 April, the festival of Pales, pastoral goddess; the day Romans celebrated the founding of the city.
The festival of Minerva celebrated on 19 March (with following days), five days after the Ides of March.
The Roman festival of Quirinus, held on 17 February. Clodius set the trial day forward to it in 56 BC, and his gangs mustered for it.

The Roman winter festival of Saturn, opening 17 December and marked by feasting, gift-giving, and licence; Cicero stormed Pindenissus on its first morning and auctioned the spoils on its third day in 51 BC.

A public thanksgiving decreed by the Senate, ordering days of prayer and offerings at the temples to honour a military success; for a commander it was the customary prelude to (though not a guarantee of) a triumph. Cicero campaigned for one after his Cilician victories of 51 BC.
Roman festival of the boundary-god Terminus, held on 23 February; Cicero dates the receipt of Atticus's letter by reckoning from it.

Festival instituted in his own honour by Verres while propraetor of Sicily (73–71 BC), supplanting the ancestral Marcellia at Syracuse. Cicero treats the founding of the Verria as a culminating impiety of the propraetorship (In Verrem 2.2).

aliases: Aeneas as he fled from Troy
entities.summary: The Trojan hero, son of Anchises and Venus, who fled burning Troy carrying his father on his shoulders and the household gods at his side; ancestor of the Roman people and of the Julian house. The famous statue group at Segesta of Aeneas bearing Anchises was carried off by Verres (In Verrem 2.4).

Greek Asklepios, god of healing, son of Apollo. His celebrated temple at Agrigentum housed a statue of Apollo inscribed with the god's name in tiny silver letters — among the works stripped by Verres (In Verrem 2.4).

The nymph (in some accounts a she-goat) who nursed the infant Zeus on Crete; her name evokes abundance and the cornucopia, and Atticus named his Epirote shrine the Amaltheum (place:amaltheum) after her (Att. 1.16.15, 1.16.18).

Greek and Roman god of prophecy, music, and healing. Cicero invokes Apollo in connection with the sanctuary at Delos and, in In Verrem 2.1, the Delian temple plundered by Verres on his eastward journey before the praetorship.

The wine-god Dionysus/Bacchus; in Fam. 7.23 the plural 'Bacchae' denotes statues of his ecstatic female devotees (maenads), the figures Gallus bought for Cicero and compared with the Muses of Metellus.

Roman goddess of women, whose December rites were open only to women; Clodius's intrusion on them in 62 BC (disguised as a woman, in Caesar's house) was the sacrilege at the root of his feud with Cicero.
Calliope, the Muse of epic poetry; in book 3 of Cicero's De Consulatu Suo she delivers the exhortation to political virtue that Cicero treats as a personal oracle.

The Dioscuri, twin sons of Jupiter and Leda, patrons of cavalry and sailors. Their temple in the Roman Forum, refurbished by Verres as curator of public buildings, supplied the pretext for the contracting fraud anatomised in In Verrem 2.1.

Roman goddess of grain (Greek Demeter), worshipped at Henna in Sicily together with her daughter Libera (Proserpina); Sicily was held to be the goddess's especial home. The plunder of her shrine at Henna and the missing statue from Catina anchor the religious indictment of In Verrem 2.4.

The river-god Chrysas, worshipped at Assorus in Sicily where a small but exceptionally holy shrine stood beside the river. Verres's attempt to plunder it was beaten off by the Assorini in arms (In Verrem 2.4).

The one-eyed giants of Homeric and Sicilian myth, traditionally located in eastern Sicily about Aetna; Polyphemus the best known. Cicero's epithet 'second Cyclops' brands Verres a monster in his own Sicily (In Verrem 2.5).

Roman goddess of the hunt, the moon, and wild places (Greek Artemis). Her great cult-statue at Segesta — carried off by the Carthaginians, restored by Scipio Aemilianus after Carthage fell, then removed by Verres — is the standing exemplum of In Verrem 2.4, and her shrine at Perge in Pamphylia is named already at In Verrem 2.1.

Roman god of the underworld (Greek Pluto/Hades), who carried off Proserpina from the meadows near Henna in Sicily. The Hennan myth gives Cicero the geographical and theological frame for the desecration narrative of In Verrem 2.4.

Wife of Amphiaraus in Theban legend, bribed by the golden necklace of Harmonia to send her husband to his death at Thebes. A statue of Eriphyle was among the family heirlooms Verres stripped from the house of the prince Antiochus at Syracuse (In Verrem 2.4).
The goddess Fortune, personifying chance and destiny; in Fam. 8.14 Caelius says Fortuna was preparing a great spectacle for Cicero in the coming civil strife.
The avenging deities (Roman Furiae, Greek Erinyes) who pursue the guilty; Cicero invokes them as the spirits of discord he fears will dog him wherever he flees.

The hero-god Heracles, son of Jupiter, famed for his twelve labours. His celebrated bronze statue at Agrigentum, whose lips and chin were worn by the kisses of worshippers, was the object of a night assault by Verres's slaves repelled in arms by the Agrigentines (In Verrem 2.4).
Roman goddess of youth (the equivalent of the Greek Hebe), with a temple on the Capitol. Cicero notes at Att. 1.18.3 that her annual rites went unperformed in early 60 BC, an ill omen for the year.

Juno of Malta, in her great sanctuary on the island; one of the most ancient and respected shrines of the western Mediterranean, plundered by Verres's slaves on the very eve of his praetorship (In Verrem 2.4, with the earlier reference at 2.1).
Juno of Samos, in the celebrated Heraion outside the city — the oldest and richest of her Greek sanctuaries. Cicero lists its despoliation by Verres among his eastern outrages in In Verrem 2.1.
Chief god of the Roman state, with his temple on the Capitol; addressed by consul Cicero in the Catilinarians.
Mother of Apollo and Diana by Jupiter, who bore them on Delos after wandering. Her shrine on Delos and her statue at Segesta both figure among the holy places violated by Verres (In Verrem 2.1).

Roman Libera, identified with Greek Persephone/Proserpina, daughter of Ceres; carried off by Dis from the meadows by Henna in Sicily. Worshipped at Henna jointly with her mother, her cult is central to In Verrem 2.4.

The personified goddess Liberty. After Cicero's exile Clodius razed his Palatine house and consecrated the site with a shrine and statue of Libertas, a 'religious dedication' the College of Pontiffs ruled in 57 BC could be lifted, clearing the way for the house's restitution (Att. 4.1-4.2).
The Roman god of war; in Fam. 7.23 Cicero jokes that a statue of Mars is no use to him, the self-styled 'author of peace.'

The Great Mother of the Gods (Cybele), so called from Mount Ida in Phrygia; her cult was officially received at Rome from Pessinus in 204 BC during the Hannibalic War. Cicero names her sanctuary among the holy places Verres did not spare (In Verrem 2.5).

The Roman god Mercury, patron of commerce and gain; in Fam. 7.23 Cicero quips he would rather a Mercury had been among the statues, as the god of trade might have helped settle the bill with Avianius on better terms. Distinct from the specific Mercurius Tyndaritanus of the Verrines.

Mercury (Greek Hermes) in his cult at Tyndaris on the north coast of Sicily, where a bronze Mercury captured from Carthage had been restored to the city by Scipio Aemilianus. Verres compelled the Tyndaritans to surrender it and prosecuted those who resisted (In Verrem 2.4).

Roman goddess of wisdom and crafts (Greek Athena). Cicero invokes her Athenian Parthenon and her temple at Syracuse, whose paintings of the cavalry of Agathocles Verres ripped from the walls (In Verrem 2.1 and 2.4).
The Greek personification of mockery, blame, and captious fault-finding; Cicero invokes him for the carping critic whom his upright provincial conduct ought to silence.
The nine goddesses of the arts and letters; Cicero calls his literary work the service owed to 'our Muses,' a playful figure for the writing he and Tiro do together.

The Roman god Saturn; in Fam. 7.23 Cicero is glad no Saturn statue was bought, punning on 'aes alienum' (debt), since the temple of Saturn housed the state treasury and Saturn was associated with money/debt.

The twin perils of the Strait of Messina in Homeric legend — Charybdis the whirlpool, Scylla the dog-girt rock. Cicero turns the pair into a metaphor for Verres's seaborne predations on Sicily (In Verrem 2.5).

Eponymous hero of Tenedos, son of Cycnus and king of the island, killed by Achilles on the way to Troy. His sanctuary at Tenedos was stripped by Verres on the journey he made to his eastern provinces before the praetorship (In Verrem 2.1).

The Eleusinian hero whom Ceres sent through the world in a winged chariot teaching mankind to sow grain. As patron-figure of the Sicilian grain economy he stood appropriately at Henna; Cicero invokes him among the deities Verres violated (In Verrem 2.4).
The Muse of astronomy; Cicero's chosen interlocutor in book 2 of De Consulatu Suo and the autobiographical poem De Temporibus Suis.
Roman goddess of love and beauty; here the subject of Apelles's famous painting, the Venus rising from the sea (Anadyomene). Distinct from the cult-specific Venus Erycina (god:venus-erycina).

Venus of Eryx, in her great mountaintop sanctuary above Drepanum in western Sicily — one of the most famous shrines of the western Mediterranean, served by sacred slaves and the source of Sicilian revenue. Verres's exactions from the Erycine sanctuary slaves and his pillage of its ornaments figure throughout In Verrem 2 (2.1 and 2.4).
Goddess of the hearth; her cult was tended by the Vestal Virgins at the temple in the Forum.

The Roman personification of Victory, with a celebrated cult-image (a small gold Victoria) at Syracuse. The figurine torn from the hand of a great statue of Jupiter Imperator is one of the showpiece outrages of In Verrem 2.4.

Roman fire-god (Greek Hephaestus), husband of Venus; in Sicilian topography his forge was placed beneath Aetna or at Lipara. His shrine and statuary at Lipara and elsewhere figure among Verres's targets (In Verrem 2.2).

A powerful Gallic tribe of central Gaul, long-standing allies of Rome and officially styled 'brothers and kinsmen of the Roman people' (fratres). Cicero plays on this honorific at Fam. 7.10.4, calling them 'our brothers the Aedui.'

A large and powerful Gallic tribe inhabiting the territory between the Rhone, Isère, and the Alps (roughly modern Dauphiné and Savoy). Plancus stationed himself among them to block Antony's route after Mutina.
A powerful Belgic tribe of north-eastern Gaul (around modern Beauvais) who mounted a major revolt against Caesar in 51 BC. Rumour in Rome had Caesar besieged among them.

The warlike Illyrian people of the eastern Adriatic hinterland against whom Vatinius campaigned from Narona in 45 BC; Cicero curses them for the trouble they give Vatinius and predicts their capture will adorn his triumph.

The equestrian order (equites), the wealthy non-senatorial class of Rome from which jury-panels and the tax-farming publicani were largely drawn; the concordia between them and the senate (concordia ordinum) was the cornerstone of Cicero's political programme (Att. 1.14.4). Distinct from the publicani (institution:publicani), who are a subset.

Warriors who fought from the British and Gallic two-horse light war-chariot (essedum), reported on with much interest in Caesar's De Bello Gallico. Cicero jokes repeatedly in the Trebatius letters about Trebatius being tricked or attacked by them in Britain.

A Gallic (Celtic) people of the central Alpine plateau (modern Switzerland) whose threatened migration and raids in 60 BC alarmed Rome; their movement two years later furnished Caesar the pretext to open the Gallic War.

The four veteran legions stationed in Macedonia that Antony summoned to Italy in late 44 BC, going to Brundisium to win them over with money; two of them (the Martian and the Fourth) later defected to Octavian.
The Berber peoples of Mauretania in northwest Africa, prized as light cavalry; a contingent of Moorish horse served with Antony's forces at Forum Gallorum (Fam. 10.30).
A warlike Belgic tribe of north-eastern Gaul, near whom Quintus Cicero was wintering as Caesar's legate in 54 BC; Cicero is unsure of their exact whereabouts and distance.

The Roman gens Papiria (archaic spelling Papisii, before rhotacism), which had both patrician branches among the minores gentes (Mugillani, Crassi, Cursores, Massones) and plebeian branches (Carbones, Turdi). L. Papirius Paetus belonged to the gens; in Fam. 9.21 Cicero refutes Paetus's claim that the family was wholly plebeian.

A Roman family whose property (bona Plotiana) was confiscated by Caesar after the civil war and sold off; Marcus Laberius bought one lot of these estates. The individuals are not named; the group is significant only as the provenance of the contested property in Fam. 13.8.

A powerful Gallic (Belgic) people of the Moselle valley, restive against Caesar in 53 BC. Cicero puns on their name and capitalis (death-penalty/excellent) in warning Trebatius to avoid them (Fam. 7.13).
An Illyrian (Dalmatian) people in the region of Narona; in 45 BC Cicero's runaway slave-reader Dionysius was reported to be among them, and Vatinius undertook to hunt him down.
A Gallic people of the southern pre-Alps (around modern Vaison and Die), allied to Rome. Their friendly territory secured Plancus's line of retreat in 43 BC.

A plebeian branch of the gens Papiria bearing the cognomen Turdus ('thrush'), named in Fam. 9.21 alongside the Carbones as the plebeian Papirii whom Cicero advises Paetus to despise in favour of the patrician lines.
The Roman state treasury, housed in the temple of Saturn; under quaestorian custody.

The Areopagus was the ancient Athenian council of elders, named after the Hill of Ares (Ares pagos) where it met. In the Roman imperial period it retained authority over civic, religious, and property matters in Athens. A formal memorandum (hypomnematismos) from the Areopagus had apparently recorded rights over Epicurus's property; Cicero asks Memmius to have it rescinded.
A Roman religious college (collegium) charged with the games and cult of Jupiter on the Capitol (the Ludi Capitolini). In 56 BC it joined the Mercuriales in expelling M. Furius Flaccus.
Roman court of (originally) 105 men chosen by tribe, hearing inheritance and property cases. The setting of several of De Oratore's reference cases (M.' Curius and Coponius, etc.).

The college of augurs, the priestly body charged with interpreting the auspices; both Cicero and Appius Claudius Pulcher were members, and Cicero invokes its rule barring co-optation of an enemy of any member (Fam. 3.10.9). Distinct from the college of pontiffs.
Chief Roman priestly college, headed by the pontifex maximus; jurisdiction over sacral law, including consecration of property.

The Roman voting assembly organised by property-classes into voting units called centuries; it elected consuls and praetors and required prior auspices. In Fam. 7.30 the presiding magistrate, having taken the auspices for the tribal assembly, irregularly used them to hold the centuriate elections and proclaim Caninius consul.

The Roman voting assembly organised by the thirty-five tribes; it elected the lower (quaestorian and aedilician) magistrates. In Fam. 7.30 the magistrate took the auspices for the tribal elections (for quaestors) and then exploited them to convene the centuriate assembly the same morning.

The decurions, members of the local senate (ordo decurionum) of a Roman municipium or colony, the governing council that managed civic affairs and property. In Fam. 13.76 Cicero addresses them together with the quattuorviri of the town.

The philosophical school (hairesis) founded by Epicurus, holding that pleasure is the highest good and that perception works by physical images (eidola). In Fam. 15.16 Cicero jokingly treats Cassius's recent conversion to Epicureanism as an interdict-style ejection from the school.

Roman patrician (later plebeian) gens whose most famous member, L. Minucius Augurinus, was honoured with a column outside the Porta Trigemina in 439 BC for distributing cheap grain. Cicero invokes the gens as a foil for Verres's hereditary disgrace (In Verrem 2.1).
A Roman college (collegium) of merchants associated with the cult of Mercury and his temple by the Circus Maximus. In 56 BC it joined the Capitolini in expelling M. Furius Flaccus.
The sovereign assembled body of citizens; legislator and elector, addressed by Cicero from the Rostra as Quirites.

The companies of Roman tax-farmers (societates publicanorum) who held the state contracts for collecting provincial taxes and also functioned as bankers; with them at Ephesus Cicero deposited the lawfully gathered surplus of his Cilician province (Fam. 5.20).

The college of fifteen priests charged with guarding and consulting the Sibylline Books and overseeing foreign cults at Rome. Membership was a coveted lifelong sacerdotal honour; Dolabella was co-opted into it in 51 BC.

An inner committee of five drawn from the twenty land commissioners of Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC. Cicero seizes on a hint that even these five are now quarrelling as a sign of the coalition fracturing (Att. 2.7.4).
The deliberative body of the Roman state, composed of ex-magistrates; addressed by Cicero as patres conscripti. Issued senatus consulta which had moral but not legal force.
The Stoic philosophical school, founded by Zeno of Citium; Cicero invokes their doctrine that urbanity (wit) is a virtue.

The pro-Spartan oligarchy of thirty men imposed on Athens after its defeat in the Peloponnesian War, who ruled by terror in 404–403 BC until overthrown by Thrasybulus. Cicero cites Socrates' refusal to leave the city even under their rule as an exemplum for staying put under a tyrant.
The Roman victory procession through the city, the highest military honour a magistrate could receive, voted by the Senate; a supplicatio was often its forerunner.

The college of priestesses of Vesta who tended the sacred hearth of Rome; together with the women of the household they conducted the Bona Dea rite, and after Clodius's intrusion in 62 BC repeated the polluted sacrifice and (with the pontiffs) helped pronounce it sacrilege.

The board of twenty established by Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC to oversee the distribution of the Campanian and other public land. Cicero treats membership of it, and the board itself, as a badge of the new regime.
Civil action for damage done by armed force, instituted by M. Lucullus as praetor in 76 BC; the form of action in Pro Tullio.
The annual edict in which each praetor laid out the formulae and remedies he would grant in his year of office; the framework of Roman civil procedure.
Roman civil law as held and interpreted by the jurisconsults — the body of legal practice within which inheritance, contracts, kinship, and property were governed.
The legislation pushed through by Mark Antony as consul in 44 BC. In Fam. 12.14 Lentulus Spinther the younger boasts of having been the first to 'break' them in Asia.

aliases: the Cornelian on wills, on coin
entities.summary: The bundle of statutes carried by Sulla as dictator (82–81 BC) reorganising criminal procedure and much of public life — including the leges Corneliae testamentaria and nummaria (de falsis), against forgery of wills and coin. Cicero arraigns Verres for violations of this code in In Verrem 2.1.

The corpus of laws carried by the tribunates of Tiberius Gracchus (133 BC) and especially Gaius Gracchus (123–122 BC) — among them the prohibition on putting a Roman citizen to death without the people's authorisation. Verres's execution of citizens at Messana is arraigned as a breach of this Sempronian guarantee (In Verrem 2.5).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Two laws of c. 150 BC governing obnuntiatio — the religious power to halt legislation by declaring the auspices unfavourable. The bulwark of the senatorial order against popular tribunes; gutted by Clodius's law of 58 BC, the grievance that runs through In Pisonem.

A law of 98 BC (consuls Q. Caecilius Metellus Nepos and T. Didius) imposing a three-week interval (trinundinum) between a bill's promulgation and its vote and banning omnibus bills. Cicero counts it among the laws the coalition trampled in 59 (Att 2.9.1).

Caesar's second agrarian law of 59 BC, distributing the public land of Campania (ager Campanus) to settlers; candidates were made to swear a self-cursing oath upholding it. FLAG: may be treated as a clause/second statute of lex-iulia-agraria-59bc rather than a wholly separate law.

The lex Cincia de donis et muneribus (204 BC), which barred advocates from taking fees and capped the size of gifts; here invoked over whether Cicero may accept the gift of Servius Claudius's library.

The plebiscite carried by P. Clodius (tr. pl. 58 BC) interdicting Cicero from fire and water, which drove him into exile and which Clodius armed with self-protective clauses meant to make its abrogation legally hazardous. The recall debate of late 58 turned on how safely it could be repealed.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Sulla's law (81 BC) regulating provincial commands, under which an outgoing governor had thirty days to leave his province after his successor arrived. Cited by Cicero on the term for Appius's departure from Cilicia.

A Sullan statute fixing the maximum sums a province could decree for the travelling expenses of embassies (legati) sent to Rome. Cicero says he recommended that allowances for Appius's envoys be assessed as close to this law as possible (Fam. 3.10.6).
(stub --- to be enriched)

The law passed by the comitia curiata formally conferring imperium on a magistrate, traditionally required before a governor took up his province. Appius Claudius disputed whether it was strictly necessary (Fam. 1.9.25).
The standing law against ambitus (electoral bribery), in this period Pompey's lex Pompeia de ambitu of 52 BC; Appius Claudius Pulcher was prosecuted and acquitted under it in 50 BC (Fam. 3.11-3.12).

The standing law against maiestas (treason, or diminishing the majesty of the Roman people), the Sullan lex Cornelia de maiestate as later amended; Appius Claudius Pulcher was prosecuted and acquitted under it in 50 BC. Cicero notes Sulla framed it so no one could be attacked with impunity (Fam. 3.11.2).

Law of 103 BC proposed by Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus (tribune) transferring election of the major priesthoods from co-optation by the colleges to a vote of 17 tribes chosen by lot; repealed by Sulla, restored by Caesar in the Lex Iulia.
Rome's first written code of law (450 BC); memorised by Roman schoolboys into the late Republic. Crassus's hyperbole at De Or. 1.195: it surpasses the libraries of all the philosophers.
A grain law regulating the subsidised corn supply at Rome, here named among the threats aimed at the equestrians in 59 BC. FLAG: generic title; the specific statute meant in 2.19.2 is uncertain.

A lex Gabinia (probably of the tribune A. Gabinius, 67 BC) that barred provincials and their cities from contracting loans at Rome and barred Roman courts from enforcing such bonds; the Salaminians needed special senatorial decrees to borrow from Brutus's agents around it.

The Sicilian grain-tithe law inherited by Rome from Hiero II of Syracuse (3rd century BC); the meticulously balanced framework for the tithe-farming of Sicilian corn between cultivators and publicans. Verres's evasions and refashioning of this code structure the argument of In Verrem 2.3.

Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC (his first consulship), which distributed public land --- primarily in Campania --- to Pompey's veterans and the urban poor. Among its provisions it explicitly ratified the civic status of Volaterrae and exempted its territory from redistribution, confirming Cicero's earlier advocacy on the town's behalf. This law is cited in Fam. 13.4 as the legal basis for Volaterrae's protection.
Caesar's law of 59 BC reorganising the regulation of provincial governors; widely held the most carefully framed of Roman extortion laws and the basis for later trials.

Julius Caesar's law on the priestly colleges, the most recent legislation on the subject as of 43 BC; it restored popular election of priests and contained a clause permitting the case of an absent candidate to be considered in the priestly comitia.

Caesar's sumptuary law of 46 BC restricting expenditure on banquets and luxury foods, enforced as part of his censorial praefectura morum; allowed larger outlays on festival days. The butt of Cicero's dining jokes in Fam. 9.15 and 9.16.

A law of 62 BC (consuls D. Iunius Silanus and L. Licinius Murena) requiring bills to be deposited with the treasury before being voted, to prevent surprise legislation. Cicero lists it among the constitutional safeguards Caesar's coalition overrode in 59 (Att 2.9.1).

Crassus's law of 55 BC against electoral clubs (sodalicia) used to organise bribery, prosecuting the systematic corruption of voting tribes. A defendant acquitted under the general lex de ambitu could still be tried afresh under this stricter statute.
Pompey's law of 55 BC reforming the album of jurors by tribe and rank; in Cicero's hope, less liable to political packing than its predecessors.

An agrarian bill of the tribune Plotius (probably c. 70 BC) to settle troops on land, cited at Att. 1.18.6 as the close model for L. Flavius's bill of 60 BC. Distinct from the lex Plotia de vi (law:lex-plotia-de-vi).
The standing statute (also Lex Plautia, of the 70s BC) establishing the criminal court for public violence (vis); under it Sempronius Rufus prosecuted Tuccius in 51 BC.

Pompey's law of 52 BC establishing a stringent court for electoral corruption (paired with his lex de vi); many were convicted under it that year, and in 49 BC Caesar planned to recall those it had condemned.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A law (of uncertain date, perhaps 154 BC) regulating the days on which the senate could meet, forbidding sittings on comitial days; cited by Cicero in Fam. 1.4 as barring senate business before the Kalends of February and during much of that month while foreign embassies were pending.

The theatre-law of the tribune L. Roscius Otho (67 BC), reserving the first fourteen rows of seats for the equestrian order; it provoked popular protest. Cicero's lost defence of it is the 'speech on Otho' in his consular corpus.

The provincial constitution drawn up for Sicily in 131 BC by the consul P. Rupilius and his ten-man senatorial commission after the First Slave War — the code regulating jurisdiction, court-composition, and inter-city procedure on the island. Verres's repeated violations of its rules supply the procedural counts of In Verrem 2.2.
A Roman statute penalizing homosexual offences (stuprum) against freeborn youths; in Fam. 8.12 and 8.14 Caelius is indicted under it and counter-indicts Appius the censor under the same law.
(stub --- to be enriched)

The law of C. Sempronius Gracchus (123 BC) requiring the Senate to designate the consular provinces before the consular elections, so that a sitting magistrate could not be arbitrarily superseded. In 56 BC the triumvirs secured that Caesar should not be succeeded under it, prolonging his Gallic command (Fam 1.7.10).

The grain law of the consuls M. Terentius Varro Lucullus and C. Cassius Longinus, 73 BC, regulating the purchase of additional Sicilian grain (the frumentum emptum and the second tithe) above what the lex Hieronica required. Verres's abuse of the requisitions made under it is the burden of In Verrem 2.3.

The law of the tribune C. Trebonius (55 BC) assigning the Spanish provinces to Pompey and Syria to Crassus, each for five years, in the wake of the conference at Luca. Discussed obliquely at Att. 4.8A.2.
(stub --- to be enriched)

aliases: senatus consultum de re publica defendenda
entities.summary: Senatorial decree commanding the consuls to see that the commonwealth take no harm; first issued in 121 BC against C. Gracchus, then in 100 BC against Saturninus, then in 63 BC against Catiline. Authorised use of force; its constitutional standing was challenged repeatedly by populares.
Junior magistracy concerned with the city, the markets, and the public games; a stepping-stone to the praetorship.

A member of the priestly college of augurs, who interpreted the will of the gods by observing signs (the auspices) before public acts. Cicero and Cornificius were both augurs, hence Cicero addresses him as conlega (fellow-augur).
Two senior magistrates elected normally every five years to revise the lists of citizens, knights, and senators, and to let the public contracts; the highest office an ex-consul could hold.
Head of the Roman state; two were elected annually, gave their names to the year, commanded armies, and presided over the Senate.
The title 'imperator', a victory-acclamation conferred on a commander by his troops after a notable success; carried in the openers of letters. Murcus and Crispus are styled imp. in Fam. 12.11.

A senator appointed for five-day terms to hold elections when both consulships fell vacant, a recurring expedient during the electoral paralysis of 54-53 BC. Cicero jokes that with so many interregna no one needs a jurist's advice (Fam. 7.11.1).

A legatus was a deputy or staff officer attached to a magistrate or province; a legatio libera (free legation) was a nominal commission granted to a senator so that he could travel on private business with the dignity and privileges of a public envoy.
An attendant of a Roman magistrate with imperium, carrying the fasces and clearing his path; Cicero charges that the governor's lictor act as the agent of the governor's own mildness.
Annually-elected magistrate ranking below consul; presided over the standing courts and (as praetor urbanus) issued the annual edict that governed civil procedure.
A promagistracy: a former consul (or appointee) granted consular imperium to govern a province or command an army. C. Cassius Longinus styles himself PROCOS. in the openers of Fam. 12.11 and 12.12.
Legally-authorised agent or attorney for an absent principal; central to Pro Quinctio, where Sex. Alfenus stands in for the absent Quinctius.
A promagistracy: an official granted praetorian imperium to govern a province or command troops. In Fam. 12.15 Lentulus Spinther the younger styles himself PROQ. PROPR. (proquaestor pro praetore).

A promagistracy: an official exercising a quaestor's powers (typically finance and supply) beyond the regular term or by special grant. Lentulus Spinther the younger and Patiscus hold it in the 43 BC eastern campaign.
Most junior of the regular magistracies; financial officers attached to the consuls and provincial governors.

The board of four chief magistrates (quattuorviri) at the head of many Italian municipia and colonies, exercising local jurisdiction and administration. In Fam. 13.76 Cicero addresses the quattuorviri together with the decurions of the town.
Magistrate of the plebs (ten elected annually); could veto magistrates and senatorial decrees and convoke the plebeian assembly.
The 'paymaster tribunes,' a propertied class who under the lex Aurelia of 70 BC made up one of the three decuriae (panels) of jurors in the criminal courts, alongside senators and knights.

A military tribune, a senior officer of a Roman legion (six per legion); Cicero notes that the mutinous cohorts at Philomelium were left without legate, military tribune, or even a centurion (Fam. 15.4).
A. Allienus, a legate who in 43 BC led four legions out of Egypt; he handed them over to C. Cassius Longinus in Syria rather than to Dolabella.

Etruscan man of letters from Volaterrae, son of the defendant of the Pro Caecina; a Pompeian pardoned by Caesar and a friend of Cicero (addressee of Fam. 6.5-6.9). In 43 BC he is among the friends of Furnius gathered at Cicero's house.

Brother of Publius Varius; sued by Atticus's uncle Q. Caecilius for receiving property from Varius by fraud (Att 1.1.3-4). He cultivated L. Domitius and Cicero and had helped Cicero and Quintus in their canvasses, putting Cicero in an awkward position when Caecilius asked him to appear against Satyrus. Distinct from the Caninius Gallus and Caninius Rebilus entities.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A. Fufius was a learned Roman (eruditum hominem) and one of Cicero's intimate friends (ex meis intimis). Cicero recommends him to C. Memmius in Fam. 13.3, noting that Memmius had already promised in person to treat him well. He is otherwise unknown beyond this reference; distinct from Q. Fufius Calenus.
Roman tribune 67 (carried the lex Gabinia for Pompey against the pirates), consul 58 BC with L. Piso; governor of Syria 57-55 BC. Convicted on his return.

A Maltese (Melitensis) who was Cicero's oldest guest-friend (hospes antiquissimus) and a close intimate; he had taken Cicero's side in the civil war even longer than Cicero himself, and obtained pardon from Caesar through Cicero's intercession. Subject of the recommendation Fam. 13.52 (46 BC).
A friend of Caesar's and of Cicero, described as a good man; his death is reported by Cicero to Tiro as ordinary news. Distinct from the Verrine witness L. Liguris.

Pompeian partisan stranded abroad (probably in Athens) after Pharsalus (48 BC); addressee of Cicero's four consolatory letters Fam. 6.1–6.4 (46–45 BC). Distinct from L. Manlius Torquatus, cos. 65 BC (person:l-manlius-torquatus).

A connection (necessarius) of Titus Antistius, residing in Bithynia after Pharsalus; Antistius took refuge with him after the battle. Caesar encountered Antistius there and ordered him to come to Rome. Plautius himself plays no further role in the letter (13.29). Identity uncertain: possibly related to the praetor A. Plautius of 51 BC but not confirmed.

Consul 151 BC (with L. Licinius Lucullus) and one of the ten commissioners who settled Greece after the sack of Corinth in 146 BC; a philhellene annalist. Cicero researches his place on the commission and recalls his statue at the Isthmus (Att. 13.30, 13.32).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A friend of Cicero's to whose care Cicero commended the sick Tiro in January 49 BC, charging him with Tiro's health and safe passage. Distinct from the polymath M. Terentius Varro.

Roman of the late Republic and early Principate who bore the cognomen Murena through adoption into or alliance with the Licinia Murena family; active correspondent and friend of Cicero in 46-44 BC, independently commending T. Manlius to Servius Sulpicius (Fam. 13.22). Likely identical with A. Terentius Varro Murena, consul suffect 23 BC, later executed for alleged conspiracy against Augustus. UNCERTAIN: the identification with the consul of 23 BC rests on the rare double name; flag for review.

A Roman businessman of longstanding intimacy with Cicero who held large and flourishing financial interests in the province of Cilicia; the subject of the letter of recommendation Ad Familiares 1.3 to the proconsul Lentulus Spinther.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A trusted courier of Cicero who met him with letters on his arrival at Athens in October 50 BC. Likely a household slave or freedman.

The leading Roman tragic poet (c. 170-c. 86 BC), author of many tragedies on Greek and Roman themes (including an Oenomaus and a Clytaemnestra). Frequently quoted by Cicero; in Fam. 9.16 Paetus quotes his Oenomaus and Cicero borrows his 'wave against a rock' simile.
A friend of Marcus Brutus mentioned in Ad Brutum 1.6; his sister was married to Glyco the physician, and he was distressed by Glyco's arrest on suspicion of poisoning Pansa.

A young slave or attendant who met Servius Sulpicius Rufus on the road with a wax tablet announcing Marcellus's death (Ad Fam. 4.12). The manuscript name 'Acidim' is textually corrupt and the figure is otherwise unknown; the form is sometimes emended to a proper name. Flagged: identity and reading uncertain.

A man at Rome, given to long-winded talk, with whom Atticus had a legal or financial matter (the 'Acutilian business'); Cicero handled it on Atticus's behalf and passed the advisory role to Peducaeus.

Tetrarch of the Galatian tribe of the Trocmi and son-in-law of King Deiotarus; Diogenes and Philo were travelling to him when they parted from Cicero at Pessinus (Fam. 2.12, 50 BC). He later seized and massacred the Roman inhabitants of Heracleia Pontica (c. 35 BC) and was executed by Augustus after the battle of Actium.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A slave/courier of Cicero, sent to attend the sick Tiro because Cicero judged him kind and fond of Tiro.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Athenian orator (c. 389-314 BC), opponent of Demosthenes in the Crown trial; after his exile to Rhodes, allegedly delivered Demosthenes's reply with the famous concession that 'you would have wondered more if you had heard the man himself'.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A Samnite gladiator from Aesernia, famous from a passage of Lucilius pitting him against Pacideianus; Cicero invokes the pairing as a proverbial image of an unequal, mutually destructive fight.
The leading tragic actor of Cicero's day, friend of Cicero. By the games of 55 BC he was elderly; in Fam. 7.1 his voice fails him at the line ``si sciens fallo''.

Mythological king of Mycenae and commander-in-chief of the Greek host at Troy, the 'king of kings' of the Iliad; a stock exemplum of supreme command who relied on the counsel of the aged Nestor. Cicero invokes him in Fam. 9.14 to flatter Dolabella while casting himself as Nestor.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
King of Sparta (c. 444-360 BC), subject of Xenophon's encomium; for Cicero, with Xenophon's Cyrus, a model ruler under whose command no harsh word was ever heard (Q.fr 1.2).
A co-heir, with Brinnius's freedman, in the estate of Brinnius; content to let Cicero's wishes settle the division (Att. 13.13).

Athenian statesman and general (c. 450-404 BC), pupil of Socrates and leader of the Sicilian expedition of 415 BC; Cicero cites the discredited tale that he threw the comic poet Eupolis into the sea during that voyage.
An intermediary at Rome through whom Cicero passed inquiries in 45 BC; he reported on Publilius's movements and relayed questions on Atticus's behalf.
A Greek geographer and didactic poet of the first century BC, nicknamed Lychnus. Cicero, returning his books to Atticus, judged him a careless man and no good poet, though not without use.

Alexander III of Macedon (356–323 BC), king from 336 and conqueror of the Persian empire. Cicero invokes him in the Verrines (2.4) for his refusal to remove the works of art at Ephesus — a contrast with Verres's plunder of the same Asian shrines.
A host or agent of Atticus on Corcyra who entertained Cicero lavishly at Actium on his homeward voyage in 50 BC. Distinct from Alexio the physician of Cicero's later letters.
Atticus's literate slave, called by Cicero 'the image of Tiro', who regularly adds his greetings at the foot of Atticus's letters.
A legate of Quintus Cicero in Asia (c. 61-59 BC), called by Cicero 'ours' in spirit, goodwill, and even his manner of life. Distinct from the later A. Allienus, legate of 43 BC.

An early Latin writer who popularized Epicurean doctrine in plain Latin prose, winning a wide unlearned readership; Cicero (and here Cassius) treats him and Catius as crude interpreters who debased Epicurus's teaching (Fam. 15.19).

The agent and envoy (legatus) of Ptolemy XII Auletes who lobbied and bribed the senate at Rome in 56 BC to secure the king's armed restoration to Egypt. Cicero reports him 'openly using money against us' in the contest over the Alexandrian command.

Mythical seer of Argos, one of the Seven Against Thebes, who foreknew his own death in the campaign but was bound by oath to join it; a recurring figure in tragedy (Aeschylus, Euripides) and a type of the man who acts rightly despite knowing he is doomed. Cited by Cicero in Fam. 6.6.6.

Daughter of T. Ampius Balbus; named alongside her mother Eppuleia in Fam. 6.12.3 (46 BC) as one of the family members whose distress indicated how heavily Ampius was bearing his exile. Otherwise unknown.
Pre-Socratic philosopher (c. 500-428 BC); the first to bring philosophy to Athens; teacher of Pericles.
Slave of L. Egnatius Rufus, sent to Asia on his master's business and commended by Cicero to the proquaestor Appuleius (Fam. 13.45).
Fourth king of Rome, traditionally 642-617 BC; grandson of Numa, founder of Ostia.
A slave/courier of Cicero who carried news of the sick Tiro between Cumae and Patrae in 53 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Leading citizen of Laodicea (inland Phrygian Laodicea, in one of the three Asiatic dioceses attached to Cicero's province of Cilicia); host and friend of Cicero during his proconsulship of Cilicia (51-50 BC), commended to P. Servilius Isauricus in Fam. 13.67. Son of Artemo of Laodicea.
A man who reported (through Hermogenes Clodius) having seen Cicero's son at Corcyra, a rumour Cicero anxiously chases down (Att. 13.24-13.25).
A friend or dependent of Atticus whom Cicero, at Atticus's request, supported in some matter at Rome in 59 BC (Att. 2.20.1). Otherwise unknown.
Senator and friend of Cicero, owner of an estate near Cicero's Pompeianum that could host visitors when the villa was unfinished.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A copyist in Atticus's household; with Pharnaces and Salvius, charged with deleting the erroneous mention of Corfidius from copies of the Pro Ligario (Att. 13.44).
A slave or freedman of the Cicero household whom the younger Marcus commends to Tiro's care in 44 BC, probably the bearer of his letter.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Freedman of Gnaeus Otacilius Naso, one of three agents managing Naso's business in the province of Sicily c. 46 BC (Fam. 13.33).

Greek philosopher (c. 130-68 BC), head of the Academy who broke with the scepticism of Philo of Larissa and revived a dogmatic, Stoicizing Platonism (the 'Old Academy'). Varro was his pupil and adherent; in Cicero's Academica Varro expounds Antiochus's position against the scepticism Cicero (after Philo) defends.

Antiochus III 'the Great' of the Seleucid empire (r. 222–187 BC), defeated by L. Cornelius Scipio (Asiaticus) at Magnesia in 190 BC and stripped of Asia west of the Taurus by the Treaty of Apamea (188 BC). Cicero refers to him at In Verrem 2.1 in the context of the Roman settlement of Asia.

King of Commagene (a small kingdom on the upper Euphrates), a Roman client. His envoys were the first to bring Cicero word in 51 BC of the Parthian crossing of the Euphrates (Fam. 15.1, 15.3). Distinct from the Seleucid Antiochi.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Dynast of Derbe in Lycaonia (south-central Asia Minor), bound to Cicero by guest-friendship and the closest familiarity. Q. Marcius Philippus had become violently angry with him and held his sons in his power; Cicero pleads for the sons in Fam. 13.73 (mid-40s BC).
A slave-actor manumitted just before his stage debut at the games of 54 BC, where he won the prize despite his small stature and weak voice (Att. 4.15.6).
Quintus Antistius Labeo, one of the conspirators against Julius Caesar and a jurist; he remained a committed republican and reportedly chose to die at Philippi (42 BC) rather than survive defeat.

One of four men (with Censorinus, Cassius, and Scaevola) by whom Quintus Cicero reported he was deeply loved in Asia (Q.fr 1.2). Identity uncertain; not securely identifiable with the M. Antonii of the registry.
Consul 44 BC, triumvir 43 BC; target of the fourteen Philippics; Cicero's death warrant came from him after the proscriptions of November 43 BC.
A person to whom Cicero forbade a copy or loan to be given, in the same breath as restricting it to L. Fadius the aedile; otherwise obscure.
A freedman of M. Fadius Gallus, named in Fam. 7.25 (45 BC) as someone from whom even Gallus should keep a literary confidence. Known only from this letter.

The most celebrated painter of antiquity (4th century BC), court painter to Alexander the Great. Cicero invokes his famous Venus Anadyomene, whose head and breast he finished exquisitely while leaving the rest sketched, as an image of his own half-completed restoration.

A Greek who had gone bankrupt (conturbat) and, Cicero complains in Att. 4.7, presumed to claim the same protections as a Roman knight against his creditor Terentius. Otherwise unidentified; distinct from the freedman-historian Apollonius and from Apollonius Molon.

aliases: Apollonius, son of Diocles, of Panhormus
entities.summary: (stub --- to be enriched)

Greek freedman of P. Licinius Crassus the younger; brought up in Cicero's house alongside Diodotus the Stoic. Served Cicero in Cilicia (51–50 BC) and Caesar in the Alexandrian war (48–47), then set out for Spain in 45 BC to write the history of Caesar's campaigns in Greek (Fam. 13.16).
Apollonius Molon of Rhodes, the leading Greek rhetorician of the early first century BC, under whom Cicero studied at Rhodes in 78-77 BC. Here the butt of a pun (myloi, mill-stones, vs Molon).
Elder brother of Clodius; consul 54 BC, censor 50 BC, governor of Cilicia 53--51 BC (Cicero's predecessor there); recipient of Fam. book 3.
A younger member of the Claudii Pulchri, compromised by association with Antony in 43 BC. Recipient of Fam. 10.29, a short letter in which Cicero promises to shield him and secure his safety.
Verres's bagman in the Sicilian grain extortions; the principal villain of In Verrem II.3.
A woman, "the dowered Aquilia," attached by a prospective marriage to the household of Quintus Cicero the younger, who balked at accepting her as a stepmother (Att. 14.13, 14.17).
Praetor 66 BC and noted jurist; contemporary of Cicero, mentioned in Att. 4.12 in the Halimetus matter.

Roman consul 129 BC (colleague of C. Sempronius Tuditanus), who completed the suppression of Aristonicus's revolt and organised the province of Asia; his consulship dates the dramatic setting of Cicero's De Re Publica. Distinct from his son M'. Aquillius, cos. 101.
A celebrated mime actress who gave great satisfaction at the games of 54 BC (Att. 4.15.6); later mentioned by Horace as a star of the stage.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Stoic philosopher (2nd c. BC); Cicero alludes to a logical puzzle of his, 'the problem of Archedemus,' as a spur to philosophical work (Att. 13.28).
Greek poet of Antioch; client of the Lucullus family; defendant in Pro Archia (62 BC) in a citizenship-status case. Cicero's defence is the famous panegyric of literary culture.
Greek iambic and elegiac poet of Paros (7th c. BC), the archetype of savage personal invective. Cicero calls Bibulus's venomous edicts against Caesar and Pompey "Archilochean" (Att. 2.20.6, 2.21.4).
Greek mathematician and engineer (c. 287-212 BC), killed at the Roman capture of Syracuse. Inventor of the celestial globe described by Sulpicius Gallus in De Re Publica book 1.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Pythagorean philosopher, mathematician, and statesman (c. 428-347 BC); friend of Plato.

Brother of King Ariobarzanes III of Cappadocia; in 51 BC he disclosed to Cicero that he had been approached to seize the throne but refused while his brother lived (Fam. 15.2). Later reigned as Ariarathes X.

King of Cappadocia (52–42 BC), styled Eusebes and Philorhomaeus. The Senate had specially commended his safety to Cicero, who while marching to Cilicia in 51 BC uncovered and warned him of a palace plot (Fam. 15.2). Son of Ariobarzanes II, whose murder Cicero recalls.

The great Alexandrian grammarian and Homeric critic (2nd c. BC), proverbial for athetizing (rejecting as spurious) any Homeric line he judged unworthy. Cicero invokes him jokingly in Fam. 3.11.5 to disown any unpolished letter attributed to him.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek philosopher (c. 435-356 BC), pupil of Socrates and founder of the Cyrenaic (hedonist) school. In Fam. 9.26 Cicero cites his unembarrassed retort about the courtesan Lais ('I have her, I am not had by her').

A Greek contact shared between Cicero and Q. Minucius Thermus (propraetor of Asia); he visited Cicero and carried Cicero's advice back to Thermus regarding the treatment of Thermus's quaestor. Identity not further recoverable from the surviving correspondence.
A letter-carrier in Cicero's service who shuttled between Terentia at Rome and the exiled Cicero in 58 BC, almost certainly a household slave or freedman.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Greek rhetorician and grammarian of Nysa engaged as a teacher for the boys of Cicero's household; at Att. 2.7.5 the young Marcus asks that the answer given about his cousin hold for him too. A noted scholar, kinsman of the geographer Strabo's teachers.
The Athenian comic poet (c. 446-c. 386 BC); Cicero likens one of Quintus's letters to him for its blend of charm and weight.
The Greek philosopher (384--322 BC); a presence in Cicero's library and study, image (imago) of whom hung above Atticus's reading-chair.

Aristoxenus of Tarentum (fl. later 4th c. BC), pupil of Aristotle and the most important ancient theorist of music, who held that the soul was a kind of harmony or attunement of the body. Cicero pairs him with Dicaearchus as a byword for genuine learning at Att. 8.4.
Academic philosopher, brother and successor of Antiochus of Ascalon as head of the Old Academy; Cicero's host at Athens in 51 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Arsaces, the dynastic name of the kings of Parthia, used by Caelius as shorthand for the Parthian monarch and his capital (Fam. 8.14): capturing "Arsaces" stands for a complete conquest of Parthia.

King of Armenia (c. 55–34 BC), of doubtful loyalty during the Parthian crisis; Cicero camped at Cybistra in 51 BC partly to remind him that a Roman army stood near his borders (Fam. 15.2). His sister was married to the Parthian prince (Fam. 15.3).
King of Persia (r. 465-424 BC) to whom the exiled Themistocles fled and offered his services; here the destination figured in a 'Themistoclean exile.'

Father of Andro of Laodicea; named only in the patronymic by which Cicero identifies his friend Andro in Fam. 13.67. Distinct from the Sicilian Artemones of the Verrines.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man whose dealings concerned Quintus's affairs in 54 BC; Cicero declines to interpose, leaving the matter to his brother's discretion. Otherwise unidentified.

Greek physician of Patrae (Achaea) who attended members of Cicero's household and won his personal confidence for both skill and loyalty. Cicero recommends him to Servius Sulpicius Rufus in Fam. 13.20. The name is a Latinised form of Greek Asklapon, derived from the healing god Asclepius.
Greek physician (c. 124-40 BC), settled at Rome; the dominant medical authority of the late Republic. Friend of Crassus the orator.
A senior centurion (primus pilus) in the army of M. Calpurnius Bibulus, killed with the first cohort in an ambush on Mount Amanus in 51 BC.
Gaius Asinius Pollio, Caesarian governor of Further Spain in 43 BC and a correspondent of Cicero. He later became a soldier, orator, historian of the civil wars, and literary patron.
A notably tall tragic actor of the mid-first century BC; Cicero jokes that the diminutive Antiphon, playing in the Andromacha, was 'taller than Astyanax' (Att. 4.15.6).
A man whom Cato had earnestly commended to Cicero; punished with exile through the malice of Athenais, he was restored by Cicero to high standing at the Cappadocian court in 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).
A figure at the Cappadocian court (likely the queen-mother) by whose malice Metras and Athenaeus had been driven into exile before Cicero intervened in 51 BC (Fam. 15.4). Identity uncertain.

A henchman of P. Clodius cast by Cicero as the "standard-bearer" (signifer) of Clodius's street-gangs (Att 2.12.2); the name pointedly recalls Athenion the slave-king of the Sicilian revolt, branding the Clodians a servile insurgency. Distinct from person:athenion-fugitivus, the historical Sicilian rebel.

aliases: Athenion the king of the runaway slaves

Stoic philosopher of Tarsus, son of Sandon (Athenodorus Cananites), cited by Cicero as an authority on what true nobility (eugeneia) is; later tutor of the young Octavian.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman knight whose case in Bithynia was decided by P. Silius in his favour at Cicero's belated request, mediated through their common friend L. Aelius Lamia. Otherwise unattested.

Probably Sex. Atilius Serranus Gavianus, tribune of the plebs 57 BC; in 54 BC his young son died and his father delivered a eulogy that Cicero had ghost-written. Distinct from the eques Atilius (person:atilius-eques).
Young prosecutor in Pro Caelio (56 BC); son of L. Calpurnius Bestia, whom Caelius had himself prosecuted earlier in 56 BC.
A man of Hypaepa in Asia who asked Cicero to ensure Quintus did not block the disbursement decreed for a statue of Q. Publicenius (Q.fr 1.2). Distinct from Attalus of Netum in the Verrines.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Caecilia Attica (born c. 51 BC), the daughter of Titus Pomponius Atticus; she later married Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa (Octavian's general). In 43 BC Brutus's inquiry about her "terms" likely refers to betrothal negotiations.
Cicero's lifelong intimate (110–32 BC), wealthy Epicurean banker, recipient of the Ad Atticum letters; later Q. Cicero's brother-in-law.
A man whose affair Cornificius had raised with Cicero; Cicero thinks there is nothing in it, since Tratorius has said nothing to him (Fam. 12.30.5). Otherwise unknown.

A Paelignian officer commanding the Pompeian garrison of five cohorts at Sulmo in February 49 BC; he opened the town's gates to Antony, an early sign of the Pompeian collapse in Italy (Att. 8.4; cf. Caes. BC 1.18).
A perfumer (pigmentarius) at Rome named in Fam. 15.17 as delighted by the death of P. Sulla, his supposed rival at auctions. An obscure tradesman, otherwise unknown.
The legendary augur of the regal period who, to prove his art to Tarquinius Priscus, cut a whetstone in two with a razor; a stock exemplum of Roman augural authority.

One of two negligible figures (with Palicanus) whose candidacy for the consulship of 63 Cicero dismisses without comment at Att 1.1.1. Probably M. Aufidius Lurco or T. Aufidius; identity uncertain, flagged. Distinct from Sextus Aufidius (person:sex-aufidius).
An unidentified man (named only by his father's praenomen, Aulus) to whom the acquittal of Valerius was reckoned a favour, in Cicero's hint of a fixed trial. Identity disputed. FLAG.
A creditor to be paid in full out of Cicero's accounts in November 44 BC. Otherwise unidentified.
A party to a debt Cicero hopes to settle through Piso; Cicero expects him to do his duty (Att. 13.4). Otherwise unknown.
A man whose death in December 45 BC left legacies in which Caesar took a twelfth and Cicero's friend Lepta a third (Att. 13.48).
A neighbour of Cicero (named with the neighbour Egnatius) in connection with business Atticus is asked to transact (Att. 13.45).
A man named with Metellus in Cicero's request to Atticus for news, in a passage of damaged text (Att. 13.21).
A Caesarian officer who posted a guard and pitched camp to protect Cicero's villa when Caesar descended on it with a retinue of two thousand at the Saturnalia, 19 December 45 BC (Att. 13.52).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A letter-carrier who delivered correspondence between Cicero, Atticus, and Cassius in the spring of 44 BC (Att. 14.19).

A friend of Cicero and Trebatius at or near Velia who had kept Nico's book On Overeating hidden from Cicero (Fam. 7.20). Identity uncertain; distinct from the generals P. Ventidius Bassus and Q. Caecilius Bassus.

A man whose death Cicero mentions at Fam. 7.9.2 ('especially now that Battara is dead') as removing any reason for Trebatius to hurry back to Rome. Otherwise unknown; the point of the allusion is lost.

A homebred slave (verna) of one Demetrius, posted with the garrison at Intimilium in 49 BC, who took a bribe from the opposing faction and strangled the local notable Domitius, triggering the town's revolt. The lowborn 'Psecade natus' of Caelius's closing joke.
Tribune; prosecuted by Caelius earlier in 56 BC; father of the prosecutor Atratinus in Pro Caelio.

The two sons of Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus (cos. 59 BC, Caesar's consular colleague) and Porcia (daughter of Cato Uticensis); both were killed by order of Ptolemy before the battle of Pharsalus in 48 BC or, in some accounts, died in service of the republican cause. In 43 BC the plural 'Bibulorum' refers to two young Bibulus kinsmen whose priestly elections Cicero sought to advance.
(stub --- to be enriched)
King of western Mauretania and a Caesarian ally; Balbus crossed over into his kingdom after absconding from Gades in 43 BC.

A deceased man of whose estate Cicero was a co-heir (with Brinnius's freedman and Albius Sabinus); the Brinnian auction and the conveyance of the Brinnian estate at Puteoli, handled through Vestorius, recur in the late 45 BC letters (Att. 13.12, 13.13, 13.50).
Tetrarch of the Trocmi (Galatia) and son-in-law of Deiotarus; bought the priesthood of Cybele at Pessinus in 58 BC under a Clodian law.
The tyrannicide (85–42 BC); praetor 44 BC; one of the principal assassins of Caesar; recipient of the Ad Brutum letters; defeated and dead at Philippi.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Praetor at Rome at the time Naevius applied to attach Quinctius's goods; his order to advertise the sale is the procedural anchor of the speech.
Cicero's next-door neighbour, who had bought a thousand iugera from M. Pilius; Cicero cites the price as a benchmark in weighing the cost of suburban gardens (Att. 13.31).

Senator, father-in-law of L. Sestius (son of P. Sestius). Received estates at valuation from M. Laberius; these had been bought by Laberius from Caesar out of confiscated Plotian property. His case is the subject of Fam. 13.8. Otherwise unattested.
Praetor in 54 BC who presided over the standing court on maiestas; it was by his edict that Gabinius was required to appear.
(stub --- to be enriched)
One of the heirs of Q. Turius, a businessman in the province of Africa; commended by Cicero to Cornificius (Fam. 12.26). Distinct from M. Anneius, Cicero's Cilician legate.

Gaius Antonius, younger brother of Marcus Antonius (Mark Antony), praetor in 44 BC and governor in Macedonia; Brutus detained him in 43 BC, and he was held responsible for instigating a mutiny in the Fourteenth Legion through treachery.
Roman consul 63 BC as Cicero's colleague; uncle of Mark Antony. Cicero exchanged provinces with him so as to keep him quiet during the Catilinarian crisis.
The praetor and jurist (d. c. 44 BC) presiding as iudex in Pro Quinctio; a celebrated authority on civil law, author of the actio de dolo malo.
A tiresome neighbour of Cicero at Formiae in 59 BC who haunted him with daily visits proposing to philosophize. Distinct from Q. Arrius the praetor (person:q-arrius-praetor).

Gaius Ateius Capito, a close friend of Cicero who is described as having supported him through every vicissitude of fortune, including financially. A consistent admirer of Caesar. He is the beneficiary (heir to half and a third) of the estate of his deceased relative Titus Antistius (quaestor in Macedonia). Cicero writes to L. Munatius Plancus asking him to secure Caesar's recognition of Capito's inheritance (13.29). Not the Augustan-era jurist Gaius Ateius Capito (cos. suff. AD 5), who is a later generation.
Brother of L. Aurelius; commended together with him to Q. Ancharius in Fam. 13.40.

C. Avianius Evander was a Greek sculptor and art-restorer active at Rome and Athens in the mid-first century BC. He is attested as restoring works by Praxiteles and other masters. He was lodging in C. Memmius's Athens property and faced eviction; Cicero interceded on his behalf in Fam. 13.2, at the request of his patron M. Aemilius.

Wealthy businessman and one of Cicero's most intimate friends. Cicero wrote several letters to provincial governors on his behalf (Fam. 13.75, c. 52 BC, and by reference in 13.35, 46 BC). He was the patron after whom C. Avianius Philoxenus took the nomen Avianius upon receiving Roman citizenship.

A son of Cicero's intimate friend C. Avianius Flaccus, sharing his father's praenomen Gaius; an intimate of Cicero whom he held uniquely dear. In 46 BC he was in Sicily, and Cicero commends him and his brother Marcus to A. Allienus, proconsul (Fam. 13.79). Distinct from his father (person:c-avianius-flaccus); the shared praenomen makes the two hard to disentangle.

Freedman of M. Aemilius Avianius, managing his patron's estate at Sicyon (Achaea) as procurator in his patron's absence. He had personally assisted Cicero in difficult times with exceptional loyalty. Cicero recommends him to Servius Sulpicius in Fam. 13.21. His cognomen Hammonius (from the Egyptian god Ammon) indicates likely eastern Mediterranean origin.

Old guest-friend (hospes antiquus) of Cicero with business in Sicily. Through Cicero's intercession Julius Caesar enrolled him among the citizens of Novum Comum. He took the nomen Avianius in honour of his principal patron C. Avianius Flaccus (Fam. 13.35, 46 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A tribune of the plebs in 51/50 BC who repeatedly interposed his veto against the senate's decrees on the consular provinces and Caesar's soldiers. Distinct from C. Caelius Caldus (cos. 94).
Judge addressed by Cicero in Pro Roscio Comoedo; consul 67 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Gaius Cassius of Parma, poet and one of Caesar's assassins; quaestor/proquaestor with the Liberators' eastern fleet in 43 BC and sender of Ad Familiares 12.13. Frequently confused in the manuscripts with the more famous C. Cassius Longinus. The last of the conspirators to die, executed on Octavian's orders after Actium (30 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Consul 49 BC alongside Lentulus Crus; one of three Marcelli of the Caesarian-civil-war years; cousin of M. Marcellus, consul 51 BC.
Consul 50 BC; brother of C. Marcellus cos. 49 and cousin of M. Marcellus cos. 51. Elected in summer 51 BC, his promotion is celebrated in Cicero's letters from the Cilician journey (Fam. 15.8, 15.9).
(stub --- to be enriched)

aliases: Gaius Claudius, son of Gaius, of the Palatine tribe
