entities.short_name: C. Claudius Pulcher, Appi filius, praetor
aliases: Gaius Claudius Pulcher, son of Appius, the praetor
entities.summary: (stub --- to be enriched)

An otherwise unidentified son of a Gaius Clodius, named in Att. 3.17 in connection with the threatened prosecution of Quintus Cicero in 58 BC; the manuscript reading and the man's identity are uncertain. Distinct from the children of the tribune P. Clodius Pulcher, Cicero's enemy.

Younger brother of Marcus Clodius Archagathus; also from Halaesa, Sicily. Shares the same Roman nomen (Clodius) with Greek cognomen (Philo). Connected to Cicero by hospitium and familiaritas; commended along with his brother to the proconsul M'. Acilius Glabrio in 13.32.

Addressee of Fam. 13.7; held a commission (negotium) from Caesar relating to land in Cisalpine Gaul, suggesting an administrative or land-allocation role. The MS headnote gives praenomen Gaius. Possibly identical with the Marcus Cluvius who was a banker at Puteoli (person:m-cluvius-puteolanus), but the C. praenomen and Gallic commission leave the identification uncertain; flagged as crux.

Quaestor assigned by lot to Cicero in Cilicia, 50 BC, and left in charge of the province (as a young man of noble family) when Cicero departed in summer 50 BC. Grandson of the consul of 94 BC; distinct from person:c-caelius-caldus-cos94 and the veto-tribune person:c-caelius-tr50.
A man named by the informer Cn. Nerius in February 56 BC as a subscriptor (accessory accuser) in the bribery prosecution of P. Sestius. Otherwise unidentified.

One of the twenty land-commissioners (XXviri) under Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC; on his death Cicero was invited to fill his place, an offer Cicero declined as disgraceful. FLAG: distinct from the existing person:cosconius (the beast-dealer of 56 BC, Q. Fr. 2.4).
Roman orator (124-73 BC), consul 75; an interlocutor in De Oratore as a young man. Cicero's source for the conversation: De Oratore is presented as Cotta's recollection.
Roman consul 75 BC, the orator of De Oratore. Burned with desire for a triumph but was prevented by death.
Roman consul 76 BC, governor of Macedonia, triumphed in 72 BC over the Dardanians. Father of the tribune of 50 BC.

Freedman of C. Rabirius Postumus (taking the nomen Curtius from his patron's adoptive family); a businessman based at Ephesus who hosted Cicero there and managed Cicero's affairs in Asia. Commended to P. Servilius Isauricus in Fam. 13.69 over a property dispute with a man of Colophon.

Praetor (probably praetor peregrinus) in 50 BC, recipient of Fam. 13.59, in which Cicero commends his old friend M. Fadius and asks for easy access to his tribunal. Distinct from the other Curtii in the corpus (M. Curtius, C. Curtius Volaterranus, Q. Curtius the juror).

A protege for whom Cicero sought a military tribunate under Caesar in Gaul in 54 BC; Caesar granted it by name. Probably the partisan later prominent as a keen Caesarian. Identification of the praenomen is conjectural.

A Roman citizen from or associated with Volaterrae, a close friend of Cicero since boyhood. He lost all his property during the Sullan proscriptions and subsequently settled on a holding in the territory of Volaterrae. Caesar enrolled him in the Senate after the civil war, but his senatorial property qualification was at risk if Q. Valerius Orca distributed the Volaterran land, making Cicero's intercession in Fam. 13.5 especially urgent. Distinct from the Marcus Curtius in the registry.

A man to whom Quintus Cicero sent a jesting but savage letter ordering the kidnapper Licinius and his son to be burnt alive (Q.fr 1.2). Identity otherwise unknown; Cicero treats him as obscure ('nescio quem').
Prosecutor in Pro Roscio Comoedo; suing Roscius the actor over a partnership in the slave-actor Panurgus.
Roman consul 122 BC, son-in-law of C. Laelius Sapiens and a historian; an interlocutor in the dialogue of Cicero's De Re Publica. Distinct from the other Fannii of the registry.
Praetor in 49 BC, a Pompeian sent ahead to Sicily with imperium when Postumius refused to go without Cato (Att. 7.15.2). Distinct from the annalist-historian C. Fannius (person:c-fannius-historian).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman commander in Gaul at the time of the events of Pro Quinctio (83 BC); Quinctius took refuge with him after being driven from the partnership land.
Gaius Flaccus, son of Lucius, who informed Cicero in 51 BC that Appius held him in high esteem. Uncertain identity; distinct from the earlier commander Gaius Valerius Flaccus.

Honourable and distinguished Roman knight (eques Romanus) who was very close to Cicero's son-in-law Gaius Calpurnius Piso Frugi; together with his brother Lucius Flavius he shows Cicero assiduous attention. Commended to the proconsul M'. Acilius Glabrio in Sicily for generous treatment (13.31).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A creditor in the affair of L. Octavius Naso's estate in Asia; Quintus had ordered that nothing of the estate be diminished until Fundanius's debt was paid, which L. Flavius (the heir) disputed (Q.fr 1.2). A friend of Cicero.

A jurisconsult and financial agent close to Cicero, frequently named in the letters as an adviser on legal and money matters. Cicero tells Terentia to consult him along with Atticus at the outbreak of the civil war.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman tribune of the plebs 123-122 BC, brother of Tiberius Gracchus; the second great popularis orator; killed in 121 BC. The famous Whither-shall-I-flee passage (De Or. 3.214) survives only here.

Tribune of the plebs 60 BC who at Att. 1.18.4 began the process of transferring P. Clodius to the plebs and proposed a popular vote on the matter in the Campus Martius. Atticus's fellow tribesman; his father Sextus had bribed that tribe. Distinct from Herennius Gallus the actor and from T. Herennius.
Roman orator and tragic poet (c. 130-87 BC), aedile 90, half-brother of Catulus. Speaker in De Oratore Book 2 on humour. Killed in the Marian-Sullan strife.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman annalistic historian and tribune (tr. pl. 73 BC); prosecuted by Cicero (praetor 66) for extortion and condemned, an event Cicero reports with satisfaction at Att 1.4.2 (66 BC). Father of the poet C. Licinius Macer Calvus (person:licinius-calvus).

aliases: C. Lucilius C. f. Pup. Hirrus
entities.summary: Tribune of the plebs 53 BC, a kinsman and partisan of Pompey; a witness to the senatus consultum of October 51 BC. After a humiliating defeat (by Caelius, for an augurate or aedileship) he turned to anti-Caesarian posturing, much mocked in Fam. 8.9.

Roman citizen described by Cicero as his cliens; exiled at some point and during his exile granted Patraean citizenship, under which he adopted the son of Lyso of Patrae, thereby creating a disputed inheritance case requiring Servius Sulpicius's oversight as proconsul (Fam. 13.19).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman consul 137 BC; surrendered to the Numantines after defeat, was handed over to them by senatorial decree, but the surrender was rejected and his civil status was contested.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman consul of 64 BC with L. Iulius Caesar; Cicero dates the birth of his son in Att 1.2.1 by their consulship. Distinct from the Figulus entities of the 40s BC (person:l-figulus, person:p-nigidius-figulus).
Roman general and statesman (157-86 BC), seven-time consul; victor over Jugurtha and over the Cimbri and Teutones. The dominant political figure of Cicero's youth.

The Roman official appointed by the Senate as Cicero's successor in Cilicia, 50 BC, with instructions to march with the legions decreed for Syria; identity uncertain beyond the cognomen Marius. Not the great general C. Marius (person:c-marius, 157-86 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Gaius Memmius (praetor 58 BC), orator and literary patron — Lucretius dedicated the De Rerum Natura to him. He acquired the site of Epicurus's ruined house and garden at Athens, intending to build there; Fam. 13.1 asks him to relinquish the claim so the Epicurean Patro can preserve the school's holy ground. Convicted of ambitus in 52 BC, he withdrew into exile at Athens and then Mytilene.

Tribune of the plebs 57 BC and active partisan of Pompey; author of the sweeping 'Messian bill' of September 57 that would have given Pompey control of all moneys, a fleet, an army, and overriding command in the provinces for the corn supply. Later aedile and a legate of Caesar.
Son of Gaius (C. f.), recipient of Fam. 13.60, in which Cicero commends the freedman L. Livineius Trypho. Distinct from L. Munatius Plancus and T. Munatius Plancus Bursa.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman equestrian from the municipality of Suessa Aurunca who served as the leading soldier (princeps) of the eighth century under Q. Metellus Creticus in the Cretan War (69–67 BC). In 43 BC he sought distinction through Brutus's patronage, recommended by Cicero.
Tribune of the plebs c. 103 BC; prosecuted Caepio and was himself prosecuted under the lex Appuleia de maiestate; defended (and acquitted) by Antonius the orator.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Praetor 61 BC and governor of Macedonia (60 BC), praised by Cicero as a model of a mild yet severe provincial judge; father of the future emperor Augustus.

Plebeian Papirius, an orator and senator killed in the massacre carried out by the praetor L. Iunius Brutus Damasippus in 82 BC just before Sulla's final victory (Fam. 9.21). Cicero rates him the only Carbo of any worth to the commonwealth. Distinct from C. Papirius Carbo cos. 120 (person:c-papirius-carbo-cos120).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A Roman officer reported killed in the Mutina campaign of April 43 BC, named by Pollio (Fam. 10.33) among the fallen. Distinct from Cicero's friend Sextus Peducaeus.
Cicero's first son-in-law, husband of Tullia (married c. 63 BC); he died young in 57 BC, much mourned by Cicero. Of the Calpurnian gens but a different branch from L. Piso Caesoninus.

Praetor 63 BC who as governor of Gallia Narbonensis defeated the Allobroges; later one of Cicero's legates in Cilicia (51 BC), commanding part of the army in the Amanus campaign and the capture of Commoris (Fam. 15.4).

Tribune of the plebs 56 BC, an ally of the demagogues and (despite the shared name) distinct from M. Cato Uticensis. His promulgatio harassed Lentulus Spinther's Egyptian command, and in February 56 he attacked Pompey bitterly in the Senate (Fam 1.5a, 1.5b).
Brother of Publius Quinctius; partner with Sextus Naevius in a Gallic cattle and farming business; died in Gaul before the events of the speech.

Roman financier (eques), son of the publican C. Curtius and adopted by his maternal uncle C. Rabirius; a banker on a vast scale entangled in the Egyptian loans to Ptolemy Auletes and defended by Cicero in the Pro Rabirio Postumo (54 BC). Patron of the freedman C. Curtius Mithres and a close friend of Cicero (Fam. 13.69).
One of the heirs of Q. Turius, a businessman in the province of Africa; commended by Cicero to Cornificius (Fam. 12.26).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Tribune of the plebs 50 BC; initially anti-Caesar, he was bought over and became Caesar's chief partisan, fleeing to him in January 49 BC. Son of the consul of 76.

Roman senator and habitual prosecutor, an enemy of Cicero and Caelius; in 51 BC he was condemned for vexatious prosecution (calumnia) after charging M. Tuccius under the lex Plotia de vi. Distinct from the vaguer Sempronius of Fam. 12.25.
Roman consul 129 BC, in whose consulship (with M'. Aquillius) Cicero set the dramatic date of the dialogue of his De Re Publica, just before the death of Scipio Aemilianus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A senator listed among those present at the drafting of the senatus consultum on the consular provinces in October 51 BC.
Tribune-of-the-plebs designate for 50 BC who was condemned (apparently for electoral bribery) before taking office in 51 BC; C. Curio stood for the vacated place. Otherwise little known.
Master of the Horse who in 439 BC killed Spurius Maelius, suspected of aiming at tyranny. A stock Roman exemplum of tyrannicide, paired with the founder L. Junius Brutus.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Praetor in 49 BC; he brought a copy of Pompey's letter to the consuls to M'. Lepidus's villa near Formiae, having earlier been Lepidus's quaestor (Att. 8.6). Probably the C. Sosius who later commanded for Antony and was consul in 32 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A man of Cales, friend of Cicero and intimate of Q. Paconius Lepta, who went to Spain with M. Varro to avoid the civil war but was caught in the renewed Spanish war of 46-45 BC; Cicero commends him to Dolabella (Fam. 9.13).
Roman astronomer and statesman, consul 166 BC; predicted the lunar eclipse before the battle of Pydna for L. Aemilius Paulus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
C. Titius, a legate of Dolabella who with Sex. Marius abandoned the cargo-fleet in Lycia and fled in a warship in 43 BC (Fam. 12.15).

aliases: Gaius Titius Rufus, son of Lucius
entities.summary: Gaius Titius Rufus, son of Lucius, praetor urbanus (city praetor) at Rome ca. 50 BC; recipient of Fam. 13.58, in which Cicero commends his fellow-tribesman L. Custidius for a case to be heard at the praetor's tribunal. Possibly identical with person:c-titius, flagged for a later merge decision.

A loyalist of 43 BC who abandoned his home and fortune to join Cassius in the East; the bearer of Fam. 12.6, recommended by Cicero as a man of the best republican sympathies. Otherwise unknown; distinct from Gaius Titius and from Lucius Titius Strabo.

Gaius Trebatius Testa, the young jurist and protege whom Cicero commended to Caesar in Gaul in 54 BC (Fam. 7.5); recipient of the affectionate, teasing Cicero-Trebatius correspondence of Ad Familiares book 7 (54-44 BC), and the dedicatee of Cicero's Topica (44 BC). Later an eminent jurisconsult.

Suffect consul 45 BC and one of Caesar's assassins; as governor of Asia Minor he was captured and murdered by Dolabella at Smyrna in January 43 BC, the first of the Liberators to be killed in the aftermath of the Ides of March.
Brother of Paula Valeria, named in 50 BC; probably the C. Valerius Triarius who was a Pompeian naval commander and prosecuted Scaurus in 54 BC. Distinct from the L. Triarius of the Verrines.

The man recommended in Fam. 13.76 to the quattuorviri and decurions of a town: Cicero asks that a holding C. Valgius bought from them in the territory of Fregellae be confirmed to him free and tax-exempt. His cognomen Hippianus connects him to Cicero's friend Q. Hippius (probably by adoption or inheritance). Otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman whose heirs were the subject of a senatorial decree (senatus consultum) bearing on Caerellia's financial interests in Asia, cited by Cicero in Fam. 13.72 (46 BC). Distinct from the publicanus Sex. Vennonius (person:sex-vennonius-publicanus).

A Roman official and close friend of Cicero, kinsman (propinquus) of C. Coelius Caldus; he wrote to Cicero recommending Caldus in 50 BC. Probably identifiable with the C. Vergilius criticised alongside Quintus Cicero in Q. fr. 1.2 as a provincial governor, suggesting a former propraetor in the eastern provinces.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Urban praetor at Rome ca. 46 BC who remanded the Strabo–Cornelius debt case from the city to the province of Cisalpine Gaul (Fam. 13.14).
Mother of Atticus and of Pomponia (Quintus Cicero's wife), of the gens Caecilia; she lived to a great age. Cicero reports on her well-being to Atticus (Att. 1.5, 1.7).
Roman noblewoman, daughter of Q. Caecilius Metellus Balearicus; sheltered Sex. Roscius the younger after his father's murder.
One of the assassins of Caesar; with his brother (also a Caecilius) he was among the conspirators of the Ides of March. Cicero supposes Sestius is not put out on his account.

Urban praetor (praetor urbanus) at Rome in 43 BC who presided over Senate meetings in the consuls' absence; encountered a religious objection (auspicial scruple) when first reading Plancus's letter to the Senate.
Early Roman comic poet (d. c. 168 BC), of Insubrian Gaulish origin; Cicero cites him as a poor authority for correct Latinity compared with Terence.

An agent or freedman (the cognomen Tryphon and gentile Caecilius suggest a freedman of the Caecilii) connected with Atticus's correspondence during Cicero's exile. Mentioned only at Att. 3.8 ("Caecilius Tryphon I have not seen"); otherwise unknown. Distinct from Q. Caecilius, Atticus's uncle.
Defendant in Pro Caecina (69 BC); Etruscan landowner whose interdictum de vi armata claim against Aebutius is the form of action.
Younger orator and politician; Cicero's protege; defendant in Pro Caelio (4 April 56 BC), the famous Clodia speech; later a correspondent in the Ad Familiares.

A wealthy Roman woman of philosophical interests and a close friend of Cicero, with extensive financial interests (loans, debt-claims, and landed holdings) in the province of Asia. In Fam. 13.72 (46 BC) Cicero presses the proconsul P. Servilius to assist her affairs, citing a senatorial decree concerning the heirs of C. Vennonius.
The conqueror of Gaul, dictator, and adversary of the Republic (100–44 BC); consul 59 BC; Cicero's patron in 56 BC after Luca; assassinated on the Ides of March.

One of Quintus Cicero's subordinates in Asia whom Cicero names (with Chaerippus and Labeo) as never having fallen under suspicion of greed in two years. Identity uncertain; distinct from the several Caesii of the Verrines and Arpinum.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A celebrated Athenian tragic actor, proverbial for furious motion without progress (he 'ran without advancing a cubit'); Cicero applies the proverb ruefully to himself (Att. 13.12).

Greek historian (c. 360-327 BC), great-nephew of Aristotle and historian of Alexander the Great's expedition; executed by Alexander. In Q.fr 2.11 Cicero dismisses his history as commonplace beside Philistus.

Caesarian senator and partisan, later consul in 39 BC; in 43 BC a friend of Furnius active at Rome who joins Cicero in urging Furnius to stay at his post rather than rush home to stand for the praetorship.

Caesarian officer and senator whom Caesar named suffect consul for the last part of 31 December 45 BC, after the death of the consul Q. Fabius Maximus that same morning; the consulship of a few hours is the butt of Cicero's jokes in Fam. 7.30 and 7.24. Distinct from the Caninius Gallus entities (person:l-caninius-gallus, person:gallus-canius).

A Papirius Carbo, brother of the Cn. Carbo whom Cicero says 'we knew', characterised in Fam. 9.21 only as a scurra (a buffoon or wit). Otherwise unidentified; one of the contemporary plebeian Carbones Cicero disparages. Flagged for further identification.
Greek philosopher (214-129 BC), head of the Academy at Athens; famous for arguing on either side, including the embassy of 155 BC to Rome with Diogenes and Critolaus.

One of four men (with Censorinus, Antonius, and Scaevola) by whom Quintus Cicero reported he was deeply loved in Asia (Q.fr 1.2). Distinct from C. Cassius Longinus the tyrannicide and from the Athenian rhetor Cassius.

Gaius Cassius Longinus, brother-in-law of Marcus Brutus and, with him, a leading conspirator against Caesar; in 43 BC he was raising the republican armies in Syria and the East against the Caesarians. He was defeated with Brutus at Philippi in 42 BC.
A rhetorician at Athens with whom the younger Marcus Cicero practised Greek declamation in 44 BC. Not the tyrannicide C. Cassius Longinus.
A fickle and sordid man of equestrian census aggrieved against Quintus Cicero in Asia (Q.fr 1.2); he carries about copies of Quintus's intemperate letters. Cicero expects to soften him too.
The father of T. Catienus, whom Quintus Cicero had treated harshly in Asia and threatened in a violent letter; Cicero does not blame the harshness but the language (Q.fr 1.2).
The patrician conspirator (c. 108–62 BC) whose plot against the consular government in 63 BC Cicero exposed and crushed; killed at the battle of Pistoria in January 62 BC.

Catius the Insubrian (a Gaul), a Latin popularizer of Epicureanism in the mid-1st century BC who wrote on physics and ethics; Cassius mocks his coinage 'spectra' (Greek eidola, the films Epicurean theory said objects shed) as 'Catian spectres' (Fam. 15.19). Cicero elsewhere calls him a recently dead Epicurean.
Roman statesman (234-149 BC), consul 195, censor 184; author of the Origines and De Agri Cultura. The model of the orator-jurist-soldier-citizen for Cicero.
The Stoic statesman (95–46 BC), great-grandson of the Censor; tribune 62 BC; champion of senatorial constitutionalism; suicide at Utica after Pharsalus.

A messenger who brought two letters to Rome on the Ides of April 43 BC, one purportedly from Brutus and one from Antony, triggering a Senate crisis over Antony's claim to the title proconsul; his loyalties were suspected.

One of four men (with Antonius, Cassius, and Scaevola) by whom Quintus Cicero reported he was deeply loved in Asia, to Cicero's vehement gladness (Q.fr 1.2). Possibly L. Marcius Censorinus, later cos. 39 BC.
One of Atticus's regular letter-carriers (tabellarii), who recurs as a courier in Cicero's correspondence; in February 49 he brought Atticus's more cheerful despatch to Cicero on the Campanian coast.

The wealthy, genial old man at whose Piraeus house Plato's Republic opens; after a brief, graceful exchange with Socrates he withdraws to a sacred rite and never returns. Cicero invokes him as the model for how he eased Scaevola out of his own De Republica.
Catilinarian conspirator; possessor of the cache of swords in his house; executed with Lentulus 5 December 63 BC.
A mutual friend who visited Cicero and vividly conveyed Cornificius's affection, manner, and very expression to him (Fam. 12.30.3). Cicero calls him congenial and now charming.
One of Quintus Cicero's subordinates in Asia whom Cicero names (with Caesius and Labeo) as above suspicion of greed. Possibly but not certainly the same Chaerippus Cicero mentions warmly in 43 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Greek philosopher of the New Academy (c. 165-95 BC); pupil of Carneades. Crassus heard him at Athens in 110 BC; Cicero presents him as the foil to the rhetoricians.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek Stoic philosopher (c. 279--c. 206 BC), the third head of the Stoic school and its great systematizer, especially in logic and dialectic; held that some statements about the future are possible even if they never come to pass, against the determinist position of Diodorus Cronus. Cicero contrasts the two views in the logic-joke of Fam. 9.4.
Freedman of Cyrus the architect who carried Trebatius's verbal greetings to Cicero (Fam. 7.14). Distinct from the Stoic philosopher Chrysippus of Soli (person:chrysippus).
Sulla's most influential freedman, in charge of the proscription auctions; the unnamed power behind the prosecution in Pro Roscio Amerino.
(stub --- to be enriched)

An otherwise obscure individual who wrote a Greek-language letter reporting on military events in Asia (likely the defeat of Dolabella) and sent it to Satrius, Trebonius's legate; Brutus forwarded a copy to Cicero.
The orator, statesman, and philosopher (106–43 BC); author of the corpus. Consul 63 BC.
A freedman of Appius Claudius Pulcher who delivered a letter to Cicero in 53 BC and quickly became friendly with him.
A contractor or workman whom Cicero summoned from Venafrum for the estate works in 54 BC; on the day appointed, a collapsed tunnel killed four of his fellow slaves and apprentices at Venafrum.
An agent of Cicero (probably his and Atticus's man of business L. Cincius) who, with the freedman Philotimus, pressed forward the finishing of Cicero's town house in 54 BC.

A business agent (probably L. Cincius, an agent of Atticus) with whom Cicero conferred on Quintus's behalf about a financial matter involving Lentulus and Sestius. A recurring man of affairs in Cicero's correspondence.

Thessalian orator and diplomat, the trusted minister of Pyrrhus of Epirus, credited with epitomizing military and tactical writings. Cited in Fam. 9.25 alongside Pyrrhus as a supposed source of Paetus's strategic expertise.

A figure of Roman proverb cited in Fam. 7.24, said to have feigned sleep to ignore a wife's adultery, hence the saying 'non omnibus dormio' ('I am not asleep for everyone'). Known only from this proverbial allusion.
A party with whom Atticus is to look into a debt (nomen); the matter is complicated if Plancus is set on it (Att. 13.33).
Syracusan put in command of the Sicilian fleet by Verres in 71 BC; his abandonment of the fleet at Helorus and the resulting loss are the spine of In Verrem II.5.

Cleopatra VII, queen of Egypt and Caesar's ally and mistress, resident with her court in Caesar's gardens across the Tiber until after his assassination; Cicero refers to her with cold contempt as "the queen" (Att. 14.8, 14.20).

Greek historian (early 3rd century BC), author of a widely-read but sensationalist history of Alexander the Great, frequently cited but also criticised by ancient scholars for unreliability. Cicero mentions him as having told Caelius the story of Alexander's victory over Darius at Issus (Fam. 2.10).
Sister of P. Clodius and widow of Q. Metellus Celer; the (probable) Lesbia of Catullus; the unnamed accuser behind the prosecution in Pro Caelio.

The youngest of the three Clodia sisters (sister of P. Clodius Pulcher), wife of M. Terentius Varro Lucullus (cos. 73); seduced by C. Memmius, after which her husband divorced her (Att. 1.18.3). Distinct from Clodia Metelli (person:clodia), the widow of Metellus Celer and probable Lesbia of Catullus.
The popularis tribune (c. 93–52 BC) whose 58 BC bill drove Cicero into exile; a patrician adopted into a plebeian family to qualify for the tribunate; killed by Milo's gang on the Appian Way.
An architect whose design (genus) for the shrine of the deified Tullia pleased Cicero; Atticus was to spur him on while the site was sought.
Defendant in Pro Cluentio (66 BC); accused of poisoning his stepfather Oppianicus; Cicero's defence is the longest of the forensic speeches and turns on the eight-year shadow of the Junian trial.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman consul 81 BC, governor of Macedonia, triumphed; convicted at Cicero's prosecution in 77.
The praetor (NOT P. Cornelius Dolabella the consul) before whom Naevius later sought to compel the sponsio that became the form of action in Pro Quinctio.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus the Younger, son of Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus (consul 54 BC); he served with Brutus and Cassius as an admiral and commander, later defected to Antony, and finally served as consul in 32 BC.
Roman senator of praetorian rank who sat as a juror at Gabinius's maiestas trial in 54 BC and voted openly to acquit; later consul 53 and 40 BC and a Caesarian commander.

aliases: Gnaeus Domitius and Lucius Metellus the censors

Scribe and curule aedile of 304 BC; published the legal calendar (fasti) and the forms of legal action, the ius Flavianum.

Son of Cn. Cornelius Lentulus Clodianus (consul 72 BC); one of the three legates sent to Gaul in 60 BC, named by Cicero with derision ("the joke of it"). A minor figure, distinct from the consular Lentuli.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man named by the informer Cn. Nerius in February 56 BC as a subscriptor (accessory accuser) in the bribery prosecution of P. Sestius. Otherwise unidentified.
An informer of the Pupinian tribe who in February 56 BC laid an electoral-bribery (ambitus) charge against P. Sestius, also naming Cn. Lentulus Vatia and C. Cornelius as accessories.

Cn. Octavius, a well-born friend of Trebatius who repeatedly invited Cicero to dinner in 54 BC (Fam. 7.16, and 7.9 where Cicero teases that he forgets whether the man is an Octavius or a Cornelius, 'a son of the earth').

Equestrian Roman businessman on intimate terms with Cicero. In 46 BC he had commercial interests in Sicily managed by three freedmen (Hilarus, Antigonus, Demostratus). Cicero recommends him warmly to the proconsul M'. Acilius Glabrio (Fam. 13.33).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Quaestor of Macedonia in 58 BC who sheltered the exiled Cicero at Thessalonica and Dyrrhachium. Later the defendant in Cicero's Pro Plancio (54 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Elder son of Pompey the Great; commanded the surviving Pompeian forces in Spain in 45 BC. Cassius, writing from Brundisium in January 45 BC, dreads his victory, calling him a fool who mistakes cruelty for virtue (Fam. 15.19). Defeated by Caesar at Munda in March 45 BC and killed soon after. Distinct from his brother Sextus Pompeius (person:sextus-pompeius).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Cnaeus Pupius, an operative agent (in operis) of the Bithynian publicani company, commended by Cicero to the quaestor Crassipes in Ad Familiares 13.9. Nothing further is known of him.
(stub --- to be enriched)
One of the heirs of Q. Turius, a businessman in the province of Africa; commended by Cicero to Cornificius (Fam. 12.26). Not the demagogue tribune L. Appuleius Saturninus.

A young man (adulescens) of disreputable earlier life prosecuted by Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus in 50 BC, his trial awaited after the acquittal of Sex. Peducaeus (Fam. 8.14). Distinct from the Africa businessman person:cn-saturninus and from the tribune L. Appuleius Saturninus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Senator and noted authority on agriculture (a speaker in Varro's De Re Rustica); a friend of Cicero whom Cicero asks to help win over the tribune Hirrus. Distinct from the earlier Tremellii.

A person (probably a debtor or party to a money matter) whose business Cicero had entrusted both to Atticus and apparently to Varro; mentioned only in passing in Fam. 9.4. The manuscript reading 'de Coctio' is usually emended to Cocceius. Identity otherwise unknown.

The young chief priest of Comana in Cappadocia in 51 BC, possessed of cavalry, infantry, and money and backed by the partisans of change; thought likely to take up arms until Cicero induced him to withdraw from the kingdom. Probably an Archelaus of the Comanian priestly dynasty; identity uncertain.
Athenian lyre-player (kitharist), the music teacher of the aged Socrates; proverbially distinguished. Cited in Fam. 9.22 as an example of a perfectly innocent name that sounds indecent.

A financier to whom Cicero thinks of turning for a loan at Att. 1.12.1, alongside Axius and Selicius; probably the wealthy senator-banker Q. Considius. Identity flagged, not merged with C. Considius Nonianus (the senator who drew Cisalpine Gaul in 49 BC).
Senator who in 49 BC drew Hither Gaul (Cisalpine Gaul) by lot; Caesar's January peace terms proposed handing the province over to him.
The early-Republican Roman patrician who, after his exile, led a Volscian army against Rome. Cicero cites him among those who impiously sought foreign aid against the fatherland.
Wife (or close female relative) of P. Sestius; in 62 BC she met with Cicero's wife Terentia to press Sestius's request that no successor be appointed to him.

Quaestor in Further Spain under Asinius Pollio in 43 BC; nephew of the elder L. Cornelius Balbus, Caesar's confidant. Pollio's letter (Fam. 10.32) recounts his outrages at Gades before he absconded with the provincial treasury.

A friend of Asinius Pollio in Spain in 43 BC, named as the keeper of the text of Balbus's praetexta; later the elegiac poet and first prefect of Egypt under Augustus. Distinct from the jurist C. Aquilius Gallus.

A jurist named Cornelius in the Trebatius correspondence: in Fam. 7.8 the teacher 'from whom you profess to have learned how to be wise,' and in Fam. 7.17 the authority Cicero mock-cites in jurists' citation-formula ('this too was the view of Q. Cornelius'). Plausibly one man; identity otherwise uncertain.
(stub --- to be enriched)

An agent or intermediary in the confidential 'Teucris' business at Att. 1.12.1, who 'did not come back to Terentia'; otherwise unidentified. Distinct from M. Cornelius the letter-carrier of Att. 1.13.1 and from the other Cornelii of the corpus.

A senatorial ally of the tribune Serranus in the house-restitution debate of October 57 BC; to win Serranus a night's delay he theatrically threw off his toga and flung himself at his son-in-law's feet (Att. 4.2.4). Identity otherwise obscure; the nickname suggests a 'horn-blower'.
Daughter of Quintus Cornificius; an older, much-married woman floated as a possible bride in Cicero's circle, a match he found unappealing (Att. 13.28).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A messenger, evidently a freedman or dependent of Q. Cornificius bearing the same name, who delivered Cornificius's letter to Cicero on the Liberalia of 43 BC; distinct from the proconsul Q. Cornificius.
A tribune of the plebs in 61 BC whose affected Catonian sternness earned him Cicero's nickname 'pseudo-Cato' (Att. 1.14.6). Distinct from M. Caecilius Cornutus, urban praetor in 43 BC.
An architect in the service of Cornelius Balbus, said to be a fine builder, whom Cicero awaited at his villa in 44 BC (Att. 14.3).
A dealer from whom C. Cato bought beast-fighters (bestiarii) for his bodyguard in 56 BC. Otherwise unidentified.
A man (an Aurelius Cotta) whom Pompey favoured in the consular jockeying of 53 BC over Milo, saying he would have Caesar press against Milo. Precise identity uncertain.
King of the Sapaean Thracians, a Roman ally; bought from Piso the head of Rabocentus, chief of the rival Bessian people, for 300 talents.
Cicero's son-in-law (briefly): married Tullia in early 56 BC after her widowhood; the marriage did not last. The horti Crassipedis are Cicero's favourite suburban dining spot in 55 BC.
The wealthiest Roman of his age (115–53 BC); consul 70, 55 BC; suppressor of Spartacus; member of the First Triumvirate; killed at Carrhae.
A physician in whom Cicero says he 'trusts' over the illness of Atticus's daughter Attica in early 45 BC; Craterus was a celebrated Greek doctor of the age.

The leading Peripatetic philosopher of his day; the younger Marcus Cicero studied under him at Athens (44 BC), and Cicero the elder admired him highly. Distinct from the Sicilian Cratippus of Tyndaris.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man about whose affairs (apparently a recent death and its public settlement) Cicero asks Atticus for news, alongside Metellus and Balbinus (Att. 13.21).

A man whose auction (auctio) had taken place at Rome in early 56 BC, mentioned by Cicero in connection with property he might buy. Not securely identified with either L. Culleolus or Q. Terentius Culleo.

A Curius who was consobrinus (maternal cousin) of C. Coelius Caldus (person:c-coelius-caldus-q50) and on intimate terms with Cicero; he wrote a warm letter to Cicero recommending his cousin. Not to be confused with person:curius-procos (the proconsul of Fam. 13.49) or person:mn-curius-patrensis (the banker of Patrae).

A proconsul named Curius, recipient of the one-line recommendation Fam. 13.49 commending Q. Pompeius Sex. f. Identity uncertain; the Perseus TEI for this letter is mislabeled n=159. Not to be confused with the banker M'. Curius of Patrae (person:mn-curius-patrensis).
A man (called a scoundrel by Cicero) who had taken possession of the estate of Sextilius in the territory of Fundi in 44 BC (Att. 14.6, 14.10).

A man named Curtius whose position (probably a vacant place on a board or in a college) is being refilled, about whom Cicero asks Atticus for fuller news at Att. 2.5.3. Not securely identified with any of the other Curtii in the corpus.

Curtius Nicias of Cos, a Greek grammarian and Homeric scholar in Cicero's and Dolabella's circle, noted as a wit and gourmet; here a party to a lawsuit Cicero is judging (Fam. 9.10) and recalled for the mushrooms served at his table.
Owner of suburban gardens (the horti Cusinii) across the Tiber that Cicero weighed among the possible sites for Tullia's shrine in 45 BC.
Persian king and founder of the Achaemenid empire (c. 600-530 BC); for Cicero, the type of the just monarch.
A Roman architect, patron of the freedman Chrysippus Vettius (Fam. 7.14); a builder also named in Cicero's letters to Atticus and Quintus. Distinct from Cyrus the Great (person:cyrus).

Celebrated mime-actress and courtesan, a freedwoman of P. Volumnius Eutrapelus (hence Volumnia); mistress of Mark Antony and later of the poet Cornelius Gallus (who called her Lycoris). Her presence at the dinner in Fam. 9.26 prompts Cicero's defence of his social ease.
A courageous officer whom Cicero esteemed, prefect of the evocati (re-enlisted veterans), sent in 51 BC to take over three cohorts from Appius. Otherwise little known.
Tribune of the plebs in 44 BC and a senatorial commander in the Mutina campaign, killed in the fighting around Forum Gallorum / Mutina in April 43 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman senator, consul 62 BC (with L. Licinius Murena), husband of Servilia; in 63 BC, as consul-designate, he spoke first in the Senate for the death penalty against the Catilinarians. At Att 1.1.2 (65 BC) Cicero reckons him a weak candidate contending with Thermus. Distinct from M. Iunius Silanus of 43 BC (person:m-iunius-silanus).

A Pompeian partisan (tribune of the plebs 54 BC, later a naval legate of Pompey); after Pharsalus he returned to Italy and, like Cicero, was named by exception in Antony's edict and left waiting at Brundisium for Caesar's pardon. Cicero calls him his only partner in his 'fault' (Att. 11.14).
A man entrusted by Appius Claudius Pulcher with commissions for Cicero in 50 BC, but who (per Fam. 3.11.5) had failed to make contact with Cicero; otherwise unknown.
A learned companion (Cicero calls him chrēstomathēs, 'fond of useful learning') staying with Quintus and Pomponia at the Arpinum estate in early 67 BC (Att. 1.6); otherwise unknown.

A Roman art-dealer and connoisseur active in the 40s BC, through whom the statues Gallus bought for Cicero (Fam. 7.23) were to pass; Cicero coins 'Pseudodamasippus' for any rival dealer. Plausibly the Damasippus whose bankruptcy and turn to Stoic philosophy Horace mocks in Satires 2.3.

The last Achaemenid king of Persia (r. 336-330 BC), defeated by Alexander the Great at the battles of Issus (333 BC) and Gaugamela (331 BC) and later murdered by his own satrap Bessus. Mentioned by Cicero in Fam. 2.10 in connection with the plain of Issus.

A designator (master-of-ceremonies or marshal at games and funerals) who remained one of the last free associates of Clodius after his followers had deserted him in late 57 BC (Att. 4.3.2). Otherwise unknown.

Decimus Iunius Brutus Albinus, one of Caesar's assassins (distinct from Marcus Brutus); as governor of Cisalpine Gaul he was besieged by Antony in Mutina in 43 BC. After the siege was lifted his troops deserted him, and he was killed while trying to reach Macedonia.

A librarius (secretary/copyist) who came to Cicero in 62 BC on behalf of P. Sestius to ask that no successor be appointed to Sestius's proquaestorship that year; Cicero judged him honest and friendly to Sestius.

A figure named Deiotarus who served as a messenger or officer bringing dispatches to Brutus in May 43 BC; likely a son or kinsman of Deiotarus I, tetrarch of Galatia, who had been a client-king and subject of Cicero's speech Pro Rege Deiotaro.
A letter-carrier shuttling between Cicero and Atticus in the spring of 45 BC (Att. 13.30-13.31).

A man in Cicero's circle in 45 BC whom Cicero is sending back and forth between himself (at Astura/Tusculum) and Tiro; from whose entourage Cicero says he has 'prised himself loose'. Not certainly identifiable with the other Demetrii in the corpus.
A courier or freedman of Atticus's mentioned in Att. 4.11 as the man through whom certain matters can be settled; not certainly identifiable with Demetrius of Magnesia.
A man Cicero mocks in a letter to Tiro of 45 BC, punning on Demetrius of Phalerum and the vulgar name Billienus to call him no philosopher but a rogue. Otherwise unidentified.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Master of the slave Bellienus who held the garrison post at Intimilium in 49 BC. Otherwise unidentified and not securely the same as any other Demetrius in the corpus; uncertain.
Greek polymath, biographer and bibliographer of Cicero's day; cited in the letters as a literary correspondent.

Sicilian Greek, Cicero's closest Sicilian personal friend. At Cicero's request, P. Cornelius Dolabella obtained Roman citizenship for him from Caesar; upon enfranchisement he took the name Publius Cornelius. When Caesar's citizenship tablet was revoked, Caesar personally confirmed Megas's grant in Cicero's presence (Fam. 13.36, 46 BC).

The Presocratic atomist philosopher of Abdera (c. 460–c. 370 BC), founder with Leucippus of the atomic theory and source of the eidola (images) doctrine that Epicurus inherited. Cited in Fam. 15.16 as the originator, before Epicurus, of the theory of perception by images. Distinct from person:democritus-sicyonius, Cicero's Achaean guest-friend.

A Greek of Sicyon, guest-friend (hospes) and intimate of Cicero, praised for his probity, worth, and liberality toward his guests. Cicero describes him as nearly the foremost man of Achaea and commends him to A. Allienus (Fam. 13.78, 46 BC). Otherwise unknown.
Athenian orator and statesman (384-322 BC); the supreme Greek orator of antiquity in Cicero's view. The Philippics and the De Corona are his canonical works.
Freedman of Gnaeus Otacilius Naso, one of three agents managing Naso's business in the province of Sicily c. 46 BC (Fam. 13.33).
A letter-carrier used by Cicero during his exile in 58 BC, charged to return quickly with news from Rome; probably a household slave or freedman.

Husband of Licinia (sister of C. Cassius Longinus), who had set out for Spain at the time of Fam. 7.23 (c. 46 BC); his absence kept Licinia from moving house. The reading and identity are uncertain; flag-not-resolve.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Dicaearchus of Messana (fl. late 4th c. BC), Peripatetic philosopher, geographer, and political theorist, a pupil of Aristotle. Author of constitutional treatises (On the Pellenians, On the Corinthians, On the Athenians) which Cicero was reading with admiration.
Head of the Egyptian embassy of 57 BC opposing Ptolemy XII Auletes; murdered in Rome under suspicious circumstances. Caelius was charged with complicity in the murder.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A freedman of Julius Caesar who carried Caesar's dispatches from Alexandria to Italy; Cicero, who had not seen him, tells Atticus to question him for news of the war and of young Quintus (Att. 11.6).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek philosopher of the Megarian (Dialectical) school (d. c. 284 BC), famous for the Master Argument and his definition of the possible: only what is or will be true is possible, so the future is fixed. Cicero plays with this determinism in Fam. 9.4 (if Varro will come, his coming is necessary; if not, it is impossible).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A freedman of L. Lucullus whose report of a bargain (concerning Hermias) provoked Cicero's angry letter to Quintus (Q.fr 1.2). Not the Stoic Diodotus who lived in Cicero's house.

Greek Stoic philosopher and Cicero's lifelong companion: lived in Cicero's house from the orator's youth to his own death in 60 BC, blind in his last years; left Cicero his fortune. The teacher whose lessons in dialectic Cicero refers back to in the philosophical works.
A Greek agent whom Caelius sent out to Cicero in Cilicia in 51 BC, together with the freedman Philo, on the business of the beasts and the Sittian bond.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A philosopher whose lectures Paetus attended; in Fam. 9.26 Cicero alludes to a 'question' (zetema) Paetus had posed to him. FLAG: otherwise unidentified, distinct from the several other men named Dio/Dionysius in the registry.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Tyrant of Syracuse (c. 432-367 BC); for Cicero, with Phalaris, the standard exemplar of tyranny.

A slave of Cicero who managed his valuable library, stole many books, falsely claimed to have been manumitted, and fled to Illyricum, where he was seen at Narona (Fam. 13.77, 46 BC). Distinct from the several free men named Dionysius in the corpus, including Cicero's learned freedman M. Pomponius Dionysius.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A dilatory building contractor employed on Quintus's villa at the Manilian estate in 54 BC; Cicero mocks his slowness and his crooked column-setting, hoping Caesius will keep the work on track.

A tragic actor (tragoedus) who at the ludi Apollinares of 59 BC pointedly directed lines of his play against Pompey, which the audience took up as anti-Pompeian. FLAG: distinct from person:diphilus-contractor (the building-contractor Diphilus of Q. Fr.).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Caesarian, consul 44 BC, married to Tullia 50 BC. Charming and impossible; a constant source of correspondence-borne anxiety to Cicero.

A local notable in the Ligurian district of Intimilium and a guest-friend (hospes) of Caesar, strangled by the slave Bellienus in 49 BC; his death set the town in arms. Distinct from L. Domitius Ahenobarbus, the Pompeian consul of 54.
(stub --- to be enriched)

An otherwise obscure man from Pisaurum on the Adriatic coast, named at Att. 2.7 as a likely recipient of the lucrative "fat" legation for collecting monies in 59 BC. Distinct from the famous tribune M. Livius Drusus.
A man named in Cicero's account of Milo's finances in 51 BC, connected with the management or claims on Milo's estate.
Roman knight and friend of Atticus and Cicero, often acting as a financial agent; appears in Att. 4.12 and the later Atticus correspondence.
Roman epic poet (239--169 BC); author of the Annales (the first Roman national epic in hexameters); revered by Cicero as the great early Latin poet, frequently quoted in the corpus.
Greek historian (c. 400-c. 330 BC), pupil of Isocrates; author of the lost universal history in 30 books.
Sicilian comic poet and gnomic philosopher of the early 5th century BC; Cicero quotes a maxim ascribed to him ('know how he uses another') in reproaching Quintus.
A leading Athenian citizen ('princeps Atheniensium') in the social circle of the younger Marcus Cicero at Athens in 44 BC; otherwise unknown. Distinct from the Sicilian Epicrates of the Verrines.

A code-name (Greek 'the powerful') in the Cicero-Atticus correspondence, usually read by editors as Pompey, here said to have been 'wanton' (lascivus). FLAG: very likely person:pompey but tagged separately pending PM merge; distinct from the unrelated person:epicrates-atheniensis and person:epicrates-bidinus.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Epicurus (341–270 BC), founder of the Epicurean philosophical school, owned a house and garden in Athens (the famous 'Garden') that served as the school's cult centre. The ruined walls of this property are the object of dispute in Fam. 13.1, with Patro seeking to preserve or rebuild on the site.

Wife of T. Ampius Balbus; named in Fam. 6.12.3 (46 BC) where Cicero notes that her conversation and tears had revealed how anxious Ampius was about his pardon, prompting Cicero to write this reassuring letter.

Polymath and chief librarian at Alexandria (3rd c. BC), founder of scientific geography and the first to compute the earth's circumference. Cicero took him as his model for a planned geographical work but found him faulted by later critics.

A slave or freedman in Atticus's service who handled his accounts and correspondence; in 45 BC he carries letters and reckons sums between Cicero and Atticus (Att. 13.12, 13.30). Distinct from Eros Turius, freedman of Q. Turius.
A freedman of Q. Turius who was attempting to make off with (avert) the Turian inheritance from the legitimate heirs. Cicero asks Cornificius to stop him (Fam. 12.26).
Prosecutor in Pro Roscio Amerino; Cicero treats him with deliberate contempt as a hireling of the Roscii.

(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
The Athenian tragedian (c. 480-406 BC); Quintus Cicero quotes a line of his on cold and the delicate constitution in Fam. 16.8, calling each of his verses a piece of testimony.

Leading citizen of Mylasa in Caria, on whom Cicero relied (when at Ephesus during his Cilician proconsulship of 51--50 BC) to send Greek civic representatives (ekdikoi) to Rome to settle the city's debt to the publican-banker Cluvius. Probably the same Euthydemus mentioned by Strabo as a magnate of the city.
A freedman of Atticus, manumitted under Atticus's new nomen so as to become T. Caecilius Eutychides (Att. 4.15.1). Cicero recalls his sympathy during the exile and welcomes the manumission warmly.
Caesar's secretary (scriba); a sum recoverable through Faberius — the 'nomen Faberianum' — was the financial linchpin of Cicero's plan to buy gardens for Tullia's shrine in 45 BC.
A man named alongside Metellus Celer as someone Clodia 'makes war with' (Att. 2.1.5, in a corrupt passage); perhaps a relation or rival of her husband. Identity uncertain. FLAG.
A tribune of the plebs designate (for 57 BC) whom Cicero in exile hoped would support his recall; Q. Fabricius later proposed the recall bill in January 57 BC (Q.fr 1.4).
A tribune of the plebs designate (for 57 BC) whom Cicero in exile hoped would support his recall (Q.fr 1.4). Identity otherwise uncertain.
Daughter of the dictator L. Cornelius Sulla and wife of T. Annius Milo; her property and dowry figure in Cicero's notes on Milo's tangled finances.
Son of the dictator Sulla (born ca. 86 BC); married Pompey's daughter; served as quaestor in Syria with Pompey; killed in Africa after Thapsus 46 BC. Owner of a notable library at Cumae.
A testator who by his own and his slave's error sealed the wrong tablets, voiding the will in which Quintus Cicero had been left a twelfth share of the estate. Otherwise unidentified.
Praetor 63 BC, propraetor of Asia 62 BC; defendant in Pro Flacco (mid-59 BC) on extortion charges; one of Cicero's allies during the Catilinarian crisis.

A subordinate active in the province of Asia in 50 BC through whom the official censure of Appius's hired witnesses might already have been arranged (Fam. 3.11.3); usually taken to be a legate of the governor of Asia. Uncertain identity; probably a Valerius Flaccus, but distinct from the propraetor of 62 BC tried in Pro Flacco.
A letter-carrier to whom Decimus Brutus entrusted a short note delivered to Cicero in May 43 BC. Named only at Fam. 11.12.
A debtor of Cicero's from whom Tiro is to extract at least part of what is owed, in a financial letter of 44 BC. Otherwise unidentified.
Former praetorian governor of Transalpine Gaul (76–73 BC); defendant in Pro Fonteio (69 BC), prosecuted for extortion by an embassy of Gauls under Indutiomarus.
Fulvia, the forceful wife of Mark Antony (earlier married to P. Clodius Pulcher and to C. Scribonius Curio); a prominent political partisan of her husbands' causes.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Gaius Furnius, a partisan of Cicero who served as legate to Munatius Plancus in Gaul in 43 BC and carried dispatches between Plancus and the Senate. He was later tribune and eventually reached the consulship under Augustus.

Tribune 67 BC (proposer of the lex Gabinia for Pompey's pirate command), consul 58 BC; one of the consuls under whom Cicero went into exile, hence one of the "two whirlwinds of the commonwealth" of Pro Sestio.

Gaius Matius Calvena, a learned Roman knight, intimate of Julius Caesar and friend of Cicero; after Caesar's death he openly mourned the dictator and helped Octavian stage Caesar's victory games, drawing criticism. Recipient of Fam. 11.27 and author of the reply 11.28.

Gaius Oppius, a Roman knight and one of Julius Caesar's chief confidential agents and men of business (with Cornelius Balbus); after Caesar's death he attached himself to Cicero's friendship. Recipient of Fam. 11.29.
A man, otherwise unknown, who left Cicero a legacy; Cicero formally entered upon Galeo's inheritance from Brundisium (Att. 11.12).

entities.canonical_name: the two Gallii (Quintus and Marcus Gallius)
entities.summary: The pair of brothers, Q. and M. Gallius, who in 51 BC prosecuted M. Calidius for electoral corruption after his defeat at the polls. Uncertain in detail; bound as the prosecuting pair Caelius names.
Tribune 56 BC, defended by Cicero on a maiestas charge in 55 BC; the trial in which Cicero ``almost killed himself'' (dirupi me paene) according to Fam. 7.1.
A close friend of Atticus; Cicero hopes his backing will help bring the gardens-owner Otho to terms (Att. 13.31).

Glyco, physician who attended consul Gaius Vibius Pansa as he died from wounds after the battle of Forum Gallorum (April 43 BC); accused by some of deliberately poisoning his patient, he was held in custody at Rome.
Greek sophist and rhetorician (c. 483-376 BC); reputedly the first to take any topic at the audience's request. The figure of Plato's Gorgias.

A rhetoric tutor at Athens useful to the younger Marcus Cicero for daily declamation; Cicero the elder ordered him dismissed in 44 BC, apparently as a bad moral influence. Author of a Greek treatise on figures. Distinct from the classical sophist Gorgias of Leontini.

The brothers Tiberius (tr.pl. 133 BC) and Gaius (tr.pl. 123–122 BC) Sempronius Gracchus, whose tribunates passed the leges Semproniae — including the citizen's right not to be put to death without trial. Cicero invokes the Gracchi as authors of the protections Verres violated (In Verrem 2.1).
A friend of Cicero and of Decimus Brutus who followed close on M. Seius in carrying news of the December 44 conference at Cicero's house back to Brutus.

Leading citizen (princeps civitatis) of Larisa in Thessaly. During Cicero's consulship of 63 BC he received beneficia from Cicero and showed lasting gratitude, becoming Cicero's hospes (guest-friend) and intimate. Cicero commends him to Servius Sulpicius Rufus, proconsul of Achaea, in Fam. 13.25. He is otherwise unattested beyond this letter.
Carthaginian general (247-c. 183 BC), commander in the Second Punic War. The figure of the Hannibal/Phormio anecdote in De Oratore 2.75-76.
A courier in Cicero's household, dispatched from Arpinum in November 44 BC ahead of a second letter to Tiro.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The Trojan hero of Homer's Iliad; here the speaker of a line from a Roman tragedy (probably Naevius's) quoted by Cicero, who praises being praised 'by a praised man.'
Wealthy and respected citizen of Messana; his shrine of Eros, a small Cupid by Praxiteles, and a Hercules by Myron were taken by Verres in 73 BC; the principal opening victim of In Verrem II.4.
A former lessee of the kitchen-garden at Cicero's Tusculan villa, who paid HS 1000 even when the plot had no amenities; cited by Cicero as a benchmark in haggling over the new rent.
Mother of Cicero and his brother Quintus. Quintus recalls her thrifty habit of sealing even emptied wine-jars in a letter to Tiro.
A Greek of Apamea in Asia whom Cicero embraced with all his civility while appeasing the Greek complainants against Quintus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek philosopher of Heraclea Pontica (4th cent. BC), a pupil of Plato known for dialogues set among ancient figures; Cicero contrasts himself with him to argue that he, a consular, should speak in his own person in De Re Publica.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A stage actor honoured by Balbus at the games at Gades in 43 BC, given a gold ring and seated among the knights in the fourteen rows. Known only from Pollio's letter (Fam. 10.32).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A slave/courier of Cicero who carried Tiro's letters in 53 BC.

The subject of a bargain in Asia about which Cicero, stirred by a report from Lucullus's freedman Diodotus, had written Quintus an angry, unbrotherly letter he later regretted (Q.fr 1.2). Identity otherwise unknown.
Leader of the people of Dionysopolis in Asia, who had been bitterly hostile to the Ciceros; Cicero won him over not by speech alone but by close friendship.
A financier from whom ready cash (some 600,000 sesterces) could be pressed to help fund the gardens purchase in 45 BC; distinct from the singer Tigellius Hermogenes.
Herodes, a Greek rhetorician and man of letters in Atticus's circle who had written attacking Cicero. Cicero jokes that he should read Dicaearchus instead of penning such letters.
Greek historian of Halicarnassus (c. 484-425 BC), author of the Histories; the canonical first historian.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The early Greek didactic poet; Cicero quotes the Works and Days ('repay in the same measure -- if you can') on requiting a literary favour (Att. 13.12).
A purchaser to whom Cicero's share of the Brinnian estate was to be conveyed by formal mancipation at Puteoli (Att. 13.50).
(stub --- to be enriched)
King of Syracuse (270–215 BC); a steady ally of Rome through the Punic Wars; framer of the lex Hieronica that governed Sicilian grain taxation under the Roman province.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A freedman of Cicero, an accountant (ratiocinator) and client of Atticus, whom rumour (Att. 1.12.2) placed with the proconsul C. Antonius in Macedonia, said to be collecting a secret share of Antonius's takings for Cicero. Distinct from Hilarus the freedman of Cn. Otacilius Naso.
Freedman of Gnaeus Otacilius Naso, one of three agents managing Naso's business in the province of Sicily c. 46 BC (Fam. 13.33).

An otherwise unknown figure who won election at Rome in 51 BC contrary to Cicero's expectations; Cicero mentions his election in Fam. 2.10 with mock-stammering surprise ('Hillo'). Identity uncertain — possibly a freedman's son or a very minor political figure.

Astronomer and geographer (2nd c. BC), founder of trigonometry and discoverer of the precession of the equinoxes, who criticized Eratosthenes' geography on mathematical grounds. Cited with Serapion at Att. 2.6 as a reason Cicero abandons his project.

Son of Philoxenus of Calacte, guest-friend and close connection of Cicero. In 46 BC Cicero wrote to proconsul Glabrio (Fam. 13.37) reporting that Hippias's property was being held in public ownership under another man's name, contrary to Calactine civic law, and asking Glabrio to intervene in equity.
Greek sophist (c. 460-400 BC), boasted of his polymath self-sufficiency: he had made the very ring, cloak, and sandals he wore (De Or. 3.127).

Tyrant of Athens, son of Pisistratus, expelled in 510 BC; he fell at the battle of Marathon (490 BC) fighting on the Persian side against his own city. Cicero cites him as one who criminally bore arms against his fatherland. Distinct from the sophists Hippias of Elis and Hippias of Calacte.

A Greek dependent (probably a freedman or learned servant) whom Quintus thought of summoning from Cicero's household in 54 BC; he set out to join Quintus in Gaul without taking a letter from Cicero, who was annoyed.

Aulus Hirtius, a literary Caesarian who continued Caesar's commentaries and was consul of 43 BC alongside Pansa. Marching to relieve the siege of Mutina, he was killed in the moment of victory there in April 43 BC.
The Greek poet of the Iliad and Odyssey; the literary touchstone for Cicero, especially in the letters where Greek tags from Homer appear constantly.
One of three men (with Venuleius and Latinus) against whom Cornificius took action in Africa in 43 BC, of which Cicero warmly approves (Fam. 12.30.6). Otherwise unknown.
The leading orator of his generation before Cicero (114–50 BC); consul 69 BC. The opposing counsel in Pro Quinctio and the prosecutor in In Verrem; later a friend.

aliases: Iamblichus the phylarch of the Arabs
entities.summary: Phylarch (tribal chief) of the Arabs of Emesa, held a friend of Rome; in 51 BC his letter confirmed to Cicero the Parthian crossing of the Euphrates and the alarm in Syria (Fam. 15.1).
A business agent or correspondent who reported to Cicero on the (failed) sale of his estates during his stay at Brundisium (Att. 11.4).
Athenian rhetorician (436-338 BC), founder of the great Athenian school of rhetoric; teacher of Theopompus, Ephorus, and many leading Greek orators.

A man named in the corrupt quotation Cicero copies from Caesar's letter at Fam. 7.5.2, whom Caesar jokingly offers to make 'king of Gaul'; the manuscript reading is garbled (M. †itfiuium†) and the figure is unidentifiable. Some editors have conjectured Mamurra, Caesar's praefectus fabrum. FLAG: textually corrupt name, identity uncertain.
Wife of C. Claudius Marcellus (cos. 50); Cicero greets her in Fam. 15.8 as gravissima atque optima femina.
Iunia Secunda, a daughter of Servilia and half-sister of Marcus Brutus; wife of Marcus Aemilius Lepidus the triumvir and mother of the children for whom Cicero and Brutus interceded in 43 BC.
Iunia Tertia (Tertulla), youngest of Servilia's daughters and half-sister of Marcus Brutus; she was the wife of Gaius Cassius Longinus.
An associate ('familiaris') of the agent Avianius, blamed together with Cicero's freedman for mishandling the statue purchase in Fam. 7.23 (c. 46 BC). Otherwise unknown; flag-not-resolve.

Roman officer serving as a legate or intermediary to Lepidus in 43 BC; Plancus used him as a go-between in attempts to draw Lepidus away from Antony. He committed suicide after Lepidus defected to Antony.
King of Numidia; ally of the Pompeians in the African campaign of 47--46 BC; defeated at Thapsus, took his own life.
Early-republican magistrate named by Cicero (Fam. 9.21) as the consular colleague of L. Papirius Crassus, four years after Crassus's dictatorship. A member of the gens Duilia.
Deformed Roman, mocked by Crassus on the bench (De Or. 2.262); the figure of the spirited self-defence with quick wit.

A wealthy Roman knight and leader of the equestrian order; he championed Cicero's recall, was banished from Rome by the consul Gabinius in 58 BC, served as aedile (probably 45 BC) with a magnificent show, and stood for the praetorship in 43 BC with Cicero's strong backing (he held it in 42 BC). Possibly the same man as the Lamia of De Oratore 2.262 (person:l-aelius-lamia); kept distinct pending verification.

A legate of Quintus Cicero in the province of Asia (c. 61-59 BC), foremost among the staff in rank, dignity, and age; a writer of history (annales). Distinct from the Stoic Q. Aelius Tubero of De Re Publica.
Son of M. Aemilius Lepidus (cos. 78); aedile 56 BC and consul 50 BC. As a young man in 56 he offered to prosecute Vatinius when produced as a witness against Sestius.
Roman general, consul 182 and 168 BC; conqueror of Perseus of Macedon at Pydna. Natural father of Scipio Aemilianus; speaker in the Somnium Scipionis.

Consul 50 BC and elder brother of the triumvir Lepidus; a republican senator who in 43 BC helped allot the consular legions and later proposed declaring Lepidus a public enemy. Distinct from his ancestor L. Aemilius Paullus Macedonicus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Pompeian legate of long standing; consul 60 BC; commander of two of Pompey's Spanish legions in 49 BC; killed in Africa in 46 BC after Thapsus.
Quinctius's travel companion to Gaul on 29 January 83 BC, named in the speech as a respectable witness to the date of departure.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A man who carried a letter from Trebatius to Cicero and reported Trebatius's instructions about it (Fam. 7.18.4). Possibly L. Arruntius the elder, the future Augustan admiral and consul of 22 BC, but the identification is uncertain.

aliases: L. Ateius L. f. an. Capito
entities.summary: A senator (probably son of the tribune C. Ateius Capito of 55 BC) listed among those present at the drafting of the senatus consultum on the consular provinces in October 51 BC. Distinct from C. Ateius Capito the friend of Cicero.
Young man, son of Lucius; one of the two Aurelii commended by Cicero to the proconsul Q. Ancharius in Fam. 13.40.
L. Aurelius Cotta, consul 65 BC and Caesar's maternal uncle. In Fam. 12.2 Cicero notes that 'in a kind of fated despair' Cotta scarcely attended the Senate after the Ides of March.

A young Roman knight (eques Romanus) and close connection of Cicero, distinguished in every respect and attentive to him. Cicero had a long friendship with his father dating from his own quaestorship in Sicily, and commends Bruttius's household, estate, and agents to the proconsul M'. Acilius Glabrio in 46 BC (Fam. 13.38).
A man of fierce temper aggrieved against Quintus Cicero in Asia whom Cicero appeased by every means (Q.fr 1.2). Distinct from the L. Caecilius witnesses and Metelli of the registry.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Half-brother of P. Cornelius Sulla and tribune of the plebs in 63 BC; in 54 BC he acted as a supporting prosecutor (subscriptor) alongside Sulla in the ambitus prosecution of A. Gabinius.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Lucius Calpurnius Bibulus, son of Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus (consul 59 BC) and Porcia (Cato's daughter), making him Brutus's cousin by marriage; he sought election as suffect consul to replace Pansa in 43 BC and later served as a naval commander under Antony.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

A friend and confidant of Cicero in the mid-40s BC who acted as an intermediary between Cicero and Varro (Fam. 9.6) and ran errands and carried messages; conventionally identified as L. Caninius Gallus (tr. pl. 56 BC). Distinct from C. Caninius Gallus (person:gallus-canius) where that identification is doubted. (Possibly identical with the tribune of 56 BC, C. Caninius Gallus = person:gallus-canius; identity uncertain, kept distinct pending review.)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A supporting subscriptor who joined C. Memmius (tribune) in the maiestas prosecution of A. Gabinius in 54 BC. Otherwise unidentified; possibly an Ateius Capito.
(stub --- to be enriched)
L. Carteius, a friend (familiaris) of C. Cassius Longinus, sent in 43 BC to transact business with Cicero on Cassius's behalf (Fam. 12.11).

aliases: Lucius Cassius and Gnaeus Servilius the censors
entities.summary: (stub --- to be enriched)
The courier to whom Caelius entrusted the first instalment of his city-news bulletin for Cicero in 51 BC. Otherwise unknown.
Leading citizen ("longe princeps") of the Etruscan municipality of Luca; commended by Cicero to Brutus as proconsul of Cisalpine Gaul (Fam. 13.13).
A confidential agent of Appius Claudius Pulcher (his praefectus fabrum) whom Cicero met at Corcyra in 51 BC and who urged him to proceed directly to Laodicea. Distinct from P. Clodius Pulcher.

Lucius Clodius, tribune of the plebs designate at the time of Ad Brutum 1.1 (43 BC), a close friend of Cicero who had been advanced by Mark Antony's patronage but was seen by Cicero as genuinely loyal to the republican cause. Not to be confused with the notorious Publius Clodius Pulcher.

A man whom Cicero, by a lapse of memory, named as supporting Ligarius in the Pro Ligario; informed by Brutus (relaying Titus Ligarius) that Corfidius had in fact already died, Cicero asks Atticus to have the name struck from every copy of the speech (Att. 13.44).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Consul 87-84 BC and leader of the Marian faction; his domination of Rome by violence and proscription (the 'Cinnae tempora') was Cicero's byword for the commonwealth held by base and ruined citizens. Killed in a mutiny in 84 BC.

L. Cornelius Lentulus Niger, flamen Martialis, an optimate of high standing who had stood for the consulship and died after an illness in 56 BC. Cicero mourns him in Att. 4.6 as a great-souled patriot spared the ruin of the commonwealth; he was the father of the L. Lentulus who prosecuted Gabinius in 54.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman citizen and friend of Cicero, linked also to T. Pomponius Atticus; shared the same voting tribe as Servius Sulpicius Rufus (tribulis), a bond Cicero exploits in his recommendation. He is the patron of the freedman L. Cossinius Anchialus, who is the actual subject of the letter (Fam. 13.23). Otherwise unattested beyond this reference.

Freedman of L. Cossinius, commended by Cicero to Servius Sulpicius Rufus in Fam. 13.23. He is described as highly regarded by his patron and by Cicero, and praised for probitas (integrity), humanitas (civility), and observantia (attentiveness). His Greek cognomen Anchialus is typical of freedmen of eastern Mediterranean origin. Distinct from the slave Anchialus (person:anchialus) of Egnatius Rufus (Fam. 13.45).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Proconsul (probably of Illyricum) c. 59 BC, recipient of Fam. 13.41-42, in which Cicero commends the business interests of L. Lucceius in his province and thanks him for acting on the first request.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Cicero's fellow tribesman (tribulis), fellow townsman (municeps) and close friend, with a case to bring before the city praetor C. Titius Rufus. Subject of the recommendation Fam. 13.58 (ca. 50 BC); otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman banker and knight, close friend of Cicero with provincial business interests in Asia and elsewhere. Cicero commends his agents and slaves in Fam. 13.43, 13.45, and other recommendations.

Praetor in 49 BC; with the younger L. Iulius Caesar he carried Caesar's peace terms between Caesar and the Pompeian leadership in January–February 49 BC (Att. 8.12). He was killed in the Mutina campaign in April 43 BC, named by Pollio among the casualties when the Martian legion was destroyed (Fam. 10.33). [Identification of the 49 BC envoy with the 43 BC casualty rests on the rare cognomen Fabatus and the attested death of L. Roscius Fabatus at Mutina — reviewer to confirm.]
Lucius Fadius, an aedile, named as the one person to whom Cicero will permit a copy of a work to be given. Distinct from the other Fadii in the correspondence.
(stub --- to be enriched)
L. Figulus, commander of Dolabella's fleet in 43 BC. In Fam. 12.13 he repeatedly dangled hope of defection, then shut himself up in the harbour of Corycus.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Tribune of the plebs 60 BC who, backed by Pompey, promulgated an agrarian bill to settle Pompey's veterans and the urban poor on land bought with the new Eastern revenues; the senate obstructed it and it lapsed. FLAG: possible duplicate of the stub person:l-flavius-eques (first_appearance in-verrem-2-1), which appears to be an earlier and distinct figure; flag-not-merge.
Roman statesman, consul 136 BC; member of the Scipionic circle. In De Re Publica book 3 he takes up the Carneadean case against natural justice.
An agent and prefect managing M. Brutus's affairs in the kingdom of Cappadocia (50 BC), to whom Cicero granted a prefecture; he behaved insolently toward Cicero.
Roman consul 72 BC, censor 70 BC; one of the senatorial elders of Cicero's day. Spoke in support of Cicero in 63 BC, declaring he had earned a civic crown.

Roman senator active in 43 BC, used by Plancus as an intermediary with Lepidus in Gaul; consul 36 BC. Remembered as a man of shifting allegiance who had earlier conspired against and then deserted the Liberators.

A long-standing intimate of Cicero, described as an excellent and most grateful man, with business interests and landholdings in the Hellespont (around Parium). Subject of the recommendation Fam. 13.53 to Q. Minucius Thermus, propraetor of Asia (51-50 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Consul 64 BC and maternal uncle of Mark Antony. A republican-leaning senator in 43 BC whose family tie to Antony tempered his votes against his nephew (Fam. 10.28).

Son of L. Iulius Caesar (cos. 64) and kinsman of the dictator; a Pompeian who served in the African war and was put to death after Thapsus in 46 BC despite the family tie. Cited at Fam. 9.7 as proof of Caesar's severity toward relatives. Distinct from his father, person:l-iulius-caesar-cos64.
Friend of P. Cuspius commended to Q. Valerius Orca in Fam. 13.6; otherwise unattested.
Founder of the Roman Republic and one of the first two consuls (509 BC); led the expulsion of the Tarquins after the rape of Lucretia.

Praetor of 82 BC and a Marian partisan who, on the eve of Sulla's victory, ordered the killing of several leading senators (including the pontifex maximus Q. Mucius Scaevola and the C. Papirius Carbo Arvina of Fam. 9.21); captured and executed by Sulla after the battle of the Colline Gate.
Son of the flamen (probably L. Cornelius Lentulus Niger, flamen Martialis); in 54 BC he laid the charge of maiestas against A. Gabinius. Distinct from the consular Lentuli.

The supreme Roman orator of Cicero's youth (140-91 BC); consul 95, censor 92, princeps senatus. Speaker in De Oratore; his death of pleurisy ten days after the Ides of September 91 BC frames Book 3 as elegy. Not to be confused with M. Licinius Crassus the triumvir.

Consul 151 BC, colleague of A. Postumius Albinus; named by Cicero only to date Postumius's consulship in his antiquarian inquiry about the ten commissioners to Greece (Att. 13.32). Grandfather of the famous L. Licinius Lucullus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Freedman of L. Regulus, commended by Cicero to C. Munatius in Fam. 13.60. Cicero loved him for his own sake on account of great services rendered during Cicero's exile, when Trypho braved many perils and repeatedly sailed in deep winter for Cicero's welfare.

A figure with whom the praetor Laterensis came to an arrangement during the suspended extortion trial of M. Servilius in 51 BC, apparently acting in Servilius's interest. Distinct from M. Lollius the agent of Metellus Nepos.
Praetor 67 BC; historian; recipient of Fam. 5.12 (the famous letter in which Cicero asks him to write up the consulship monographically).
An agent and friend of Appius Claudius Pulcher who met Cicero at Tralles in July 51 BC bearing Appius's letter and instructions. Distinct from C. Lucilius the satirist.
Consul 74 BC; commander in the Mithridatic War; brought Archias to Rome under his patronage; great-grandfather of the Lucullan luxury tradition.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Originally from Catina (Sicily) but enrolled as a decurion at Neapolis; enfranchised along with other Neapolitan residents before the Social War citizenship grants. His brother died recently at Catina leaving an inheritance; he also has longstanding business interests in Sicily. Cicero commends him to the proconsul Acilius Glabrio in 13.30.
Roman consul 65 BC; supporter of Cicero, advocate of his recall from exile. Triumphed from Macedonia.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman consul 91 BC; opponent of Drusus's reform programme. The opponent of Crassus's last great speech (De Or. 3.4).
Lucius Marcius Philippus, consul 56 BC; stepfather of the young Octavian and a villa-neighbour of Cicero on the Bay of Naples. The living Philippus of the late letters (not the consul of 91 BC).
An associate of M. Brutus and, with M. Scaptius, a creditor in the Salaminian loan; named alongside Scaptius in Cicero's account of the affair from Cilicia.

Roman senator and quaestor who served under Cicero, probably during Cicero's Cilician proconsulship (51-50 BC). Their bond (the quaestor-imperator tie) was one of the most respected in Roman public life and Cicero describes the relationship as his closest adult friendship. He is heir to his deceased brother M. Mindius, who had traded at Elis in Achaea; Cicero asks Servius Sulpicius to adjudicate the contested estate (Fam. 13.26). Otherwise unattested beyond Fam. 13.26.

A Roman officer who served under Caesar and held praetorian rank; one of the secondary conspirators in the assassination of Caesar on the Ides of March 44 BC. Cicero's cryptic one-line note (Fam. 6.15) is his immediate congratulation on that day.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Tribune of the plebs in 58 BC and a loyal partisan of Cicero, the first to move his recall and to attempt action against Clodius's measures. Cicero in exile asks Atticus how the dangerous third clause of the recall bill escaped Ninnius's notice (Att 3.23.4).
Freedman, coheir with Cicero in his patron's estate; commended to the proquaestor Appuleius in Fam. 13.46 "as one of my own household."
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

A deceased man whose estate L. Flavius (the praetor-elect of 58) inherited; Quintus Cicero's instructions to the procurators about his goods sparked Flavius's complaint (Q.fr 1.2). Distinct from the L. Octavii of the Verrines.
Roman consul 121 BC; carried the senatus consultum ultimum against Gaius Gracchus and killed him. Convicted by the Mamilian commission in 109 BC and exiled.

Lucius Oppius, son of Marcus, a businessman (negotiator) operating at Philomelium in Phrygia and a close friend of Cicero. He managed the affairs of the banker L. Egnatius Rufus; commended to Q. Gallius in Fam. 13.43 and 13.44. Distinct from C. Oppius, Caesar's agent.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Patrician Papirius, dictator (with L. Papirius Cursor as master of the horse) in the 415th year after the founding of Rome and consul four years later with K. Duilius (Fam. 9.21). Cicero notes he was the first of the gens to drop the older spelling Papisius in favour of Papirius.

Distinguished patrician Papirius bearing the cognomen Cursor ('runner'). In Fam. 9.21 he appears as master of the horse to the dictator L. Papirius Crassus and as a 'very much honoured man' in the family roll-call; the cognomen marks the celebrated line of Papirii Cursores of the Samnite Wars.

Patrician Papirius of aedilician rank (aedilicius), named in Fam. 9.21 as the founder of the line of the Papirii Massones; Cicero treats him and 'the many Massones' as among the patrician branches of the gens whose ancestor-masks Paetus might claim.

Early patrician of the gens Papiria, named by Cicero (Fam. 9.21) as the first chief of the patrician Papirii; consul and later censor with L. Sempronius Atratinus in the 312th year after the founding of Rome (mid-5th century BC by the traditional reckoning), when the name was still spelled Papisius.
Wealthy literary friend of Cicero's, resident at Naples; recipient of the long sequence Fam. 9.15--9.26 in the late 50s and 40s.
Roman senator, consul 58 BC with A. Gabinius; father-in-law of Julius Caesar (whose daughter Calpurnia he had given Caesar in marriage). Defendant of In Pisonem.

One of the creditors of Publius Varius, thought (ridiculously, says Cicero) likely to act as magister bonorum vendendorum — the foreman directing the auction of the bankrupt's goods — if the estate were sold up (Att 1.1.3).
An associate of Naevius bringing slaves for sale from Gaul; sighted Quinctius at Vada Volaterrana and reported it to Naevius, triggering the procedural ambush.
A friend of Cicero who in December 50 BC was wounded and robbed of a letter from Atticus near the tomb of Basilus on the Via Appia outside Rome. Distinct from the tribune L. Quinctius of 74 BC.
Counsel for the defence in Pro Tullio; tribune 74 BC, an opponent of senatorial control of the courts.
