A very particular friend of Cicero whose ruin (calamitas) made Cicero the more attentive to him; patron of the freedman L. Livineius Trypho. Mentioned in Fam. 13.60 (c. 46 BC). Distinct from M. Atilius Regulus, the early-Republican exemplum.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A wealthy Epicurean friend of Atticus, devoted to philosophy; Cicero jokes that Saufeius will send Atticus the philosophical 'consolation' for his grandmother's death.

Lucius Scribonius Libo (consul 34 BC), a senator connected by marriage to Sextus Pompey and Mark Antony; one of the men Lupus convened at Cicero's house in December 44 to discuss Decimus Brutus's position.

Early-republican patrician, colleague of L. Papirius Mugillanus as consul and then as censor (Fam. 9.21), in the traditional chronology around the mid-5th century BC. Distinct from the later prosecutor L. Sempronius Atratinus of Pro Caelio (person:atratinus-prosecutor).
(stub --- to be enriched)
One of the heirs of Q. Turius, a businessman in the province of Africa; commended by Cicero to Cornificius (Fam. 12.26).

Son of P. Sestius (the tribune) and grandson-in-law of C. Albinius (his mother was Albinius's daughter). Described in Fam. 13.8 as optimus adulescens. Distinct from L. Sestius Pansa (person:l-sestius-pansa) already in the registry.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A Roman whom Cicero, with C. Septimius and Laenas, sought to swear that he was excused by illness in early 45 BC; three witnesses were needed.

Roman commander serving as governor of Syria under the Caesarian regime; in early 43 BC he declared for Cassius and brought his troops to the Liberators, a key moment in consolidating their eastern power.

Lucius Tillius Cimber, one of the Liberators who participated in the assassination of Julius Caesar (44 BC); he served subsequently in the East under Brutus and Cassius and helped carry dispatches in 43 BC.

Roman knight, close friend of Cicero, owed money in Cisalpine Gaul by P. Cornelius. The case had been remanded by the urban praetor Volcacius from Rome to the province; Cicero asks Brutus to push the recovery (Fam. 13.14).

An Antonian officer who in May 43 BC led Antony's cavalry toward Pollentia but arrived an hour after Decimus Brutus's garrison had secured the town. Probably the L. Trebellius (tribune 47 BC) who had been an associate of Mark Antony; distinct from the M. Trebellius of the Pro Quinctio.
(stub --- to be enriched)

First cousin and close companion of the orator, son of his father's brother; he travelled with Cicero in Greece and on the Sicilian quaestorship. His death (c. 68 BC) is lamented at the opening of Att. 1.5.
A legate serving under Cicero in Cilicia in 51 BC, sharing command of cohorts and auxiliaries in the Amanus campaign (Fam. 15.4). Distinct from the L. Tullius of the Verrines.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A young man who went out to Athens with Cicero's son Marcus in 45 BC; Cicero corresponded with him and became entangled in his debts (the Montanus affair).

Lucius Valerius, styled jurisconsult, a member of Cicero's household and intimate circle whom Cicero commended to Appius in 53 BC; possibly the legal expert Cicero elsewhere consults. Uncertain whether identical with any other Valerius in the corpus.

A professional informer (index) at the center of the 59 BC "Vettius affair," who alleged a plot to assassinate Pompey and denounced a string of optimates. The story collapsed as incoherent and Vettius soon died in prison.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Pompey's praefectus fabrum and trusted confidant; the man with whom Cicero went late at night to Pompey's house in February 55 BC (Q.fr. 2.7).

aliases: L. Villius L. f. pom. annalis
entities.summary: A senator listed among those present at the drafting (scribendo adfuerunt) of the senatus consultum on the consular provinces in October 51 BC.
A tribune of the plebs in 51 BC who joined in vetoing the senate's decree on the consular provinces.

Consul 66 BC and a consular voice in the 56 BC senate debate over the restoration of Ptolemy Auletes; he proposed that Pompey be charged with restoring the king. Distinct from C. Volcacius Tullus, consul 33 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
One of Quintus Cicero's subordinates in Asia whom Cicero names (with Caesius and Chaerippus) as above suspicion of greed. Distinct from the later jurist-Labeones of the registry.

Decimus Laberius (c. 106–43 BC), a Roman knight and writer of mimes whom Caesar compelled to perform his own work on stage at the games of 46 BC, a public humiliation. Cicero names him with Publilius as the entertainments he watched with forced equanimity.
Crassus's mother-in-law (daughter of C. Laelius the wise); her preserved old Roman accent is praised by Crassus as 'Plautine' (De Or. 3.45).
Roman statesman (c. 188-after 129 BC), consul 140; close friend of Scipio Aemilianus. Second speaker of De Re Publica and namesake of De Amicitia.

One of three men (with C. Septimius and L. Statilius) whom Cicero hoped would swear to his being excused on grounds of illness in early 45 BC; he had undertaken the matter. Probably a Popilius Laenas.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Famous Greek courtesan of Corinth, the proverbial type of the high-class hetaira and the lover of Aristippus. Cited in Fam. 9.26 in Aristippus's quip about mastering rather than being mastered by pleasure.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Acca Larentia, a semi-mythical Roman woman honoured as a benefactress of the Roman people; variously identified as the nurse of Romulus and Remus or as a wealthy courtesan who left her estate to Rome. The pontifices offered annual sacrifice at her altar in the Velabrum on the Larentalia (23 December).

A friend and agent of A. Caecina who acted as an intermediary between the exiled Caecina and Cicero in Rome; he reported to Cicero that the Kalends of January had been set as Caecina's deadline to leave Sicily. Known only from Fam. 6.8.
One of three men (with Venuleius and Horatius) against whom Cornificius took action in Africa in 43 BC, of which Cicero warmly approves (Fam. 12.30.6). Otherwise unknown.
A member of the Cornelii Lentuli whose ships Cicero hoped to use to transport Atticus's art shipments from Greece to Italy in 67 BC (Att. 1.8.2, 1.9.2). Specific identity uncertain.
Consul 49 BC; staunch Pompeian; killed in Egypt in 48 BC after Pharsalus.
A young Cornelius Lentulus, son of a flamen, named by Vettius among the alleged conspirators of 59 BC. Otherwise obscure. [FLAG: identity uncertain.]
Consul 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; recipient of the long Egyptian-question correspondence in Ad Familiares book 1 (early 56 BC).
Praetor 63 BC, Catilinarian conspirator, executed in the Tullianum on Cicero's orders 5 December 63 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
An Athenian (probably a philosopher or teacher) in the company of the younger Marcus Cicero at Athens in 44 BC. Distinct from the Sicilian Leonidas of the Verrines.

Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, Caesar's former master of horse, in 43 BC governing Narbonese Gaul and Nearer Spain. He joined his army to Antony's after Mutina and became the third member of the triumvirate.

Sister of C. Cassius Longinus and wife of the man (Dexius) who had gone to Spain; in Fam. 7.23 (c. 46 BC) Tullia negotiated with her over the house Gallus hoped to take, but Licinia would not move while her husband was absent. Often identified with the Junia/Licinia of the Cassius-Brutus family circle; flag-not-resolve.

Roman orator and neoteric poet (82–c. 47 BC), son of the historian Licinius Macer and a friend of Catullus; leader of the Atticist movement in oratory. In Fam. 15.21 Cicero explains to Trebonius that he praised Calvus's learned but underpowered talent partly to spur him on. Distinct from C. Licinius Macer the historian.
A letter-carrier who came to Cicero late one December evening in 57 BC, after the Senate was dismissed, so that Cicero could write Quintus a report of the day's proceedings. Otherwise unidentified.
A man called by Quintus Cicero a plagiarius (kidnapper/slave-stealer) in Asia, accused with his son of extorting the tribute; Quintus's letter ordering both burnt alive scandalised Cicero (Q.fr 1.2).
A runaway slave of the tragic actor Aesopus who passed as free at Athens with Patro the Epicurean, then fled to Asia and was arrested at Ephesus; Cicero asks Quintus to recover him (Q.fr 1.2).
A wealthy Roman woman whose will (late 50 BC) named Dolabella among co-heirs to a third of the estate on condition he change his name. Not the famous Livia Drusilla, wife of Augustus.
A neighbouring landowner near Quintus's Laterium estate in 54 BC; the road to Laterium was deliberately routed to avoid his land, and he had failed to repair his own stretch.
Agent of Q. Caecilius Metellus Nepos at Rome, charged with the books of his Spanish province (Fam. 5.3).
A building contractor (redemptor) whom Cicero urged on at Quintus's building-site in Rome in 56 BC. Otherwise unidentified.

Gaius Lucilius (c. 180–103/2 BC), the founder of Roman verse satire, famous for the outspoken freedom (libertas) with which he named and lampooned prominent contemporaries. Trebonius cites him as a precedent for his own satirical licence.
Youngest brother of Mark Antony, tribune of the plebs 44 BC; in 43 BC he led Antony's advance cavalry into Transalpine Gaul. He later commanded the anti-Octavian Perusine War of 41-40 BC.
Wife of Tarquinius Collatinus, raped by Sextus Tarquinius; her suicide precipitated the expulsion of the Tarquins.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Tribune of the plebs 61 BC who, though he had entered office under a disqualifying technicality, was released from the leges Aelia et Fufia in order to promulgate a new anti-bribery law (Att. 1.16.13). Cicero calls him 'the lame man' (claudus homo).
Legendary Spartan lawgiver; established the dual kingship, the gerousia, and the famous discipline of the Spartan state.
Mythological Argonaut, son of Aphareus, proverbial for superhumanly keen eyesight (said to see through wood and earth). Cicero uses 'lynx-eyed' as a byword for sharpness of perception in Fam. 9.2.
(stub --- to be enriched)

aliases: Lyso of Lilybaeum, the son of Lyso
entities.summary: Son of the elder Lyso of Lilybaeum (person:lyso-lilybitanus) and grandson of an unnamed notable. His family was one of the highest standing in western Sicily. Cicero commends him to proconsul Glabrio in 46 BC (Fam. 13.34), citing an inherited ancestral guest-friendship (avitum hospitium).

Prominent citizen of Patrae in Achaea and old guest-friend (hospes) of Cicero. He spent nearly a year at Rome living under Cicero's roof; as a Pompeian sympathiser, his property was at risk after Caesar's victory, but Cicero and other patrons successfully interceded with Caesar on his behalf (Fam. 13.19).

Manius Acilius Glabrio, proconsul (governor) of Sicily in 46–45 BC, the recipient of Cicero's run of recommendation letters Fam. 13.30–39. Distinct from the M'. Acilius Glabrio who presided as praetor over the trial of Verres in 70 BC (the stub person:m-glabrio) — an earlier member of the same gens; the identification of the two is a prosopographical crux left for a later merge decision.

Roman businessman with a household (domus) at Sicyon in Achaea; accompanied Cicero on his Cilician proconsulship (51-50 BC) and remained in the Greek east on commercial business. Cicero recommends him and his household to Servius Sulpicius in Fam. 13.21. Otherwise unknown.

Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, Caesar's magister equitum and consul 46 BC; in 44 BC governor of Gallia Narbonensis and Hither Spain and, from the Ides, pontifex maximus. He soon allied with Antony and Octavian as the third member of the triumvirate.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Owner of a villa in the Pomptine country at which Cicero put up while travelling in April 53 BC (Fam. 7.18.3). Otherwise unknown; the cognomen Philemo suggests freedman or Greek descent.

(stub --- to be enriched)

Senator and one of Cicero's four legates during his proconsulship of Cilicia (51--50 BC); offered the legateship by Cicero unsolicited, when others had been refused. Served alongside Cicero in the Parthian-frontier campaign and was the subject of a recommendation to Q. Minucius Thermus over a dispute with the city of Sardis.

(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman orator (143-87 BC), consul 99, censor 97; with Crassus the supreme orator of Cicero's youth. Grandfather of Mark Antony the triumvir. Speaker in De Oratore; killed by Marius's partisans in 87.
Proquaestor (probably of the province of Asia in 45–44 BC); recipient of Fam. 13.45 and 13.46 and several other recommendation letters of the same period.
Roman consul 101 BC, victor in the Sicilian Slave War; defended in a repetundae trial by Antonius the orator (the famous tunic-tearing scene of De Or. 2.124, 195).

A son of Cicero's intimate friend C. Avianius Flaccus and brother of the younger C. Avianius; an intimate of Cicero. In 46 BC he was with Cicero at Rome, while his brother was in Sicily; Cicero commends both to A. Allienus, proconsul (Fam. 13.79).
A financial agent whom Cicero approves bringing into the negotiation over the half-share of the Tullianum property.

A good and brave man, an old friend of Cicero, commended to P. Sulpicius Rufus, proconsul of Illyricum (Fam. 13.77, 46 BC). He had seen Cicero's runaway slave Dionysius at Narona and was to brief Sulpicius on the matter. Otherwise unknown.

The M. Metellus who gave gladiatorial games at Rome in June 60 BC, which Cicero was glad to leave behind as he set out for Antium. Probably a praetor or aedile of the Caecilii Metelli; identity not securely fixed. FLAG.

Tribune of the plebs 53 BC who had proposed conferring a dictatorship on Pompey; in 51 BC his candidacy at the plebeian aedilician elections collapsed amid laughter when that old proposal was recalled. Later a Caesarian officer.
An acquaintance of Cicero and Paetus who, in Fam. 9.23, met Cicero in the Gallinarian Wood and reported that Paetus was laid up with gout.

Cicero's closest friend at Arpinum and stepfather of Q. Fufidius. One of the three aediles of the municipality in ca. 46 BC, the year Cicero put forward his son Marcus and his nephew Quintus for the same college.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman senator floated by some as a possible consular competitor of Cicero's in 65 BC (Att 1.1.1); he had been one of the jurors who exposed the corruption of the Iunian iudicium of 74 BC. He stood for the aedileship of 64 with Cicero rather than the consulship.
(stub --- to be enriched)

M. Calpurnius Bibulus, Caesar's colleague in the consulship of 59 BC. After being overpowered he withdrew to his house and issued obstructive edicts watching the sky, so that the year was mockingly dated to the consulship of 'Julius and Caesar'.
A man named in connection with the affair of Zeuxis of Blaundus in Asia (Q.fr 1.2); the conversation reported about him prompted Cicero to receive Zeuxis into intimacy. Identity otherwise uncertain.
(stub --- to be enriched)

M. Porcius Cato 'the Censor' (234–149 BC), consul 195, censor 184; the model of old Roman gravitas and prosecutorial severity in extortion cases — most famously his trial of L. Minucius Thermus. Cicero invokes him in the Verrines as the founding type of the Roman provincial prosecution (In Verrem 2.2).

Cicero's only son, born ca. 65 BC. Aedile of Arpinum in ca. 46 BC; later sent to Athens for philosophical studies (the addressee of De Officiis), and consul suffect in 30 BC. The reluctant heir to the orator's name.
Roman consul 166, 155, 152 BC, victor over the Alpine Insubres; perished at sea on a later embassy. Cicero's example of an undeserved death.
Consul 51 BC; uncompromising optimate, leading voice for ending Caesar's Gallic command early. Augural colleague of Cicero. Pardoned by Caesar in 46 BC (Pro Marcello); murdered en route home in 45.

Elder of two brothers from the town of Halaesa in Sicily, bearing a Roman nomen (Clodius) and Greek cognomen (Archagathus). Connected to Cicero both by hospitium (guest-friendship) and familiaritas (intimate friendship). Commended to the proconsul M'. Acilius Glabrio in 13.32.

Banker and financier of Puteoli, an intimate friend of Cicero, with extensive lending interests in the Greek cities of Asia. Pompey was a backer of his Asian business; Cicero pressed his case repeatedly with the governors of Asia and Bithynia in 51--50 BC. Mentioned in his will Cicero among his heirs (Att. 13.46.3).
(stub --- to be enriched)

The man to whom Atticus handed a letter for Cicero at Tres Tabernae (Att. 1.13.1); a courier or travelling acquaintance, otherwise unidentified. Distinct from the Cornelius of the Teucris affair at Att. 1.12.1.

(stub --- to be enriched)
An obscure figure whom Cicero at Att 1.1.2 (65 BC) jokes it might be 'not impossible' to put forward as a consular candidate against the weak field of Thermus and Silanus. Otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A contemporary of Cicero involved in the will dispute with Mundus. Not to be confused with the early poet Quintus Ennius.
A senator listed among those present at the drafting of the senatus consultum on the consular provinces in October 51 BC; later a Pompeian officer in Africa.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Marcus Fadius Gallus, a cultivated Epicurean intimate of Cicero and an art-collector; recipient of Fam. 7.23-7.27, including the famous letter on the statues Damasippus bought on his account. Brother of Q. Fadius (the jointly-held Herculanean estate of Fam. 9.25); also commended in Fam. 13.59 (50 BC).
Roman knight of Arpinum, one of the three Arpinate legates sent in ca. 46 BC to Cisalpine Gaul to oversee the municipality's revenues. Otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman knight, son of a friend of Caelius, who travelled to Cilicia on business in 51 BC; Caelius commends him to Cicero and asks tax-immunity for the leased public lands he handles. Otherwise unknown.

Buyer of the Rabirian house at Naples for 130,000 sesterces (Att. 1.6). Possibly identical with M. Fonteius, the former governor of Narbonese Gaul whom Cicero defended c. 69 BC, but the identification is uncertain.
A Roman knight whom Cicero calls worthless; in 56 BC the colleges of the Capitolini and Mercuriales expelled him as he lay at the feet of every member begging. Otherwise unidentified.
Marcus Gallius, son of Quintus; a Caesarian officer who in 47 BC returned slaves to Sallustius and was charged with conveying legions across into Sicily ahead of Caesar (Att. 11.20).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A legate of Quintus Cicero in Asia (c. 61-59 BC), bound to the Ciceros by family ties (the Gratidii were an Arpinate kin of Cicero's), who labours over his own and the brothers' reputation alike.
Quinctius's former advocate, absent on a fresh embassy when Pro Quinctio was delivered; Cicero replaced him at short notice.

Roman accuser-orator (early first century BC), of base reputation; ridiculed by Crassus on the bench in the Plancius case. Not to be confused with Cicero's friend M. Iunius Brutus the assassin of Caesar.

M. Iunius Brutus, a Roman jurist of the second century BC, author of a dialogue treatise on the civil law (De iure civili); cited in Fam. 7.22 alongside Sex. Aelius and M'. Manilius. Distinct from M. Iunius Brutus the tyrannicide and from the accuser-orator of De Oratore.

Tribune of the plebs 83 BC and a Lepidan commander; he surrendered Mutina and was put to death by Pompey at Regium in 77 BC. Father of the tyrannicide M. Iunius Brutus. Cicero lists him among the men whose killing Caesar threatened to make Pompey answer for. Distinct from his son (person:brutus).

Marcus Iunius Silanus, an officer active in the Mutina campaign of 43 BC: he commanded one of Mark Antony's two praetorian cohorts at Forum Gallorum (Fam. 10.30, 14 April 43), and is reported by Lepidus (Fam. 10.34) as having gone over to Antony, together with Culleo, against Lepidus's wishes.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Purchased estates (the bona Plotiana) from Caesar after the civil-war confiscations, then transferred them at valuation to C. Albinius. Mentioned in Fam. 13.8. Distinct from Decimus Laberius the mime (person:laberius) already in the registry.

A man of the highest integrity and singular modesty, valued by Cicero and his brother Quintus for many services and his loyal counsel; he had business pending in P. Silius's province. Commended by Cicero to P. Silius, propraetor of Bithynia, in Fam. 13.63 (51-50 BC).

Elder son of M. Licinius Crassus the triumvir; quaestor under Caesar in Gaul and later governor of Cisalpine Gaul. One of the "two Crassi" whom Cicero invokes in Fam 5.8 as judges of his goodwill toward their father.

Republican senator of 43 BC and father of Livia (later wife of Augustus); with Paullus he helped arrange the assignment of the Fourth and Martian legions. He fought for the Liberators and died after Philippi. Distinct from the tribune M. Livius Drusus of 91 BC.

A praetor remembered as a jurist for a ruling on manumission oaths, cited by Cicero as legal precedent that a slave wrongly claiming freedom was not thereby freed. Father of M. Livius Drusus the reforming tribune of 91 BC; distinct from that son and from the other Drusi.
Tribune of the plebs 91 BC; carried (and lost) a sweeping reform programme — judicial, agrarian, and citizenship; assassinated late in 91 BC. His death precipitated the Social War.

Roman consul 73 BC; brother of L. Lucullus; triumphed over the Bessi from Macedonia in 71 BC. Supporter of Cicero's recall.
Brother of L. Lucullus; praetor 76 BC, instituted the formula for damnum vi armata datum which is the form of action in Pro Tullio; consul 73 BC.
Roman jurist, consul 149 BC; in command in Africa during the early stages of the Third Punic War. Speaker in De Re Publica.

(stub --- to be enriched)

Son of Cicero's friend and interpreter Marcilius; treated generously by Q. Minucius Thermus on Cicero's recommendation and came to thank him at Laodicea. Subject of Fam. 13.54, in which Cicero further asks that the young man's mother-in-law not be made a defendant.
Cicero's near neighbour and intimate friend at Cumae and Pompeii; recipient of Fam. 7.1--7.4 (the Cumae-circle letters), distinguished as a man of leisured cultivation.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman negotiator (businessman) who had resided and traded at Elis in the western Peloponnese. He is the deceased brother of L. Mescinius, who inherited his Achaean estate; the disputed settlement of that estate is the subject of Fam. 13.26. Otherwise unknown beyond this reference.

Tribune of the plebs 56 BC who, with C. Cato, obstructed the consular elections; prosecuted under the lex Tullia de ambitu and acquitted in July 54 BC (Att. 4.15.4).

Marcus Octavius, son of Cnaeus, who took particular care to tell Cicero in 51 BC that Appius valued him highly; possibly the aedile of 50 BC. Uncertain; distinct from M. Octavius Ligus of the Verrines.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A Roman knight from the township of Atella, a connection of Cicero's and a military tribune in Caesar's army in Gaul. Cicero commends him warmly to Quintus in Q.fr 2.12.

One of the three brothers Gaius, Gnaeus and Marcus Carbo in Fam. 9.21; prosecuted by P. Flaccus and condemned as 'a great thief' for misconduct in Sicily. Otherwise little known.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Pompeian legate who, with Afranius, commanded the seven legions in Spain in 49 BC; later defeated by Caesar at Ilerda.
A landowner from whom Cicero's neighbour C. Albanius had bought a thousand iugera, cited in Cicero's reckoning of land prices (Att. 13.31); perhaps a kinsman of Q. Pilius Celer.
Praetor 64 BC; Cicero's man on the Senate floor through the Egyptian commission of 56 BC; bearer of dispatches between Rome and Lentulus Spinther in Cilicia.
The heir of Marcus Planius, a man of Cales and intimate of Q. Paconius Lepta, in the same predicament as C. Subernius and commended together with him to Dolabella (Fam. 9.13).
Greek freedman of Atticus's, scholar; tutor to the young M. Cicero and Q. Cicero; reading-companion of Cicero's at Cumae in 55 BC.
Marcus Porcius Cato, son of Cato of Utica; he fought at Philippi under Brutus and died in the battle (42 BC), having also served alongside young Marcus Cicero at Athens.

(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman consul 267 and 256 BC; captured by the Carthaginians and (in the legend) tortured to death after returning to Rome on parole and advising the senate against terms.

Addressee of Fam. 13.8; held authority over the distribution or protection of estates in the aftermath of the civil war, probably as a land-commissioner or official under Caesar. Distinct from the Rutilii already in the registry (P. Rutilius Lupus, P. Rutilius Rufus). Otherwise unattested.

Agent and courier of Brutus who appears in Cicero's letters as a messenger between Rome and Brutus's camp; probably the same Scaptius known from the Ad Atticum letters as Brutus's financial representative in Cyprus.
Marcus Seius, an associate of Cicero present at the December 44 meeting at Cicero's house concerning Decimus Brutus; he carried Cicero's opinion back to Brutus.

A Roman tried for extortion (de repetundis) before the praetor M. Iuventius Laterensis in 51 BC, in a notorious case Caelius reports at length; the verdict was left in suspense between acquittal and condemnation. Probably the Servilius later attacked in the senate over peculation.
A neighbour of Quintus near Laterium who had promised to allow water to be led across his estate; Cicero meant to take the matter up with him at Rome in 54 BC.

Quaestor of M. Iunius Brutus, addressed in Fam. 13.10. Came into Cicero's circle early; was active in the publicani partnerships (with heavy losses); carried Cicero's letters to Caesar from Brundisium ca. 47 BC. Named 'M. Varro' in the Latin MS (a copyist error for 'M. Terentius', as the English makes clear). Distinct from M. Terentius Varro the polymath.

M. Terentius Varro of Reate (116-27 BC), antiquarian, polymath, and the most learned Roman of his age; author of the Antiquitates, De Lingua Latina, and De Re Rustica. A prickly figure close to Pompey, whom Cicero courted warily in 59 BC.

The last surviving member of the Titurnian family (familia Titurnia), with whom Cicero had a long-standing tie of connection. Cicero commends him warmly to the proconsul M'. Acilius Glabrio in Sicily, asking that he find sufficient protection through Cicero's recommendation (Fam. 13.39, 46 BC).
Common acquaintance of Naevius and Quinctius, named by Naevius as his arbiter in the partnership dispute.
A Roman whom C. Sempronius Rufus had earlier prosecuted, and whom Sempronius prosecuted again under the lex Plotia de vi in 51 BC to forestall his own trial. Otherwise little known.
Father of the orator and of Quintus, an equestrian of Arpinum of scholarly leanings and poor health who maintained the family at Rome. His death is reported (28 November) in Att. 1.6.
Plaintiff in Pro Tullio (NOT the orator); his slaves and pasture were attacked by armed slaves of P. Fabius.

Cicero's scriba (clerk/secretary), who kept and rendered the accounts of Cicero's Cilician proconsulship (51-50 BC); the figure L. Mescinius suspected of mishandling the provincial accounts in Fam. 5.20. Distinct from Cicero himself and from the freedman Tiro.

Marcus Valerius Messala Corvinus (64 BC–AD 8), Roman aristocrat, orator, and general; fought for Brutus at Philippi (42 BC), then submitted to Octavian and became a distinguished Augustan patron of letters. In 43 BC he served as a trusted emissary carrying Cicero's letters to Brutus.
Young noble who took up Roscius's cause and brought it to court; later consul 61 BC.
Augur and consul 53 BC after long delay; author of works on the auspices and on Roman families; nephew of Hortensius.
Roman knight (eques Romanus) and close personal friend of Lucius Munatius Plancus; dispatched by Plancus as a verbal messenger to Cicero in spring 43 BC (Fam. 10.7). Nothing else is known of him.
A man who had recently seen Tiro and from whom Cicero received one of Tiro's letters in early 49 BC.
Friend of Atticus, attending an auction at Larinum on the Ides of May 55 BC; otherwise unknown.

The man to whom Cicero entrusted a letter conveying Atticus's thanks to Antonius (Att. 1.16.16); a courier or intermediary, otherwise unidentified. Manuscript spelling varies between Mallius and Manlius.
Consul 77 BC and princeps senatus; a senior consular voice present at the crowded December 57 BC Senate debate on the Campanian land. Distinct from M. Aemilius Lepidus the triumvir.

An eques from Formiae, Caesar's praefectus fabrum in Gaul, who grew notoriously rich on the spoils of the Gallic campaigns and was a favourite target of Catullus. Cicero lists his wealth among the scandalous fruits of indulging Caesar. Distinct from the corrupt conjectural name at Fam. 7.5.

Consul of 66 BC, an elder consular who in late January and February 49 BC was among the senators with Cicero on the Campanian coast weighing whether to follow Pompey out of Italy. Distinct from the triumvir M. Aemilius Lepidus and from Mamercus Aemilius Lepidus Livianus.
A correspondent who informed Cicero that he had been excused before the augur Appuleius in 45 BC; Cicero sent letters to him alongside those to Montanus.

Cicero's friend and interpreter (interpres) during his Cilician proconsulship (51-50 BC), of singular and almost incredible loyalty, restraint and modesty, noted through his long term of attendance (apparitio). Father of M. Marcilius, on whose behalf Cicero wrote Fam. 13.54 to Q. Minucius Thermus.
A magistrate (likely praetor or other officer) in Rome in April 43 BC who received one of the letters brought by Celer Pilius; otherwise poorly attested.
Cicero's son (65 BC – c. 25 BC); studied at Athens; a tribune under Brutus and later consul under Augustus 30 BC.
A slave-courier in Cicero's household, sent back and forth between Cicero and the convalescent Tiro to carry letters in the autumn of 50 BC.
King of Numidia (c. 238-148 BC); ally of Rome from the Second Punic War onward. The setting host of the Somnium Scipionis.
One of the two enemies (with Pollio) whom M. Fadius faces in the dispute over the Herculanean estate (Fam. 9.25). Otherwise unidentified.

A person involved in some public affair (the 'Matrinius business') that made Caelius's reputation famous even across the Taurus mountains (Fam. 2.15, 50 BC); details are otherwise lost. Possibly distinct from person:c-matrinius-eques (the Verrines equestrian Matrinius), though the identification cannot be ruled out.

The Colchian sorceress of Greek myth; here the title-role of Ennius's tragedy Medea Exul, which Cicero quotes and 'plays' (Medeam agere) in Fam. 7.6 to tease Trebatius about leaving home for profit abroad. Mythological/literary figure, not a historical person.

A hot-headed young Roman, named at Att. 2.7 with "this bloodthirsty youth" as a bitter enemy of the coalition; later associated with violence in the Catilinarian aftermath and with Crassus's Parthian expedition.
A Greek of Antandrus, called by Cicero a most fickle man, whom he nonetheless cultivated to quiet the complaints against Quintus in Asia.
Slave or freedman of Cicero's, sent ahead to attend Tiro during his illness (Fam. 16.13).

The Spartan king of Greek myth whose wife Helen was carried off by Paris, igniting the Trojan War. Cicero casts him at Att. 1.18.3 as the cuckolded husband, a cipher for M. Lucullus whose wife Memmius seduced.
A courier or agent (probably a freedman or Greek secretary) to whom Cicero would entrust copies of his speeches for delivery to Lentulus Spinther in Cilicia (Fam. 1.9.23). Otherwise unknown.
A member of Atticus's household whose labour, alongside that of Dionysius, helped Tyrannio arrange and label Cicero's library (Att. 4.8.2).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A surveyor or water-engineer working on the channelling of water for Quintus's and Cicero's estates near Arpinum in 54 BC; he had contracted with Quintus at three sesterces a foot for a 3,000-pace water-run.
Consul 60 BC; husband of Clodia (Catullus's Lesbia); died suddenly in 59 BC.

Q. Caecilius Metellus Macedonicus (cos. 143 BC), conqueror of Macedonia, who built the Porticus Metelli at Rome and adorned it with a celebrated group of statues of the Muses (the 'Musae Metelli' of Fam. 7.23). Identification of the Metellus owner of the Muses is the standard scholarly view; flag if uncertain.
Tribune 62 BC (blocked Cicero's farewell consular speech); consul 57 BC (withdrew opposition to Cicero's recall); governor of Hither Spain 56--55 BC.
A courtier of King Ariobarzanes III of Cappadocia who had suffered through the machinations of Athenais; Cicero re-established him in standing and favour with the king in 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).

A skilled book-arranger or librarian who was helping put Cicero's library in order in the late 50s/40s BC; the index of the collection waited on his judgement. Distinct from the earlier rhetorician Metrodorus of Scepsis.
Greek rhetorician (c. 145-70 BC), of Asia Minor; famous for his prodigious memory. Antonius heard him as proconsul on the way to Cilicia.
A man whom Cicero promises to keep in mind, paired with Herodes among the small favours Atticus has asked; otherwise obscure.
Tribune 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; killed Clodius in the affray on the Appian Way in 52 BC; defended (unsuccessfully) by Cicero in Pro Milone.
Athenian general, victor of Marathon (490 BC); died in Athenian prison after a fine he could not pay.

A meat-seller (macellarius) at Rome named in Fam. 15.17 as delighted by the death of P. Sulla, his supposed rival at auctions. An obscure tradesman, otherwise unknown; the identification is uncertain and he is distinct from person:m-mindius the negotiator of Elis.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A banker or financial agent based at Tarentum through whom Cicero arranged payments in 47 BC; Cicero complains that he had 'taken care of only twelve thousand' sesterces (Att. 11.14-11.15). Distinct from L. Minucius Basilus.

Mithridates VI Eupator, king of Pontus c. 120–63 BC; Rome's most formidable Eastern enemy of the late Republic, fought across three wars (89–85, 83–81, 73–63 BC). Cicero invokes him repeatedly in the Verrines (already at 2.2) as the threat that made Sicily's loyalty and good government crucial.

Censor 272 BC and three-times consul, conqueror of the Samnites and Pyrrhus; as censor he cut a channel through the mountain to drain the Lacus Velinus into the Nar, the engineering work still disputed by Reate and Interamna in Cicero's day (Att. 4.15.5).

Roman businessman residing at Patrae (Patras) in Achaea; old friend of Cicero (friendship dating to Curius's first appearance in the Forum); very close intimate of T. Pomponius Atticus, who held him in uniquely high regard. Commended to Servius Sulpicius Rufus in Fam. 13.17; also appears in Ad Atticum in relation to Atticus's Greek business affairs.
A dependent of Cicero's whom Cicero is 'heating up' over the Tusculan garden in the same way he urges Tiro to press Helico; a minor figure of the household economy.

Half-sister of Q. Metellus Celer and Q. Metellus Nepos and third wife of Pompey the Great (married c. 79 BC, divorced 62 BC). In Fam 5.2 Cicero recalls enlisting her, on the strength of her tie to Pompey, to deter Nepos from his attack.

Lucius Munatius Plancus, a Caesarian governing Transalpine Gaul (Gallia Comata) in 43 BC and consul-designate for 42; he is the principal addressee of Cicero's Ad Familiares book 10. After months of temporizing he ultimately went over to Antony and Lepidus.
A man embroiled in a will/testament dispute with Marcus Ennius; Cicero, admitting his curiosity, twice asks Atticus for news of how Mundus's dealings over the will turn out.
Consul-elect for 62 BC; defendant in Pro Murena (late November 63 BC) on charges of canvassing brought under Cicero's own bribery law.
An agent — Mustela Tamisius — through whom Atticus pursued the gardens business for Cicero in 45 BC; Cicero judged him 'a thoroughly suitable man,' though he was also one of Faberius's co-sureties.
(stub --- to be enriched)
An agent in an assassination attempt of 44 BC who, Cicero reports, "has paid the penalty"; Cicero presses Atticus to learn the full story and, in particular, by whom Myrtilus had been suborned.
Pompey's praefectus fabrum, sent from Brundisium as an envoy to Caesar to treat for peace in March 49 BC, even as Pompey himself was being besieged. Caesar records releasing him.
Roman epic and dramatic poet (c. 270-201 BC); author of the Bellum Punicum and several tragedies and comedies.
(stub --- to be enriched)

The aged king of Pylos in the Iliad, proverbial for long-lived wisdom and eloquent counsel to the younger Greek commanders at Troy. Cicero adopts the Nestor role in Fam. 9.14, presenting himself as the seasoned adviser whose counsel even an Agamemnon would honour.
A Greek slave or courier in Atticus's service whom Cicero kept by him on the road to Cilicia in 51 BC; Cicero praises his outstanding service as a letter-carrier and treats him liberally.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Quintus's overseer (vilicus) at the Laterium estate, whom Cicero approved in 54 BC; he had been the contractor for a small outbuilding at 16,000 sesterces but gave it up when Quintus enlarged the work without raising the price.
A learned grammarian and friend of Cicero, then in his company, who debated points of Latinity and Greek usage with him. Distinct from Nicias of Smyrna.
A Greek of Smyrna, dismissed by Cicero as one of the most trifling characters, whom he nonetheless courted to appease the Greeks aggrieved by Quintus.

A Greek physician and medical writer, author of a treatise On Overeating (peri polyphagias), teacher of Sex. Fadius at Velia (Fam. 7.20). Distinct from the pirate Nico of the Verrines (person:nico-pirata).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Second king of Rome, traditionally 715-673 BC; the Sabine king, founder of Roman religion and of the priestly colleges.
A man whom Cicero, on Atticus's warm recommendation, gladly received into friendship in 59 BC (Att. 2.20.1; also 2.22, 2.24). Probably a Puteolan eques; otherwise little known.
A man whose business arrangement or model (the 'forma Numisiana') Cicero alludes to in a private financial matter Quintus had been reconsidering in Sardinia. Otherwise unidentified.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A Greek of Colophon whom Cicero cultivated, with the other minor Greeks he names, to defuse provincial resentment of Quintus in Asia.

Gaius Octavius, adopted in Caesar's will as Gaius Iulius Caesar Octavianus and the future emperor Augustus. In 43 BC, aged nineteen, he fought against Antony at Mutina, then marched on Rome to seize the consulship and joined Antony and Lepidus in the triumvirate.

Dictator of Tusculum and son-in-law of Tarquinius Superbus, whom he backed in the attempt to restore the monarchy at Rome. Cicero pairs him with Porsena as foreign powers Tarquin led against the fatherland. Distinct from the scribe L. Mamilius.

A creditor or agent to be paid in full out of Cicero's accounts in 44 BC; probably the jurist Aulus Ofilius, a pupil of Servius Sulpicius and friend of Caesar. The identification is likely but not certain.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Woman who had been the wife of a Mindius; accused by Cicero of having carried off property forming part of an inheritance in which L. Mescinius had an interest. Cicero urges Servius Sulpicius (proconsul of Achaea) to arrange that she be brought to Rome to face proceedings (13.28).

Banker-brothers of Velia on the Lucanian coast who shared lodgings or business with Atticus; Cicero looks to them as a source of travel funds during the war (Att. 8.7). Distinct from Caesar's agent C. Oppius and the negotiator L. Oppius.
A Roman matron whose daughter the young Cornificius betrothed himself to in 50 BC. Probably to be identified with the Aurelia Orestilla once married to Catiline; the identification is not certain.
King of Parthia (c. 57–37 BC); his armies destroyed Crassus at Carrhae in 53 BC. Father of the prince Pacorus, who led the 51 BC invasion of Syria named in Cicero's Cilician dispatches (Fam. 15.1).
Parthian commander who invaded Syrian Cyrrhestice with Pacorus in 51 BC; pursued by C. Cassius from Antioch, he was wounded in the fighting and died of the wound a few days later.
Owner of a suburban property that Cicero hoped to acquire for Tullia's shrine in 45 BC; his coming onto the market briefly revived the negotiations. Probably a Roscius Otho.
Wife of Gaius Lollius; a debt of 100,000 sesterces owed to her was among the obligations Cicero pressed Atticus to settle in 45 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman officer who had held an independent command (and the title imperator) and was a supporter of Brutus in 43 BC; he encountered Dolabella in Achaia, refused to give him money, and donated 20,000 sesterces of his own to Brutus's cause. He was standing as a candidate for the praetorship of 42 BC.

Consul-designate for 65 BC who was convicted of electoral bribery (ambitus) and barred from office, then condemned in 62 for complicity in the Catilinarian conspiracy; his confiscated house at Rome was bought by the consul Messalla (Att. 1.13.6).
An officer in Antony's army in 43 BC, named by Pollio (Fam. 10.33) as commanding one of Antony's legions after the retreat from Mutina.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Recipient of the recommendation Fam. 13.51; otherwise unattested in the corpus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Debtor of L. Titius Strabo in Cisalpine Gaul, ca. 46 BC; named only in Fam. 13.14, otherwise unknown.

One of the high-standing creditors jointly suing in the Varius/Satyrus affair (Att 1.1.3). Possibly the young P. Cornelius Scipio Nasica later adopted as Q. Caecilius Metellus Pius Scipio Nasica (person:q-metellus-scipio); identity flagged, not merged, on the praenomen.
A tribune of the plebs in 51 BC who joined in vetoing the senate's decree on the consular provinces. Distinct from other P. Cornelii in the corpus.

A tribune of the plebs (or tribune-designate) of 53 BC, devoted to Cicero, who was talked out of acting in the dictatorship affair; Cicero distinguishes him from the triumvir's family by the cognomen Iunianus.

Younger son of the triumvir M. Crassus, husband of Cornelia; served brilliantly under Caesar in Gaul (commander of cavalry against the Aquitani) and accompanied his father to Parthia, where he died in the cavalry charge that broke at Carrhae in June 53 BC. Cicero's favourite of the rising Republican nobility.
Younger son of M. Licinius Crassus; Cicero's literary admirer; cavalry officer with Caesar in Gaul; killed at Carrhae in 53 BC alongside his father.
Twice senior officer of the principal tax-farming partnership in Africa; a friend whom Cicero indulges with serial recommendation letters in Fam. 13.6 and 13.6a.
Defendant in Pro Tullio; admitted (through his counsel L. Quinctius) that his armed slaves had attacked the plaintiff's establishment.

The prosecutor (probably a Valerius Flaccus) who in Fam. 9.21 secured the condemnation of M. Papirius Carbo for extortion/theft connected with Sicily. Identity uncertain; distinct from the propraetor defended in Cicero's Pro Flacco. Flagged for further identification.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman citizen of Consa whom Verres had crucified at Messana facing Italy; his cry "civis Romanus sum" is the architectural climax of In Verrem II.5.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman consul 57 BC; the chief mover of Cicero's recall from exile. The Lentulus of the Fam. 1 correspondence.

Son of P. Cornelius Lentulus Spinther (consul 57 BC). Proquaestor pro praetore of Asia in 44-43 BC; after the murder of Trebonius he held the province for the Liberators and sent the field report and senatorial dispatch preserved as Ad Familiares 12.14 and 12.15. Not to be confused with his father, the consul of 57.

A friend (necessarius) of Cicero and a litigant in 43 BC whose case before arbiters could not be postponed because the parties were bound by a formal compromise and an oath; Cicero advised him to come (to Rome/Africa) in person (Fam. 12.30.5). Distinct from L. Lucceius and Q. Lucceius Rheginus.

A companion (familiaris) of M. Claudius Marcellus (cos. 51) who stabbed him to death with a dagger after dinner near the Piraeus in May 45 BC, then killed himself. The motive (variously reported as a money quarrel or madness) was debated in antiquity. Reported in Servius Sulpicius Rufus's account, Ad Fam. 4.12.

Stepson (privignus) of P. Cornelius Sulla, who in 54 BC joined Sulla as a supporting prosecutor (subscriptor) in the ambitus prosecution of A. Gabinius. Probably distinct from C. Memmius the consular candidate.
Roman knight, otherwise unattested; commended in Fam. 13.51 to P. Caesius.

Senator and polymath, praetor 58 BC; after Varro the most learned Roman of his age, a Pythagorean and authority on astrology, divination, and grammar; a Pompeian in the civil war, condemned and died in exile in 45 BC.
A close partisan and former quaestor of Pompey; in 56 BC his and Libo's open canvassing helped fix the impression that Pompey coveted the Egyptian command. Later prosecuted for ambitus in 52 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

An intimate friend (familiaris) of M. Claudius Marcellus (cos. 51) who, in May 45 BC, came at night to Servius Sulpicius Rufus to report Marcellus's mortal wounding and to ask that doctors be sent (Ad Fam. 4.12). Distinct from the other Postumii in the registry.
The defendant in Cicero's first surviving speech (81 BC); brother and heir of Gaius Quinctius; embroiled in a partnership dispute with Sextus Naevius.

Publius Rupilius, son of Publius, of the Menenian tribe, who served as magister (managing director) of the Bithynian publicani company at the time of Ad Familiares 13.9, c. 51-50 BC. Not to be confused with the consul of 132 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A friend and political associate of Cicero (tribune of the plebs 56 BC) who in 44 BC carried letters between Cicero and Decimus Brutus. Referred to in Fam. 11.5 as "our friend Lupus."

Roman senator, consul 105 BC, Stoic philosopher, historian; condemned for extortion in 92 BC and exiled to Smyrna, where the young Cicero met him. Cicero credits him as the source of the De Re Publica conversation.
Roman consul 105 BC, austere Stoic; condemned in 92 BC for repetundae as legate in Asia, despite his probity. The locus classicus of judicial injustice.
A man to whom Cicero ordered thirty thousand sesterces paid, out of money he had received from Cn. Sallustius (Att. 11.11). Distinct from Cicero's friend Cn. Sallustius.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Creditor of Gaius Quinctius; on his death the debt to his children had to be settled by Publius Quinctius, the Scapula children handled by Aquilius's good offices.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Publius Servilius Casca Longus, tribune of the plebs 44 BC and one of the Liberators who assassinated Julius Caesar on the Ides of March 44 BC, striking the first blow; he subsequently joined Brutus and Cassius in the East, and died after Philippi (42 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Son of the Isaurian conqueror; consul 48 BC with Julius Caesar and again 41 BC; in 43 BC a consular senator opposed to Cicero's Plancus motion, leaning toward the Antonian faction despite his aristocratic lineage.

Propraetor of Bithynia and Pontus in 51--50 BC, contemporary with Cicero's proconsulship of Cilicia. The recipient of Fam. 13.61--65 and other recommendations during the same season. Probably the father of P. Silius cos. suff. 3 BC.

Roman businessman of Nuceria and Numidia whose financial affairs (the 'Sittian bond') Caelius repeatedly presses Cicero to look after; later a freebooting commander who aided Caesar in Africa. Uncertain whether the bond concerns this Sittius personally or an associate.

Son of the adventurer P. Sittius; he remained at Rome during his father's exile and stood trial there in 57/56 BC. Cicero, in Fam. 5.17, says he supported the young man at his trial with work, counsel, labour, influence, and testimony, and praises his devotion to his absent father.
Son of P. Cornelius Sulla; in 54 BC he joined his father as a supporting prosecutor (subscriptor) in the ambitus prosecution of A. Gabinius.

Patrician who in 65 BC canvassed openly and early for the consulship of 63 (Att 1.1.1), where Cicero reports him being refused 'in the old way'; he failed of election. Probably P. Sulpicius Galba, distinct from Servius Sulpicius Galba the tyrannicide (person:servius-sulpicius-galba); identity flagged, not merged with the stub person:p-galba-iudex.
Roman orator and tribune of the plebs 88 BC; an interlocutor in De Oratore as a young man. Killed in the Marian-Sullan strife in 88 BC.
P. Sulpicius Rufus, legate and then imperator/proconsul of Illyricum 48-46 BC, recipient of Fam. 13.77. Distinct from the homonymous orator and tribune of 88 BC (person:p-sulpicius-rufus).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Equestrian agent (pro magistro) of the Roman publican company that held the contract on the scriptura, the pasturage-tax of the Asian provinces. A long-standing intimate of Cicero, recommended to P. Silius in 51--50 BC for help in concluding settlement-agreements (pactiones) with the cities of Bithynia.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Tribune of the plebs in 43 BC who, at Servilius's request, interposed a veto (intercessio) against Cicero's senatorial motion honoring Plancus; Cicero eventually dislodged him. Not to be confused with the later Triumviral-era tribune of the same name.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man who in February 56 BC indicted P. Sestius for public violence (de vi), the same day Cn. Nerius charged him with bribery. Otherwise unidentified.
P. Turullius, quaestor commanding a republican squadron (the fleet L. Tillius Cimber had assembled in Bithynia) in the 43 BC eastern campaign; later one of Caesar's assassins. Named in Fam. 12.13.
An attentive friend (homo officiosus) at Rome who wrote to the exiled Cicero describing how Terentia had been summoned from the shrine of Vesta to the tabula Valeria. Otherwise obscure.
One of the first consuls of the Roman Republic (509 BC and three times after); carried the law on appeal (provocatio) and on lowering the rods to the people.
The prosecutor of M. Aemilius Scaurus for electoral bribery (ambitus) in 54 BC; the manuscript reading 'Tnarius' is standardly emended to 'Triarius'. Identity and praenomen are debated.

Roman who defrauded Atticus's uncle Q. Caecilius of a great sum of money (Att 1.1.3); his brother was Aulus Caninius Satyrus, against whom Caecilius brought a fraudulent-conveyance action. Otherwise unknown.

A flamboyant Roman traveller and friend of Pompey whom Cicero met in Cilicia in 50 BC; Cicero calls him a great rascal (magnus nebulo), travelling with chariots, a litter, a baboon, and wild asses, and whose effects later proved to contain compromising portraits of married ladies.

Publius Ventidius Bassus, a general of low birth who rose through Julius Caesar's campaigns; in 43 BC he commanded Antonian forces and was declared a public enemy by the Senate, but later achieved renown defeating the Parthians (38 BC) and served as suffect consul.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman eques and wit (his cognomen Eutrapelus means 'the witty one'), patron and former owner of the actress Cytheris, and host of the dinner described in Fam. 9.26 at which Cicero reclined near her. A friend of Antony and later correspondent of Cicero.
A gladiator celebrated in Lucilius as the best of all, matched against Aeserninus the Samnite; Cicero casts himself in the role to describe a ruinous duel with Pompey he chose to avoid.
A man who lodged complaints against Quintus Cicero's rulings in Asia; Cicero dismisses him as not even a Greek but a Mysian or rather a Phrygian. Distinct from Q. Paconius Lepta.
Parthian prince, son of King Orodes II; led the Parthian invasion of Syria in 51 BC that prompted Cicero's official dispatches (Fam. 15.1). Killed in battle against Ventidius at Gindarus in 38 BC.
A rhetor (teacher of declamation), described by Cicero as very practised and able, who in 54 BC was instructing his nephew, young Quintus Cicero. Otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Stoic philosopher (c. 185-110 BC), head of the Stoa; close friend of Scipio Aemilianus and Laelius. Major source for Cicero's De Officiis.
A person Tiro is told to call on in connection with Cicero's affairs in November 44 BC. Sex and precise identity are uncertain.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A dependent (probably a freedman) of Cicero whom Cicero wants to bid for the lease of the kitchen-garden at the Tusculan villa, so as to drive up the rent the regular gardener will pay.

The Trojan prince and herdsman of Mount Ida who abducted Helen, wife of Menelaus. Cicero invokes him at Att. 1.18.3 as a cipher for the seducer C. Memmius, who outdid the original by dishonouring both Lucullus brothers.
Patiscus, a proquaestor and ship commander in the republican eastern fleet of 43 BC, bound closely to Lentulus Spinther the younger. Named in Fam. 12.13 and 12.15.

An agent or local official in Cicero's Cilician province who took charge of organising the panther hunt on behalf of the curule aedile Caelius Rufus (Fam. 2.11, 50 BC). His precise status is unknown; he may be a Roman equestrian, a local magnate, or a freedman acting for Cicero. Distinct from the proquaestor Patiscus of 43 BC (person:patiscus).

Patro was scholarch (head) of the Epicurean school at Athens after Phaedrus's death c. 70 BC. He sought permission from C. Memmius to build on or preserve the plot of land in Athens where Epicurus's house had stood; Cicero interceded on his behalf in Fam. 13.1. A close acquaintance of Cicero and Atticus, though Cicero disagreed sharply with his philosophy.
Sister of (C. Valerius) Triarius; in 50 BC she divorced her husband on the day he was due home from his province in order to marry D. Iunius Brutus Albinus.
Citizen of Alabanda in Caria whose case in the province of Bithynia young Tiberius Claudius Nero wished held over until he could arrive in person. Otherwise unattested.
A freedman of Lentulus serving as Cicero's accensus (orderly) in Cilicia, who reported Appius's complaints to Cicero in 50 BC. Distinct from Pausanias of Alabanda.
A man on whose motion (postulatio) the praetor Laterensis acted in the extortion proceedings against M. Servilius in 51 BC. Distinct from Pausanias of Alabanda; otherwise unattested.
Cicero's intermediary in the affairs of Byzantium, whom he asks Atticus to summon to settle the Byzantine business (Att. 14.8). Distinct from the mythical Pelopidae alluded to at 14.12.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Athenian statesman and orator (c. 495-429 BC); leader of Athens in its greatest age; pupil of Anaxagoras.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Phaedrus was an Epicurean philosopher and predecessor of Patro as head of the Epicurean school at Athens. He had been known to Cicero since Cicero's youth (before Cicero heard Philo of Larissa c. 88 BC), and was remembered as a good man as well as a philosopher. His 'entreaty' was cited by Patro as moral authority for his claim to Epicurus's property.
A courier to whom Cicero entrusted an earlier letter to Quintus during his exile (Q.fr 1.4). A slave or freedman; identity otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Tyrant of Acragas (sixth century BC); proverbial for cruelty (the brazen bull).
(stub --- to be enriched)

The host of a memorable dinner recalled jokingly by Cicero and Paetus (Fam. 9.16.8, 46 BC); probably a freedman or financier whose name had become a private byword for a banquet. Possibly the Phamea who was grandfather of the singer Tigellius. flag-not-resolve: identity uncertain.
A trusted freedman of Appius Claudius Pulcher; Cicero conferred with him at Brundisium about which part of Cilicia to enter first when succeeding Appius.
A copyist in Atticus's household; with Antaeus and Salvius, charged by Cicero with striking the name of Corfidius from every copy of the Pro Ligario (Att. 13.44). Not King Pharnaces II of Pontus.

King of the Cimmerian Bosporus, son of Mithridates VI; he invaded Asia Minor during Caesar's absence and was crushed by Caesar at Zela in 47 BC (the 'veni, vidi, vici' campaign). Named in Fam. 15.15 as the unexpected terror that prolonged the civil war.
A courier or agent carrying news between Cicero and Atticus in 67 BC (Att. 1.11.3); the Greek name suggests a freedman or slave. Otherwise unknown.

Slave or freedman of A. Manlius Torquatus who visited Cicero and reported faithfully on his master's troubled state of mind, prompting Fam. 6.1. Otherwise unattested; his Greek name ('fond of silver') is typical of servile nomenclature.
A courier (tabellarius) who carried a letter of Paetus to Cicero, named at the opening of Fam. 9.15 (46 BC). Probably a slave or freedman; otherwise unattested.
(stub --- to be enriched)

King of Macedon 359--336 BC, father of Alexander the Great. Proverbially associated with the saying that no fortress is impregnable to which an ass laden with gold can climb, quoted by Cicero at Att. 1.16.12 of Pompey's electoral bribery. Distinct from the stub person:philip-v.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Sicilian Greek historian (c. 430-356 BC) and partisan of the Syracusan tyrants Dionysius I and II; author of a history of Sicily admired by Cicero as a compact Thucydides. In Q.fr 2.11 Cicero praises his close, sharp, brief style.
A freedman whom Caelius sent out to Cicero in Cilicia in 51 BC, bearing instructions and letters about the panther-hunt and the Sittian business.

Philo of Larissa (c. 159–84 BC) was the last undisputed head of the Platonic Academy. He came to Rome c. 88 BC, where he profoundly influenced the young Cicero. His arrival in Rome marks a turning-point in Cicero's philosophical education, superseding the earlier Epicurean influence of Phaedrus.

Debtor of the Puteolan banker Cluvius, of the city of Alabanda in Caria. Had pledged property as security (hupothekai) on a loan, which had been forfeited by 51--50 BC; Cicero asked the propraetor of Asia, Q. Minucius Thermus, to compel either the surrender of the security or payment.

Mythological hero of the Trojan cycle, abandoned on Lemnos with a festering wound; subject of Sophocles's surviving tragedy and of two lost Roman tragedies, Accius's and Pacuvius's. A figure for the wronged man who watches the spectacles that grieve him.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Greek Epicurean philosopher and erotic poet (c. 110-c. 35 BC); resident in Piso's household. His epigrams survive in the Greek Anthology, his prose works on the Herculaneum papyri.
A freedman and financial agent who handled Cicero's money and the currency-exchange (permutatio) at Ephesus in 51-50 BC and carried his correspondence to and from the province.
Freedman of Terentia; chief manager of Cicero's household business and accounts in the late 50s and through the civil war.

Father of Hippias of Calacte; a notable of the Sicilian city Calacte. Named only as patronymic in Fam. 13.37. Uncertain attribution: the Philoxenus named as Hippias's father (Fam. 13.37) is probably a different person from C. Avianius Philoxenus (Fam. 13.35), who has a Roman name and Cisalpine connections — the coincidence of the name Philoxenus is noted but the two are treated as distinct.

Philoxenus of Cythera (c. 435-380 BC), celebrated dithyrambic poet at the Syracusan court who, in the famous anecdote, chose to be sent back to the tyrant Dionysius I's quarry-prison rather than praise his bad verses. Cicero invokes him in Att. 4.6 as the type of the man who refuses to flatter power.
An agent or slave seen with Mescidius leading water to Quintus's villa near Arpinum in 54 BC. Distinct from the Sicilian guest-friend C. Avianius Philoxenus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Wife of Atticus from c. 56 BC; mother of Pomponia (Caecilia Attica). A frequent dinner-partner in the late-50s correspondence.
A young Clodian partisan, brother-in-law of P. Clodius, who as pontifex assisted in the consecration of the site of Cicero's razed house in 58 BC. Mentioned with hostility at Att. 4.8A.3.
The Theban lyric poet (c. 518-438 BC); Cicero quotes him in Greek ('my mind is divided on speaking the plain truth') when weighing how to handle a difficult man (Att. 13.38).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Tyrant of Athens (c. 600-527 BC); credited with first arranging Homer's books in their canonical order.
Consul 58 BC, father-in-law of Caesar; the other of the "two whirlwinds" of Pro Sestio (with Gabinius); proconsul of Macedonia and target of In Pisonem (55 BC).
A reputedly very learned man, devoted to Greek letters, in the company of the praetor Clodius in Asia in 54 BC (Att. 4.15.2). Otherwise obscure.
Athenian philosopher (c. 428-348 BC), pupil of Socrates, founder of the Academy.
An Epicurean of Sardis, often at Athens, who arrested the runaway Licinus at Ephesus on learning from Aesopus's letters that he was a fugitive (Q.fr 1.2). Not the philosopher Plato.
Roman comic playwright (c. 254-184 BC); twenty surviving plays, the great body of early Latin comedy.

One of the corrupt jurors who acquitted P. Clodius in 61 BC, named with Thalna and Spongia at Att. 1.16.6; otherwise unidentified. Distinct from the comic playwright T. Maccius Plautus (person:plautus).

Brother of L. Munatius Plancus; born Munatius, adopted into the Plotia gens as T. Plotius Plancus. Commanded Plancus's advance cavalry in 43 BC and served as envoy between Plancus and Cicero at Rome; later a celebrated rhetorician.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A woman in Decimus Brutus's circle (probably a freedwoman or relative) who in 43 BC sent a courier giving Cicero the chance to write to Decimus. Named only at Fam. 11.8.
A slave in Cicero's household who served as a letter-carrier and agent during the civil war years. Named in Ad Familiares 14.6 (48 BC), where Cicero presses Terentia to dispatch him.

One of the two enemies (with Mato) of M. Fadius in the lawsuit over the Herculanean estate (Fam. 9.25). FLAG: not securely identifiable with the famous C. Asinius Pollio; treated here as a distinct minor figure.
Greek historian (c. 200-118 BC), brought to Rome as a hostage; member of the Scipionic circle. His Histories analyse the Roman mixed constitution.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Wife of P. Vatinius; in 45 BC Vatinius commended her to Cicero from his Illyrian command, and Cicero promised to attend to her affairs through his friend Sura.

A friend and correspondent of Cicero; Cicero mentions writing to him in a Tusculan letter to Tiro of 44 BC. Probably Q. Pompeius Bithynicus, later governor of Sicily; the identification is plausible but not certain.

A man (apparently a freedman or client of Pompey) at Laodicea with whom P. Vedius lodged his effects; on his death in 50 BC his estate, thought to concern Pompey the Great, was sealed by C. Vennonius.
The general (106–48 BC); consul 70, 55, 52 BC; conqueror of Mithridates; member of the First Triumvirate; defeated at Pharsalus and murdered in Egypt.
Sister of T. Pomponius Atticus and wife of Quintus Cicero; their marriage was notoriously quarrelsome. In 56 BC she complained to Cicero of Quintus.
A dealer from whom C. Cato bought beast-fighters (bestiarii) in 56 BC, alongside Cosconius. Distinct from T. Pomponius Atticus; otherwise unidentified.
A Roman matron, mother or kinswoman of one of the suitors for Tullia's hand in 50 BC; Cicero finds her dilatory and evasive in the marriage negotiations.

Pontius Aquila, tribune of the plebs in 45 BC, notorious for refusing to rise from his seat as Julius Caesar passed during a triumph; he joined the republican cause and was killed fighting at the battle of Mutina in April 43 BC, receiving posthumous honours on Cicero's motion.

Son of Q. Titinius (adopted into the gens Pontia); he served in the Caesarian force besieging Pompey at Brundisium in 49 BC. Cicero, who also calls him 'Titinius's son,' excuses his conduct as done out of fear.

Porcia (died c. 43 BC), daughter of Marcus Porcius Cato Uticensis and wife of Marcus Brutus; she was previously married to Gaius Calpurnius Bibulus. Ancient sources record that she died in 43 BC, the circumstances disputed.
King of Clusium in Etruria who marched on Rome in support of the exiled Tarquins. Cicero names him among the foreign forces Tarquinius Superbus brought against his own city.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Posidonius of Apamea (c. 135-51 BC), the great Stoic philosopher, historian, and polymath who taught at Rhodes, where Cicero heard him. Cicero asked him to write up his consulship more elaborately; Posidonius declined.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Wife of Servius Sulpicius Rufus and mother of the younger Servius; a society lady mentioned in Cicero late-Republican letters (and reputed an intimate of Caesar). In Ad Fam. 4.2 she visits Cicero at Cumae and urges him to write to her husband.

A member of Caesar's inner circle named alongside Pansa, Hirtius, Balbus, Oppius, and Matius in Fam. 6.12.2 (46 BC); identity uncertain, possibly C. or T. Postumius, a Caesarian officer or official of the period. Flagged for further identification.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A person through whom Cicero communicated his goodwill to Trebianus before writing directly; named alongside Sestius, Atticus, and Theudas in Fam. 6.10.1 (46 BC). Identity uncertain; the diminutive form of the nomen Postumius suggests a minor or younger figure.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Son of the elder Precilius; commended by Cicero to Caesar in Fam. 13.15 for his modesty, humanity, and deep affection for Cicero. Described as optimus adulescens. Otherwise unattested.

Elder Precilius, father of the young Precilius commended to Caesar in Fam. 13.15; an intimate of Caesar (tui necessari) and close friend of Cicero. He is described as having chided Cicero for not joining Caesar, yet Cicero calls him amicissimum. He cites and endorses the Euripidean line 'miso sophisten.'
A man Cicero loved ("valde enim illum amavi"), recently deceased, whose estate (the hereditas Preciana) came to Cicero by inheritance in 50 BC. Distinct from the living Precilii of Fam. 13.15.
A Roman (probably the tribune-elect/senator Procilius later tried in 54 BC) who in May 56 BC swore that the Senate's refusal of a thanksgiving to Gabinius was unprecedented.
Reader (lector) attached to M. Marius; mentioned in Fam. 7.1 as a man who could be put to read anything but Cicero's speeches aloud.

Named by Caelius as the mother of the lowborn slave Bellienus, foil to the 'man sprung from Venus' (Caesar). Psecas is a stock servile name (a hairdressing slave-girl), so the figure may be a contemptuous invention rather than a real woman; uncertain.
King of Egypt c. 80–58 and 55–51 BC; expelled in 58, restored by A. Gabinius's Roman force in 55. The Egyptian-restoration debate runs through the Spinther correspondence of 56.
King of Egypt; expelled by Alexandrian uprising 58 BC, restored to the throne by A. Gabinius's army from Syria in spring 55 BC; father of Cleopatra VII.

Cicero's young second wife, married in late 46 BC and estranged soon after Tullia's death in 45 BC, when her conduct displeased him; her letters and her mother's overtures pressed for a reconciliation he resisted.

Brother of Cicero's young wife Publilia; he pressed the marriage and later a reconciliation, and his planned journey to Africa is tracked across the early letters of 45 BC. Not the mime poet Publilius Syrus.

Publilius Syrus, a Syrian-born former slave who became the leading writer and performer of mimes in Caesar's Rome, celebrated for his pithy moral maxims. He competed against Laberius at Caesar's games of 46 BC.

King of Epirus (319-272 BC) and celebrated general who fought Rome 280-275 BC; reputed author of a treatise on the art of war (tactica). Cited jokingly in Fam. 9.25 as one of the military authorities Paetus had supposedly been reading.
Greek philosopher and mathematician (sixth century BC); founder of the Pythagorean school in Croton.
A young man, described by Cicero as fine and learned, who in 54 BC prosecuted C. Memmius on a charge of electoral bribery (ambitus).
Stoic philosopher and jurist, nephew of Scipio Aemilianus through his sister; speaker in De Re Publica.

Senator and jurist, a firm partisan of Brutus; in the Senate debate of April 43 BC he argued that the letter attributed to Brutus lacked Brutus's seal and dateline and was therefore a forgery, defending Brutus's reputation.
Praetor 56 BC, then proconsul of Macedonia 55–54 BC; recipient of Fam. 13.40 (a recommendation for the Aurelii) and Fam. 13.41–42.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A wealthy Roman senator and moneylender, friend of Cicero and Varro (he is an interlocutor in Varro's De Re Rustica). Listed at Att. 1.12.1 among those Cicero might approach for a loan.

Atticus's maternal uncle, a wealthy and notoriously difficult moneylender whose estate Atticus eventually inherited (taking the name Q. Caecilius Pomponianus Atticus). At Att 1.1.3-4 he is defrauded by P. Varius and asks Cicero to appear against A. Caninius Satyrus, which Cicero declines, straining their relations.

A Pompeian officer who in 46 BC raised a revolt in Syria, killed the Caesarian governor Sex. Iulius Caesar, and held out at Apamea; in 44-43 BC he remained a republican strongpoint in the east before his troops were absorbed by Cassius.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Caesarian governor of Further Spain in 48-47 BC, notorious for his rapacity and the mutiny it provoked; the man Pollio names as "Q. Caesar" (a manuscript corruption) as the precedent for Balbus's misconduct. Not to be confused with the tyrannicide C. Cassius Longinus.
Cicero's nephew, son of his brother Quintus and Pomponia. Aedile of Arpinum in ca. 46 BC; later embroiled in the political treacheries of 44–43 BC and proscribed with his father in November 43.
Quintus Cicero's son and Cicero's nephew, born ca. 67 BC; later one of the assassins of Caesar (sometimes confused with M. Cicero the son).
One of the heirs of Q. Turius, a businessman in the province of Africa; commended by Cicero to Cornificius (Fam. 12.26). Distinct from the Verrines-era juror Q. Considius.
(stub --- to be enriched)
An associate or agent of P. Sestius with whom Cicero spoke in 62 BC while working to prevent a successor being appointed to Sestius's proquaestorship.

Poet, orator, and soldier; quaestor 48 BC, an augur (Cicero's colleague in the college), and proconsul of Africa Vetus in 44-43 BC. Cicero's friend and the addressee of Ad Familiares book 12; killed in 42 BC defending his province against the Caesarian T. Sextius.
A tribune of the plebs designate (for 57 BC) whom Cicero in exile hoped would work for his recall (Q.fr 1.4). Distinct from the Curii of the Ad Familiares (the cousin of Caldus and the proconsul).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Caesarian partisan made suffect consul by Caesar in October 45 BC; he died on the morning of 31 December 45 BC, the last day of his term, prompting Caesar to appoint Caninius Rebilus consul for the remaining hours. The 'consul they were calling consul' whose curule chair was set up and then removed when his death was announced (Fam. 7.30). Distinct from Q. Fabius Maximus Cunctator (person:q-fabius-maximus-cunctator).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A legate of Appius Claudius Pulcher in Cilicia who met Cicero at Brundisium in 51 BC to convey Appius's instructions and reported on Appius's goodwill toward Cicero.
Brother of Cicero's friend M. Fadius; in Fam. 9.25 he advertises for sale the Herculanean estate held jointly with his brother, provoking the lawsuit Cicero asks Paetus to settle.

A henchman of Milo who in November 57 BC led keen men out of Milo's Annian house and killed leading members of Clodius's gang, and later heaped abuse on Metellus Nepos in the electoral struggle on the Campus Martius (Att. 4.3.2, 4.3.4). Otherwise unknown.

Roman knight of Arpinum, stepson of M. Caesius. Served as military tribune under Cicero in Cilicia (51–50 BC), and was sent in ca. 46 BC as one of three Arpinate legates to Cisalpine Gaul to oversee the municipality's revenues there. Twice commended by Cicero to Brutus (Fam. 13.11, 13.12).
Consul 47 BC and close ally of Mark Antony; in 43 BC an active opponent of Cicero in the Senate, rebutted by Cicero's Philippics; governed Gaul until his death in 40 BC.
Tribune of the plebs in 62 BC; one of those to whom P. Sestius had written, whom Cicero had to persuade to trust him rather than Sestius's letters in the matter of Sestius's proquaestorship.

Recipient of the recommendation letters Fam. 13.43 and 13.44, a governor (apparently a provincial magistrate) to whom Cicero commends L. Oppius and the affairs of L. Egnatius Rufus, c. 58 BC. Praenomen and nomen are uncertain: the manuscript opener reads "Quintius Gallus" / "GALLO", commonly normalized to Q. Gallius.

A connoisseur or gourmand whose 'sauce' (Haterianum ius) Cicero contrasts jokingly with 'Hirtius's' in the gourmet banter of Fam. 9.18 (46 BC). Often connected with the rhetorical/culinary set around the Paetus letters; precise identity uncertain (possibly an ancestor of the Augustan orator Q. Haterius). Flagged for further identification.

A very close connection of Cicero, on whose behalf Cicero writes Fam. 13.76 to the quattuorviri and decurions of a town, recommending C. Valgius Hippianus (who took his cognomen from Hippius). Otherwise unknown.
Son of the orator Q. Hortensius Hortalus; a dissolute young man in 50 BC, later proconsul of Macedonia and a partisan of the Liberators, executed in 42 BC. Not to be confused with his father.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Pompeian legate who served in Africa and was stranded there when the war turned; prosecuted for treason in 46 BC, he was defended by Cicero in the Pro Ligario and pardoned by Caesar. He later joined the conspiracy against Caesar and was proscribed by the Triumvirs in 43 BC.
