(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman general and orator (149-87 BC), consul 102 with Marius, victor at Vercellae against the Cimbri. Father-in-law of Hortensius. Speaker on the second day of De Oratore.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

entities.short_name: Catulus the elder of Cicero's day
entities.summary: Roman consul 78 BC, censor 65; \textit{princeps senatus} from c. 67. The chief honourer of Cicero on the Catilinarian conspiracy: gave him the title \textit{parens patriae} in the senate.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman knight of Arpinum, one of the three Arpinate legates sent in ca. 46 BC to Cisalpine Gaul to oversee the municipality's revenues. Otherwise unknown.
Roman senator and governor of Bithynia; joined Cassius with his troops in 43 BC alongside Statius Murcus, significantly boosting the Liberators' military position in the East.
Proconsul in the East (Asia) in the mid-40s BC, recipient of Fam. 13.73-74. Distinct from L. Marcius Philippus (cos. 91), the De Oratore interlocutor.

Consul 68 BC, married to a sister of P. Clodius Pulcher and thus Clodius's brother-in-law. At Att. 1.16.10 Cicero puns that 'Rex' (the cognomen, also Latin for 'king') made no mention of Clodius in his will, an inheritance Clodius had hoped for. Distinct from person:rex-recipient (an uncertain 'Rex' addressed in Fam. 13.52).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman consul 109 BC, victor over Jugurtha; refused to swear to Saturninus's agrarian law in 100 BC and went into exile rather than yield, returning the next year. The model of dignified retreat.
(stub --- to be enriched)

entities.canonical_name: Quintus Caecilius Metellus Pius Scipio Nasica
entities.summary: Q. Caecilius Metellus Pius Scipio Nasica (cos. 52), Pompeian commander who died after Thapsus in 46 BC. In Fam. 12.2 the 'villa of Metellus' where Antony rehearsed a speech is identified with his former property.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Curator of the Via Flaminia and a consular candidate whom Cicero at Att 1.1.2 (65 BC) reckons the strongest of the field should he fall back into Cicero's own election year. The roadworks gave him public visibility useful to a canvass.

Praetor by 53 BC, propraetor of Asia in 51--50 BC, contemporary with Cicero's proconsulship of Cilicia. The recipient of a cluster of recommendation letters (Fam. 13.53--57) covering the affairs of Cicero's friends and clients in the province. Pompeian in the civil war; killed at Apamea in 43 BC.

A man named Quintus Mucius who carried one of Cicero's letters (probably Fam. 4.8) to the exiled M. Marcellus at Mytilene in 46 BC (Fam. 4.9.1). His identity is uncertain; he may be the Q. Mucius Scaevola who was Appius Claudius Pulcher's quaestor in Cilicia (person:q-mucius-scaevola-quaestor-51), but the bearer here cannot be securely identified. FLAG: possible duplicate of person:q-mucius-scaevola-quaestor-51 — left distinct pending PM review.

Roman jurist (c. 159-88 BC), consul 117, augur, son-in-law of Laelius and father-in-law of Crassus the orator. Speaker on the first day of De Oratore. To be distinguished from his cousin Q. Mucius Scaevola the Pontifex.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Appius Claudius Pulcher's quaestor in Cilicia, whom Appius had asked to govern the province in the interval before Cicero's arrival in 51 BC; Cicero met him at Ephesus. Uncertain; possibly the same Q. Mucius Scaevola who was tribune in 54 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Cicero's praefectus fabrum during his Cilician governorship and a faithful friend and correspondent. He was with Cicero at Brundisium after Pharsalus and could report on his state of mind to Terentia.
Caesar's great-nephew (grandson of his sister Iulia) and legate; later praetor 48 and suffect consul 43 BC. In March 49 he forwarded Caesar's correspondence from Capua to Cicero.
A kinsman (necessarius) of Cicero's friend Atticus, brother (or close relative) of Atticus's wife Pilia; in 51 BC he prosecuted M. Servilius for extortion. A respected orator of the period.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Tribune of the plebs 52 BC and grandson of the dictator Sulla, condemned and reduced to poverty after Clodius's death; in 51 BC he was running a bathing-business at Baulis. Caelius jokes that he was too poor and starved to have murdered anyone.

Quintus Pompeius, son of Sextus, an old and close friend of Cicero who had repeatedly relied on Cicero's recommendations to support his fortune, standing, and authority. Commended to the proconsul Curius in Fam. 13.49. Distinct from Q. Pompeius the consul of 141 BC (person:q-pompeius-cos141).

A worthy man and intimate of the Ciceros for whose statue a sum had been decreed in Asia, which Attalus of Hypaepa feared Quintus might block (Q.fr 1.2). The name is a corrupt and uncertain reading in the manuscripts.
The most celebrated Roman comic actor of his day (d. c. 62 BC); a friend of Cicero, who acknowledged learning oratorical delivery from him; defendant in Pro Roscio Comoedo.
(stub --- to be enriched)

An agent and friend of Lentulus Spinther at Rome whom Cicero employs in everything connected with the Egyptian command (Fam 1.5a), judging him the most prudent, loyal, and devoted of Lentulus's men. Probably the moneylender Q. Selicius mentioned elsewhere in the correspondence.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A friend or agent of Appius Claudius Pulcher who carried letters between Appius and Cicero in 50 BC (Fam. 3.10, 3.11, 3.12); in 3.12 he is sailing with Cicero off Side. Distinct from the several other Servilii in the corpus.
A friend of Milo against whom Clodius's hired gangs raised an uproar at the Graecostasis during the December 57 BC court debate. Otherwise unidentified; distinct from Sextilius Rufus of the 40s BC.
Owner of an estate in the territory of Pompeii or Nola which Cicero, hunting for suburban property in 45 BC, asks Atticus to find out whether it is for sale (Att. 13.8).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Most likely Q. Titurius Sabinus, a legate of Caesar in Gaul; named in Quintus Cicero's letter from Transalpine Gaul (53 BC), whose slave-boys Quintus promises to reward. Identification with this Sabinus is uncertain.

A good and honourable Roman who carried on business in the province of Africa and died there, leaving a group of fellow-businessmen as his heirs. The subject of Cicero's recommendation to Cornificius in Fam. 12.26.
Praetor 57 BC; propraetor of Africa Vetus 56 BC; later one of Caesar's commissioners settling veterans in Italy.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Cicero's younger brother (102–43 BC), praetor 62 BC, governor of Asia 61–58 BC, legate of Caesar in Gaul.
Aged senator defended by Cicero in Pro Rabirio Perduellionis Reo (63 BC), revived charge of perduellio for the killing of the tribune L. Saturninus 37 years earlier in 100 BC.

A man with the cognomen Rex, recipient of Fam. 13.52 (opener CICERO REGI S.), recommended A. Licinius Aristoteles of Malta. Identification uncertain; likely Q. Marcius Rex, but the cognomen alone is the only firm datum.
Rhinthon of Tarentum (3rd c. BC), comic poet and founder of the burlesque "phlyax" tragicomedy (hilarotragedy); Cicero quotes a verse of his on the worthlessness of public men at Att. 1.20.3.

A person connected to Q. Minucius Thermus or his household in Asia, on whose behalf Cicero had rendered some unspecified service; the name is Greek or of Greek origin, suggesting a freedman or provincial associate of Thermus.
Legendary founder and first king of Rome; deified after his death as Quirinus.
The greatest comic actor of late-Republican Rome (c. 126-62 BC), friend of Cicero, defended by him in 76 BC (Pro Roscio Comoedo); the Roman touchstone of perfect technique.
A Roman woman named in Fam. 9.21 as the mother of a Carbo who was a friend of Cicero ('Rubria's son'); Cicero pointedly declines to say anything about him. Otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man (probably a freedman or agent) of Trebatius's, much liked and missed at Velia, whom Trebatius had transferred to oversee his building project (Fam. 7.20). Otherwise unidentified.

An otherwise unidentified friend of L. Papirius Paetus who, according to Fam. 9.24, warned Cicero of plots against him at Aquinum and Fabrateria in 43 BC. His full name and identity are not established by the ancient sources.
A man named Rufus travelling with Cicero and his brother Quintus in early 49 BC who joins in sending greetings to Terentia and Tullia in Fam. 14.14. His full identity is uncertain.
Tribune 63 BC; proposer of the agrarian bill against which Cicero spoke the three De Lege Agraria speeches.

An agent (possibly a freedman) of C. Scribonius Curio who, in Curio's absence, was eager to declare public games in his patron's name. Known only from Cicero's letter Fam. 2.3, where Cicero dissuades the project.
Mother of the orator Gaius Aurelius Cotta; gathering exempla for his Consolatio in 45 BC, Cicero asks Atticus whether she outlived her son — a test case for a parent's endurance of a child's death.
An emissary of Appius Claudius Pulcher the younger who, in 51 BC, turned and prosecuted his own principal for violence (de vi). Identity uncertain; distinct from the P. Tettius of the Verrines.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Quintus Salvidienus Rufus, one of Octavian's earliest and most capable generals, of obscure origins; he commanded forces for Octavian in 43–40 BC and was executed in 40 BC after allegedly opening negotiations with Mark Antony.
A friend or freedman through whom Cicero sent his letter (Fam. 9.10) to Dolabella; otherwise unidentified.
A man Cicero brands as wicked and personally insolent toward him, in a context of grievance; his fuller identity is obscure.
The legendary last king of Assyria, a byword in Greek and Roman moralizing for luxury and for an inglorious death in his own bed.

A partisan of the Caesarian party; Cicero names him sarcastically among Antony's disreputable estate-managers ("Saserna is a fitting colleague"), grouping him with Matius and Postumus. The Sasernae were a family active in Caesar's circle.

A Roman officer serving as legate (legatus) under Gaius Trebonius in Asia; after Trebonius's murder he continued to relay military intelligence westward, and in May 43 BC received dispatches that he forwarded to Brutus.
Tribune 103 and 100 BC; killed by senatorial action under the senatus consultum ultimum in 100 BC, the precedent revived against Rabirius in 63 BC.
A man Cicero invokes as the type of the relentless creditor -- 'speak to Polla as though you were speaking to that Scaeva' -- in pressing the recovery of a debt (Att. 13.23); identity uncertain.
One of four men (with Censorinus, Antonius, and Cassius) by whom Quintus Cicero reported he was deeply loved in Asia (Q.fr 1.2). Possibly Q. Mucius Scaevola, a tribune of 54 BC; identity uncertain.
Roman general and statesman (185-129 BC), consul 147 and 134; conqueror of Carthage and Numantia. The central figure of De Re Publica.
Roman general (236-183 BC), conqueror of Hannibal at Zama. Adoptive grandfather of Scipio Aemilianus; the speaker of the prophecy in the Somnium Scipionis.
Thessalian noble of Crannon, patron of Simonides; killed when his hall collapsed during a banquet — the catastrophe in which Simonides discovered the loci method.

A friend of the consular Q. Lutatius Catulus and, with Gaius Arrius, one of the two intrusive Formiae neighbours whose company Cicero laments in 59 BC. A reputed traveller, later cited by Pliny as a geographical source.

A disreputable busybody and rumour-monger of 43 BC who carried gossip between Octavian's camp and Decimus Brutus, including the report of Cicero's quip that the young Caesar should be "praised, decorated, and lifted up." Decimus Brutus calls him "a man entirely true to himself."
(stub --- to be enriched)

A man (probably one of two eloquent Selii known from the period) cited jokingly by Cicero at Fam. 7.32.2 as an example of a defendant glib enough to argue himself free. Precise identification uncertain.
A man over whom Cornificius had to excuse himself to Cicero, the affair dating to the 'blind season of servitude' under Caesar's autocracy; his fuller identity is uncertain.
A Greek mathematical geographer whose technical treatise Atticus sent to Cicero in 59 BC and which Cicero professed scarcely to understand. Cited at Att. 2.6 as a critic of Eratosthenes.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man whose business Cicero leaves to Tiro's judgement in a letter of 44 BC, described as one who 'does not despise old age.' Precise identification is uncertain.
A praetor of 54 BC who, with M. Cato, threatened to forbid Pomptinus's triumph. Identity uncertain; possibly C. Servilius.
Cicero's repeated client in a case before P. Silius, propraetor of Bithynia, in 51--50 BC; the case had also been taken up by Tiberius Claudius Nero. Otherwise unattested.
An otherwise unidentified tribune of the plebs in 43 BC who received Antony's letter from Celer Pilius and read it out in the Senate; distinct from the consular senator Servilius in Ad Brutum 2.2.

A learned kinsman ('brother') of L. Papirius Paetus, a textual critic of early Latin comedy able to distinguish genuine from spurious verses of Plautus; called by Cicero 'the most lettered of men' (Fam. 9.16.4). Generally identified with the grammarian and Plautine scholar Servius Clodius.
A Roman caught twice in three days in adultery, the subject of Caelius's gossip to Cicero in 50 BC. Otherwise unknown.
A prosecutor whom Appius Claudius Pulcher repeatedly tried to set on Caelius, finally indicting him under the Lex Scantinia (Fam. 8.12). Otherwise obscure.

One of Caesar's assassins and an ancestor of the emperor Galba; in 43 BC he served with the consular armies before Mutina. His eyewitness dispatch (Fam. 10.30) is the fullest surviving account of the battle of Forum Gallorum.

Servius Sulpicius Rufus (c. 106–43 BC), consul 51 BC and Rome's greatest Republican-era jurist; a close friend of Cicero who died in January 43 BC on a senatorial embassy to Antony, receiving posthumous honours. He moved in the Senate that age requirements for the cursus honorum be relaxed for Octavian.

Son of the jurist Ser. Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51) and a cousin of Decimus Brutus; one of the men Lupus brought together at Cicero's house in December 44. Identification with Brutus's cousin "Servius" is probable but not certain.
Sixth king of Rome; instituted the centuriate organization of the Roman people by census class.
Tribune 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; defendant in Pro Sestio (March 56 BC) on charges of public violence; acquitted unanimously.
A political enemy of Cicero who was condemned in the courts in 56 BC, to Cicero's open satisfaction. Otherwise unidentified.

Sextus Aelius Paetus Catus (cos. 198 BC), a foundational early Roman jurist, author of the Tripertita (the first systematic treatise on the civil law); cited in Fam. 7.22 as one of the authorities holding the disputed view on actio furti.
Quinctius's procurator at Rome and a relation of Sextus Naevius; tore down Naevius's notice of attachment and retook a slave Naevius had seized.
A distinguished Roman knight with affairs in the province of Africa, one of the heirs of Q. Turius; an attentive client of Cicero, separately commended to Cornificius (Fam. 12.26 and 12.27).
Henchman of P. Clodius (the tribune of 58); presided over the Compitalia of 1 January 58 BC against the senate's authority. Likely the author of much of the Clodian legislation.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A physician at Velia, pupil of the doctor Nico, from whom Cicero carried off a copy of Nico's book On Overeating (Fam. 7.20). Distinct from the Fadius brothers M. Fadius (person:m-fadius) and Q. Fadius (person:q-fadius).
Sex. Marius, a legate of Dolabella who in 43 BC suddenly abandoned the cargo-fleet in Lycia and fled in a warship (Fam. 12.15).
The antagonist in Pro Quinctio; a sometime auctioneer turned partner of Gaius Quinctius in Gaul; the speech accuses him of attempting to seize P. Quinctius's goods by procedural fraud.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Son of the elder Sex. Peducaeus, propraetor of Sicily; a friend of Cicero and Atticus and a man of high principle, whose approval of Cicero's conduct in the civil war Cicero valued as he had once valued the father's counsel.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Defendant in Pro Roscio Amerino; son of the murdered Sex. Roscius the elder; charged with parricide.
Murdered father in Pro Roscio Amerino; first man of Ameria, friend of the Metelli, the Servilii, the Scipios; killed near the Pallacinian baths in 81 BC.
A man of business on whose behalf Pompey sought a prefecture from Cicero in Cilicia (50 BC); Cicero refused, as he refused such requests to others, and Pompey accepted the refusal.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A close friend (familiaris) of T. Annius Milo, used by Cicero as the courier of Fam. 2.6 to Curio in 53 BC. Otherwise unknown.
Sextilius Rufus, commander of the large fleet of C. Cassius Longinus in Syria, 43 BC (Fam. 12.13).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Younger son of Pompey the Great; survivor of Munda who in 44 BC commanded forces in Spain and the western seas, becoming a rallying point for the Republicans against the Caesarians. Later controlled Sicily and the grain supply until his defeat by Octavian's fleet.

A proconsul of Further Spain in an earlier era of civil strife (probably the Sullan period) under whom, Pollio recalls, the Senate of a Spanish community was massacred or driven out by seditious factions. Known only from Pollio's letter (Fam. 10.32).

A close friend and confidential agent of Cicero who sheltered him at Vibo in southern Italy during the exile of 58 BC. He serves again as a trusted intermediary in the post-Pharsalus letters from Brundisium.
The slave of Felix the testator, who shared in the blunder of sealing the wrong will-tablets in 54 BC, costing Quintus Cicero his bequest. Otherwise unknown.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Consul-elect for 62 BC at the time of the Catilinarian debate; opened the senatorial discussion on 5 December 63 BC voting for the death penalty for the conspirators.

A man named Silius whose 'message' had alarmed Paetus and prompted his letter to Cicero (Fam. 9.16.1, 46 BC); the news apparently concerned some danger or property matter. Distinct from P. Silius Nerva the Bithynian propraetor. flag-not-resolve: identity uncertain.
Greek lyric poet (c. 556-468 BC), credited as the inventor of the art of memory. The Scopas anecdote (Cic. De Or. 2.351-353) is the founding story of the loci-and-images mnemonic system.

An Epicurean philosopher resident at Naples (Neapolis), teacher of Virgil and the poet Varius Rufus; described by Cicero as 'our friend' in Fam. 6.11.2 (45 BC), suggesting a warm personal acquaintance across philosophical boundaries.
Athenian philosopher (c. 470-399 BC), the founder of the philosophical-ethical tradition; condemned to death at Athens.
Athenian lawgiver and one of the Seven Sages of Greece (c. 638-558 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
The Athenian tragedian (c. 496-406 BC); Cicero alludes to him jokingly ('something in the Sophoclean style') when teasing Tiro about possible literary compositions.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A young slave of Cicero, his reader (anagnostes), whose death in early 61 BC grieved him more than a slave's death seemed to warrant (Att. 1.12.4). Distinct from Sositheus of Entella in the Verrines.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Roman critic and arbiter of plays selected for the public stage; the man whose taste oversaw the dramatic offerings at Pompey's games of 55 BC.
Roman senator, brother of Lucius Mummius the destroyer of Corinth; member of the Scipionic circle and a speaker in De Re Publica.
An amanuensis to whom Cicero dictated 'syllable by syllable,' contrasted with Tiro, who could take down whole passages at a stretch (Att. 13.25).

One of the corrupt jurors who acquitted P. Clodius in 61 BC, named with Thalna and Plautus at Att. 1.16.6 as worthless 'rubbish'; the name (literally 'sponge') is otherwise unknown and probably a derisive nickname.
Freedman of Quintus Cicero, much trusted and (in Cicero's opinion) too prominent in the household. Recurring offstage figure in the Q.fr. correspondence.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Citizen of Thermae in Sicily; his trial in absentia by Verres on a fabricated capital charge is the central narrative of In Verrem II.2.
The dictator (138–78 BC); marched on Rome twice; victor of the civil war of 83–82 BC; abdicated 79 BC. The proscriptions of his regime are the political background of Pro Roscio Amerino.
Nephew of the dictator; convicted of electoral bribery in 66 BC, defended by Cicero in Pro Sulla (62 BC) on a charge of complicity in the Catilinarian conspiracy.
The nomenclator of Quintus Cicero in Asia, said to have written an objectionable letter in his master's name (Q.fr 1.2). A slave or freedman; not the dictator L. Cornelius Sulla.

A friend or agent of Cicero at Rome in 45 BC ("Sura noster"), through whom Cicero arranged to convey his services to Vatinius's wife Pompeia. Identity uncertain; not the Catilinarian Lentulus Sura (executed 63 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A devoted companion of Cicero who shared all the hardships, journeys, voyages, and dangers of Cicero's exile (58-57 BC) and stayed at his side until given leave to depart. Recommended as one of Cicero's household to the proconsul P. Servilius in Fam. 13.71 (46 BC); otherwise unknown.
Roman praetor c. 105 BC, governor of Sardinia; an Epicurean. Triumphed in Sardinia, then was condemned at Rome — Cicero's parallel for Piso.

A senator and close friend of Cicero; tribune 63 BC, praetor c. 59, governor of Asia and then Cilicia, and a committed Pompeian in the civil war who was later pardoned by Caesar through Cicero's intercession. In Fam. 13.70 Cicero, citing his intimate friendship with Balbus, recommends Balbus's freedman T. Ampius Menander to the proconsul P. Servilius.
Freedman of Cicero's friend T. Ampius Balbus, described as an honest and modest man esteemed by both his patron and Cicero. Recommended to the proconsul P. Servilius in Fam. 13.70 (46 BC).

Relative of C. Ateius Capito. Held Macedonia by lot as quaestor and was trapped there when Pompey arrived in 49--48 BC. Participated minimally in Pompeian coinage operations at Apollonia before withdrawing to inner Macedonia. After Pharsalus fled to A. Plautius in Bithynia, was ordered to Rome by Caesar, fell ill, and died at Corcyra. Had made a will at Rome in the consulship of Paulus and Marcellus (50 BC) leaving Capito the heir (13.29).
Tribune of the plebs 57 BC, who worked for Cicero's recall from exile, and Cicero's quaestor in 63 BC; later condemned and exiled, the addressee of the consolatory letter Fam. 5.18.

Proconsul of Sicily in 46-45 BC and a personal friend of Cicero; he and his legates were stationed at Mutina before taking up the Sicilian command. Cicero commends the exiled Caecina to him in Fam. 6.8 and is the addressee of Fam. 6.9.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man from whom, in the settlement of M. Cluvius's estate, a legacy was to be paid; named by Balbus among the dispositions of the Cluvius inheritance (Att. 13.46).
Caesar's most able legate during the Gallic Wars who defected to Pompey and the Senate when the civil war broke out in 49 BC. He later fought against Caesar at Pharsalus and died at Munda in 45 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman negotiator (businessman) resident at Thespiae in Boeotia, a long-standing admirer of Cicero who shared his literary interests. Cicero recommends him to Servius Sulpicius in Fam. 13.22, jointly with a letter from A. Terentius Varro Murena. Distinct from the earlier senatorial T. Manlius entries in the registry.
A man relieved, along with Valerius's sureties, of a heavy financial burden by Cicero's contrivance in the settling of the Cilician accounts (Fam. 5.20, 49 BC). Otherwise unidentified.

Younger brother of Lucius Munatius Plancus; tribune of the plebs in 52 BC and a supporter of Clodius, later a Caesarian. In Fam. 10.6 Cicero mentions him alongside Furnius as a positive influence available to counsel his brother.
A close friend of Cicero, commended to Cornificius in 43 BC; he managed the accounts and business affairs of Cicero's friend Dionysius.

Son of T. Pinnius the elder; a young man of cultivated parts (mire studioso et erudito et modesto) under Cicero's guardianship in 51--50 BC. The Bithynian city of Nicaea owed his estate eight million sesterces; Cicero asked P. Silius to expedite payment.
Roman intimate of Cicero, deceased by 51--50 BC; named Cicero in his will as guardian (tutor) of his son and as second heir, a mark of close trust. Otherwise unattested.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Cousin of the murdered Sex. Roscius; named by Cicero as one of the murderers and conspirators with Chrysogonus.
Cousin of the murdered Sex. Roscius; named by Cicero as one of the actual murderers and conspirators with Chrysogonus.
A partisan paired with Calvisius Sabinus as the two halves of Cicero's jest 'the Minotaur' (Minos-Taurus) in 43 BC; both were opponents in the senatorial debate over Cornificius.

Titus Titius, son of Titus, a legate (legatus) addressed by Cicero in Fam. 13.75 (c. 52-51 BC), apparently holding a charge over the grain supply. Cicero recommends to him the wine-merchant C. Avianius Flaccus in matters of grain delivery and timing. Distinct from C. Titius (person:c-titius) and the city praetor C. Titius Rufus (person:c-titius-rufus-pr).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A man whose courier (tabellarius) carried one of Decimus Brutus's fuller letters to Cicero in May 43 BC. Named only at Fam. 11.12.
A man whose affairs (the "Tadianum negotium") Atticus had settled to his satisfaction, reported by Cicero at Att. 1.8.1. Praenomen unspecified; distinct from the Verrine P. and Q. Tadius.

A Cilician dynast in the Amanus region, reckoned the most loyal Roman ally beyond the Taurus; he warned Cicero in 51 BC that Pacorus had crossed the Euphrates (Fam. 15.1). Later (founder of a local dynasty) styled king under the title Philantonios.
Fifth king of Rome, of Etruscan and Greek descent; son of Demaratus of Corinth.
Seventh and last king of Rome, traditionally 535-509 BC; expelled after his son Sextus's rape of Lucretia, leading to the founding of the Republic.
The Roman comic playwright (c. 195/185–159 BC); Cicero quotes a line of his ('this day brings a different life, demands different ways').
Cicero's first wife (c. 98–c. 6 BC), of independent property; mother of Tullia and Marcus the younger; divorced 46 BC.
An officer of Lepidus posted to guard the Alpine passes who, with Silanus, defected to Antony in 43 BC before returning; reported in Lepidus's letter (Fam. 10.34).

entities.canonical_name: Tertia, daughter of Isidorus the mime
entities.short_name: Tertia, daughter of Isidorus the mime
entities.summary: (stub --- to be enriched)

A Roman whose vestibule on the Sacra Via gave Cicero refuge when Clodius and his gang set upon him in November 57 BC (Att. 4.3.2). Otherwise unknown; possibly a neutral or sympathetic householder rather than a partisan.

One of the corrupt jurors who acquitted P. Clodius in 61 BC, named contemptuously by Cicero with Plautus and Spongia as 'rubbish of this kind' (Att. 1.16.6); otherwise unidentified, perhaps a member of the Iuventii Thalnae.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Athenian general, architect of the victory at Salamis (480 BC); ostracized and exiled to Persia, where he died.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek historian of Mytilene and the intimate adviser and propagandist of Pompey, often serving as his confidential go-between. At Att. 2.5 he is the channel through whom the coalition's offer of an Egyptian commission might reach Cicero.

A freedman (libertus) of M. Claudius Marcellus (cos. 51) who served as a courier between his patron in exile at Mytilene and Cicero at Rome in 46 BC; he carries Cicero's letters Fam. 4.9 and 4.10, and Cicero vouches for his loyalty and goodwill toward Marcellus.
Greek philosopher (c. 371-287 BC), Aristotle's successor as head of the Peripatetic school; author of the Characters and a lost treatise On Style much used by Cicero.
Greek historian (c. 380-c. 320 BC), pupil of Isocrates; author of the lost Hellenica and Philippica.

A learned Greek of Cnidus, friend and favourite of Julius Caesar, who used his influence to obtain benefits for his city and for clients. In Q.fr 2.10 Cicero notes he will satisfy Theopompus over the Commagene business. Distinct from the historian Theopompus of Chios (person:theopompus).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A freedman (libertus) of Trebianus who acted as a channel of communication between his patron and Cicero during Trebianus's exile; named in Fam. 6.10.1 as the most recent person to whom Cicero had opened his heart about Trebianus's situation.

A freedman of C. Scribonius Curio (tribune 50 BC), to whom Cicero entrusted a letter about Curio's priesthood when writing from camp at Pindenissum in late 51 BC (Fam. 2.7.3). Distinct from Thraso of Tyndaris (person:thraso-tyndaritanus).
(stub --- to be enriched)

The Athenian democratic general who went into exile and returned in arms to overthrow the Thirty Tyrants in 403 BC, restoring the democracy. Cicero offers him as the precedent of armed resistance, set against the quietists who survived the Cinnan terror by accommodation.
Athenian historian (c. 460-400 BC), author of the History of the Peloponnesian War; the model of dense and weighty prose for Cicero.

A Greek poet in the circle of Cicero and Atticus, evidently an epigrammatist with antiquarian interests; at Att. 1.9.2 he asks (through Cicero) for material on the rites of the Eumolpidae, and he recurs at Att. 1.12.
Ti. Cannutius, tribune of the plebs in 44 BC and a bitter opponent of Antony. In Fam. 12.3 he hauls Antony before a contio and is reported by Antony to be acting on Cicero's counsel.

Young Roman noble (b. c. 85 BC), of the Claudian gens; quaestor under Cicero in Cilicia (51--50 BC). Suitor for Tullia in 50 BC, but lost out to Dolabella. Father, by Livia Drusilla, of the future emperor Tiberius. Pompeian, then Caesarian; praetor 42 BC.

Consul 177 and 163 BC, censor 169, and augur; father of the reforming tribunes Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus. While governing Sardinia he realised, as augur, that he had presided over the consular elections at Rome under a flawed auspice, prompting the consuls to abdicate.
Tribune of the plebs 133 BC; carrier of the agrarian law and the Pergamene-inheritance bill; killed by senatorial opponents in 133. The breaking-point of De Re Publica's framing time.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Sardinian singer and musician of Caesar's circle, grandson of Phamea; in Fam. 7.24 (45 BC) he is the 'pestilential' fellow estranged from Cicero, the 'pretty piper' grandson of Phamea. Famously satirized by Horace in Satires 1.2 and 1.3.
King of Armenia, defeated by Pompey in the 60s BC and reduced to a Roman client. Clodius's eastern legation of 59 BC was nominally a mission to greet him, which Cicero repeatedly mocks.

The captive Armenian prince, son of Tigranes II, whom Clodius freed from custody for a bribe in 58 BC, provoking Pompey's anger. Cicero cites the episode at Att. 3.8 as evidence that no real political rupture is in the offing. Not to be confused with his father, Tigranes II 'the Great' (person:tigranes-armenia).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Cicero's slave, manumitted ca. 53 BC, secretary, literary collaborator and lifelong intimate; recipient of all letters in Ad Familiares book 16; later editor of Cicero's correspondence and posthumous biographer.

A friend whom Cicero consoles on the death of his children in Fam. 5.16 (c. 52 BC); the praenomen is not given in the letter's salutation and the addressee cannot be securely identified with any other Titius in the corpus.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A Pompeian who had served with Cicero as colleague in the aedileship; consoled by Cicero in exile after the civil war (Fam. 6.20-21, 45 BC). Later proscribed by the triumvirs in 43 BC, reportedly betrayed by his own son to Octavian.

Early Roman comic poet (writer of fabulae palliatae, c. 2nd century BC), quoted several times by Cicero (Tusc. 4.35; De Fin. 2.13). In Fam. 9.21 Cicero alludes to a 'tag from Trabea' that Paetus had used.

An agent connected with Q. Cornificius's command in Africa who reported to Cicero on its state (Fam. 12.23); Cicero praises his fidelity, diligence, and prudence. In Fam. 12.30 his silence about the Attius Dionysius affair is taken by Cicero as proof there is nothing in it.

A Pompeian supporter who remained in exile or awaiting pardon after the civil war; Cicero consoles him in Fam. 6.10 (46 BC) and congratulates him on his return in Fam. 6.11 (45 BC), the latter secured through the intercession of Dolabella. His praenomen and full nomen are unknown; he may be the Trebianus attested in Umbria.
Cicero's beloved daughter (c. 79–45 BC); married three times (Piso, Crassipes, Dolabella); died after childbirth in February 45 BC, an event that crushed Cicero.
Third king of Rome, traditionally 673-642 BC; warrior-king who instituted the fetial law of just war.

A woman whose estate ('bona Turpiliae') was the subject of a contested will in Fam. 7.21 (44 BC); Servius Sulpicius held her testament invalid for want of testamentary capacity (factio testamenti). Known only from this letter.
A frantic and sordid man (Cicero's words) from whose grasping jaws Quintus snatched a dishonourable greed with the highest equity; one of the detractors complaining of Quintus's severity in Asia.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Greek grammarian and scholar (Tyrannion the Elder) brought to Rome as a war captive; he taught in Cicero's household and helped arrange Cicero's library. In 56 BC he was tutoring the young Quintus.

The Homeric hero Odysseus (Latin Ulysses), proverbial for cunning and for a long delayed homecoming. In Fam. 1.10 Cicero jokes that Valerius, like Ulysses returning to Ithaca, will recognize none of his own if he lingers in Apulia.
A man to whom Cicero wrote about paying Tiro's doctor's fee in 53 BC; otherwise unknown.
A Roman jurist of Cicero's day, named at Fam. 7.8.2 alongside Manilius as one of the legal circle to whom Cicero will 'complain' about Trebatius's career-choices. Otherwise little known.

An otherwise obscure Valerius acquitted at Rome (60-59 BC) with Hortensius as his defence counsel; Cicero hints the verdict was arranged. Identity uncertain. FLAG: not securely identifiable with any standing Valerius in the registry.

A contractor (manceps) under whose name money had been provided to Cicero in Cilicia, with Valerian sureties (praedes Valeriani) bound for it; the obligation could not be transferred to Volusius (Fam. 5.20, 49 BC). Otherwise unknown.
An interpreter or dragoman who at Att. 1.12.2 reports to Cicero the rumour about his freedman Hilarus and Antonius, naming Cn. Plancius as its source; otherwise unidentified.

A 'comrade' (sodalis) of Cicero named alongside Laberius in Fam. 7.11.2 as someone who could put the figure of a 'British jurisconsult' on the stage, implying a writer of mimes or farces. Identity disputed; sometimes connected with L. Valerius the jurisconsult of Cicero's circle.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A close friend of Appius Claudius Pulcher whom Cicero sent along one of the two roads to meet Appius near Iconium in 50 BC. Uncertain; identity among the several Varros not securely fixed.
Tribune 59 BC; Caesarian; cross-examined by Cicero in In Vatinium (March 56 BC) as a hostile witness in the Sestius trial.
The man left in charge of Bibulus's province (Syria) when Bibulus departed late in 50 BC. Praenomen and gens uncertain; identified from the surface form alone.
A landowner near Quintus's Laterium estate (the name is textually corrupt) who had built up the road well in front of his property, unlike his neighbour Locusta. Identity uncertain.

One of three men (with Latinus and Horatius) against whom Cornificius took action in Africa in 43 BC, of which Cicero warmly approves; apparently officials Cornificius stripped of insignia (Fam. 12.30.6). Distinct from the Verrines-era publicanus Venuleius.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Praetor and propraetor of Sicily 73–71 BC; prosecuted by Cicero for extortion in 70 BC; withdrew into exile after the first action.
Verrius, a mutual friend of Cicero and Lentulus Spinther the younger; the consul Pansa gave him assurances on Spinther's behalf about the Asian command (Fam. 12.14).

Distinguished Roman haruspex (diviner of entrails) who famously warned Julius Caesar to beware the Ides of March (Suet. Iul. 81); in Fam. 9.24 Cicero invokes him humorously as a prophet of Paetus's social ruin if he stops attending dinner-parties.
A financial agent and money-man whom Cicero repeatedly consulted in 49 BC over funds, rates of exchange, and the purchase of property during his flight.

A man named Vettius whom Cicero suggests Atticus call in over the building wall by his Palatine palaestra in 59 BC, apparently as a practical adviser or surveyor. Possibly, but not certainly, the political informer L. Vettius of the same year; kept distinct pending confirmation.

A Vibius from whom Cicero received the books of a (silly but not useless) poet, which he was copying to return (Att. 2.20.6). Identity uncertain; possibly connected with the poet Alexander of Ephesus whose works are at issue.
Gaius Vibius Pansa Caetronianus, consul of 43 BC with Hirtius. Mortally wounded at Forum Gallorum during the campaign to relieve Mutina, he died of his wounds in April 43 BC.
The opposing party in the suit between him and the grammarian Nicias over which Cicero sat as judge (Fam. 9.10); otherwise unknown.

A learned jurist and a maternal cousin of Cicero who drafted the recall law that the tribune T. Fadius proposed to bring forward in late 58 BC; Cicero much prefers Visellius's draft to that of Sestius (Att 3.23.4). Identification with the C. Visellius Varro known as Cicero's cognate jurisconsult is probable but not certain.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A woman at Rome whom Cicero faults in Fam. 14.16 for showing too little courtesy to Terentia. Sometimes identified with the mime-actress Volumnia Cytheris (freedwoman of P. Volumnius Eutrapelus, mistress of Antony), but the identification is uncertain.
A senator named Volumnius with whom Cicero had much dealing, mentioned in Fam. 7.32.1 only to be distinguished from the witty P. Volumnius Eutrapelus. Identity otherwise uncertain.

A man named in the Cilician accounts of Fam. 5.20 (49 BC): L. Mescinius wished an obligation (nomen) transferred to him from Valerius, but Cicero's experts judged it could not be done and that Valerius's sureties remained bound. His freeing was Cicero's own counsel. Distinct from the M. Volusius who saw Tiro (Fam. 16.12).
(stub --- to be enriched)
An Athenian financial agent; a debt owed by Xeno, together with the Epirote business, is among the money matters Cicero presses Atticus to arrange (Att. 13.37).

Xeno, an Epicurean of Athens and Apollonis, Atticus's friend and Cicero's banker in the province, through whom Cicero arranged the exchange of provincial funds. The Apollonis banker of Att. 5.13 is the same man.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek philosopher (c. 396--314 BC), head of the Academy after Speusippus, proverbial for integrity; at Att. 1.16.4 Cicero recalls that an Athenian court excused him from swearing the witness-oath, trusting his bare word, as a parallel to the honour shown himself.
A friend of Tiro's at Thyrreum in Acarnania who offered to host and help him during his illness in 50 BC.
Athenian historian and pupil of Socrates (c. 430-354 BC); author of the Anabasis, Hellenica, Memorabilia, Cyropaedia.

A friend of Cicero whose Epicurean leanings Trebatius is said once to have shared (Fam. 7.12.1); the name is corrupt in the manuscripts (printed daggered, '†Zeius'), often emended to Seius. Identity uncertain.

Greek philosopher (c. 334-262 BC) of Citium in Cyprus, founder of the Stoic school at Athens. In Fam. 9.22 Cicero cites his doctrine that each thing should be called by its own name, while noting the Academy's quarrel with the Stoa.
A letter-carrier (probably a slave or freedman of Paetus) who delivered one of Paetus's letters to Cicero, mentioned at the opening of Fam. 9.15 (46 BC). Otherwise unattested.

A man of Blaundus in Asia whom Quintus Cicero wished to prosecute as a matricide; Cicero, finding him almost more distinguished than his own city, instead received him into friendship to defuse Greek complaints. The case is a flashpoint in Cicero's reproach of his brother's severity (Q.fr 1.2).
(stub --- to be enriched)

The famous gymnasium and grove northwest of Athens where Plato taught and which gave its name to the Academic school of philosophy. Denied burial within the city walls, Servius Sulpicius Rufus had Marcellus cremated and a marble monument raised for him in the Academy (Ad Fam. 4.12).

The Greek mainland south of Macedonia — the region of Athens, Corinth, Sparta, and the old Peloponnesian and Boeotian cities. From 146 BC under direct Roman control through the governor of Macedonia, formally a separate province only under Augustus; Verres traversed it on his eastward journey before the praetorship (In Verrem 2.1).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Promontory and harbour at the mouth of the Ambracian Gulf in Acarnania, western Greece, a regular waypoint for ships crossing to Italy; Cicero stops here in November 50 BC.
A town of the Hirpini in Samnite Apulia, on the Via Appia southeast of Beneventum; Cicero stopped there on his return journey from Cilicia in December 50 BC.
The temple of the goddess Ops at Rome, near which a statue of Scipio Africanus the Younger stood; the state treasure was later stored there.
The temple of the earth-goddess Tellus on the Esquiline at Rome, near which Quintus's house lay; Cicero saw to its upkeep and placed Quintus's statue there in 54 BC.

An island in the Saronic Gulf between Attica and the Peloponnese, once a wealthy maritime power but in ruins by Cicero's day. Servius Sulpicius Rufus cites it in Ad Fam. 4.5 as one of the dead and broken cities he saw on his voyage, a memento-mori consolation for the death of Tullia.

Ptolemaic Egypt, in Cicero's day still an independent kingdom under the later Ptolemies, though increasingly Roman client and the subject of repeated annexation rumours. The kingdom is named in In Verrem 2.4 in connection with the candelabrum sent to the Capitoline by Antiochus IV's heirs.
The island of Aenaria (modern Ischia) in the Bay of Naples, to which Antony crossed in 49 BC to promise the exiles their recall.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A region of central Greece, named by Cicero by way of rhetorical contrast with the obscure Cilician uplands in which he was forced to campaign.
The Roman province of Africa (modern Tunisia and adjacent), administered from Utica; the western Mediterranean's chief grain region after Sicily.

The rich tract of public land around Capua in Campania, long a prize in Roman land politics from the Gracchi to Caesar's law of 59 BC; Decimus Brutus proposes drawing on it, with the Sullan lands, to settle the four legions.
The Pomptine marshes and surrounding district of southern Latium, on the road south from Rome. Cicero wrote Fam. 7.18 there in April 53 BC while staying at a villa near Ulubrae.
The territory of Vescia, on the Latium–Campania border near the mouth of the Liris, on the road between Cicero's coastal villas; he turns aside through it on his journeys.
Sicilian city on the south coast; site of the temple of Hercules whose statue Verres tried to remove in In Verrem II.4.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek city in Caria, on the road between Mylasa and Tralles, in the Roman province of Asia. Several of its citizens (Philocles, Pausanias) appear in Cicero's letters of 51--50 BC as parties to debt litigation or recommendation.
The Alban district in the hills southeast of Rome, around the Alban Mount; here the site of Pompey's villa, which Cicero planned to visit on his way to the city.
The great Hellenistic city of Egypt. Cicero alludes (Fam. 7.17.1) to Roman creditors who went to Alexandria with promissory notes to recover the debts of Ptolemy Auletes and came away empty-handed.
The mountain range walling off Italy from Transalpine Gaul; in 43 BC the Alpine passes were held by Lepidus's garrisons, blocking movement between Spain/Gaul and Italy (Fam. 10.31).

Coastal town and resort in Etruria, north-west of Rome on the Tyrrhenian coast (near modern Palo Laziale), the site of fashionable seaside villas; one of the possible landing-points considered for Caesar's return from Africa in 46 BC (Fam. 9.6).
A coastal town of Acarnania in western Greece, about 120 stadia south of Leucas, where Cicero halted on his homeward voyage in 50 BC.

A shrine and ornamented pleasure-ground that Atticus laid out on his estate at Buthrotum in Epirus, named for the nymph Amalthea who nursed the infant Zeus; Cicero alludes to it at Att. 1.13.1 and admired it as a model for his own villas.
The Amanus mountain range dividing Cilicia from Syria. In Fam. 12.15 deserting troops of Dolabella came down through it from Syria into Cilicia.
Greek city and port on the Gulf of Ambracia in Epirus (north-western Greece, modern Arta); Marcus Tullius Cicero the Younger was stationed there with cavalry units under Brutus in spring 43 BC.
Umbrian municipality; home of the Roscii in the case of Pro Roscio Amerino.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town of the Hernici in Latium, southeast of Rome on the Via Latina. Cicero lodged in its territory, at T. Titius's house, en route from Rome in April 56 BC.
Port city on the Adriatic coast of Picenum, one of the places Caesar seized in his advance into Italy in January 49 BC.
A coastal city of the Troad in the province of Asia, home of Megaristus (Q.fr 1.2).
The Anti-Lebanon mountain range in Syria, in the region Pompey reorganized during his Eastern settlement. Cicero invokes it to mock Pompey (Sampsiceramus) as an oriental tribute-taker.
Capital of Seleucid Syria; birthplace of the poet Archias; centre of Greek culture in the eastern Mediterranean.
Coastal town south of Rome; site of one of Cicero's villas; setting of several Atticus letters.
Apamea on the Orontes, a fortress city of Syria. Q. Caecilius Bassus held out here; in Fam. 12.12 Cassius notes Bassus would have kept it 'shut up' against him had his soldiers not defected.
The mountain chain running down the Italian peninsula; in 43 BC its passes shaped the movements of Antony, Ventidius, and Decimus Brutus after the relief of Mutina.
Apollonia ad Rhizonem (modern Pojan, Albania), a major Greek colony on the Illyrian coast; one of Brutus's principal bases in 43 BC alongside Dyrrachium when he commanded forces in the region.
A town of Lydia in the province of Asia whose people Quintus had instructed not to let L. Octavius Naso's estate be diminished before Fundanius was paid (Q.fr 1.2).
Region of southeastern Italy. In Fam. 1.10 Cicero teases the jurisconsult Valerius about retreating to his Apulian estate, where he plays the lone wise man among rustics.
A stream near Tusculum whose water was apportioned by allotment among the neighbouring estates; water rights to it were a recurring concern of the Tusculan landowners, Cicero among them.
A town in the Liri valley on the Via Latina (modern Aquino, Lazio), not far from Cicero's estate at Arpinum; mentioned in Fam. 9.24 as a site of plots against Cicero in 43 BC.
The fertile south-western corner of the Arabian peninsula. Cicero reports a rumour that Pompey was thinking of it, with Egypt and Mesopotamia, as a place of refuge.
A landmark at the foot of Mount Amanus, traditionally marking altars set up by Alexander the Great; Cicero held his camp there for four days during the Amanus campaign of 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).
An estate of Quintus Cicero near Arpinum; in 56 BC building work on it was being held back until Quintus's return from Sardinia.
A river of Narbonese Gaul near Forum Voconii (the modern Argens, near Fréjus); Lepidus pitched camp on it facing Antony in 43 BC.
A district and street in Rome running from the Forum toward the Subura, known for its booksellers' and craftsmen's shops; Quintus Cicero bought property there (Att. 1.14.7).
(stub --- to be enriched)
The kingdom of Armenia beyond the Euphrates, ruled by Artavasdes II, a power of uncertain loyalty between Rome and Parthia during the crisis of 51 BC (Fam. 15.2–15.3).
An important town of Apulia in south-eastern Italy. Caesar halted there on the Kalends of March 49 BC during his advance on Brundisium.

Volscian hill-town in southern Latium, Cicero's native municipality (and Marius's). Granted Roman citizenship in 188 BC; its only elected magistracy was a college of three aediles. Cicero's family estate sat on an islet in the Fibrenus river below the town.
Town in northern Etruria (modern Arezzo), one of the places Caesar seized in his advance into Italy in January 49 BC.

aliases: Asia, Macedonia, Hispania, Gallia, Africa, Sardinia
entities.summary: The Roman province of Asia, organised from Attalid Pergamum by M'. Aquillius after 129 BC; the wealthiest of Rome's eastern provinces, with the cities of Ionia and the Aegean coast. Cicero pairs it with Macedonia, Spain, Gaul, Africa, and Sardinia in his opening stocktaking of provincial misgovernment (In Verrem 2.1).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A small island and villa at the mouth of the river Astura on the Latian coast near Antium; Cicero's secluded retreat, where he withdrew to grieve for Tullia in the spring of 45 BC.

A municipium in Campania, situated between Capua and Neapolis (modern Sant'Arpino). Held ager vectigalis (revenue-bearing public land) in Cisalpine Gaul; the threatened taxation of that land is the subject of Fam. 13.7. The town had long-standing ties of gratitude to Cicero.
Principal city of Attica and seat of the most famous classical democracy; pre-eminent centre of Greek philosophy.
Auction halls at Rome; Cicero invokes them in Pro Quinctio as the milieu of Naevius the auctioneer.
Fashionable seaside resort on the Bay of Naples; hot springs, villas, and (by Cicero's day) a reputation for moral laxity.
Greek coastal city in Caria, on the gulf opposite the island of Cos, in the Roman province of Asia. Among the cities indebted to the publican-banker Cluvius in 51--50 BC.
A small resort settlement on the bay of Naples between Baiae and Misenum, dotted with seaside villas and baths. In 51 BC the impoverished Q. Pompeius Rufus was running a bathing-business there.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Thracian people of the Rhodope mountain area; perpetually at war with Rome and her allies through Cicero's time.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Roman province in northwest Asia Minor (modern northwestern Turkey), bequeathed to Rome by its last king Nicomedes IV in 74 BC and combined with Pontus as a double province; rich source of tax-farming contracts for the publicani.
A city of Phrygia in the province of Asia, home of Zeuxis the alleged matricide (Q.fr 1.2).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A town on the Via Aemilia in Cisalpine Gaul (modern Bologna), east of Mutina; in 43 BC Decimus Brutus was summoned there by the consul Pansa, only to learn on the road that Pansa had died of his Forum Gallorum wounds.
A small town on the Appian Way near Rome; site of the affray of 18 January 52 BC in which Clodius was killed, an event Cicero uses as a chronological marker.

The island beyond Gaul, brought to Roman notice by Caesar's expeditions of 55 and 54 BC; not yet a province in Cicero's day. The correspondence around the British expedition runs through the letters of summer 54 (Ad Familiares 7.7 and following).
The principal port of southern Italy on the Adriatic; the embarkation and return point for Cicero's exile crossings.

A town in southern Illyria (Illyricum), on the route between Apollonia and Dyrrachium. Its citizens (the Bulliones) undertook to give satisfaction to L. Lucceius at Pompey's arbitration, the business Cicero presses on the proconsul Culleolus (Fam. 13.42).
Town on the coast of Epirus opposite Corcyra (modern Butrint, Albania), where Atticus held estates; Cicero's brother Quintus and nephew waited there in late 50 BC.
Greek city on the Bosphorus, gatekeeping the Black Sea grain trade; a frequent target of profitable senatorial legations.
Harbour town on the Latian coast (modern Gaeta), the port nearest Cicero's Formian villa; goods shipped from Greece for his estates were landed here.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A town of Campania on the Via Latina (modern Calvi Risorta), a Roman colony famous for its wine and pottery; the home town of C. Subernius and of M. Planius's heir, with which Cicero claims a great bond (Fam. 9.13).
The promontory at the western mouth of the Mediterranean (the Rock of Gibraltar), one of the Pillars of Hercules; the point from which Balbus crossed to Mauretania.

The fertile coastal region of west-central Italy around Capua and the Bay of Naples; its rich public land (the ager Campanus) was distributed to veterans and the poor under Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC. Cicero owned villas along its coast and sheltered there in 49 BC.
The 'Field of Mars' on the Tiber flats outside the old city, the site of the centuriate and tribal voting assemblies. To 'go down to the Campus' meant to stand for election there.

A mountain range and pass on the Via Egnatia in Illyria/Epirus (modern Albanian highlands east of Dyrrachium); Brutus's camp at its foot on 19 May 43 BC marks his position advancing eastward toward Macedonia.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town in Apulia in south-east Italy (modern Canosa di Puglia) on the route to Brundisium; Cicero mentions a host of Atticus from there at Att. 1.13.1.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The chief hill of Rome, site of the temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, where the consuls take vows on the Kalends of January.

Kingdom and region of central Asia Minor (modern Turkey); in the context of letter Ad Brutum 1.5, it refers to where Gaius Marius was located when he was elected augur under the Lex Domitia (c. 102 BC), establishing the precedent for absent candidacies.
The chief city of Campania; a key Pompeian rallying-point in early 49 BC, where Cicero wrote several of these letters.

Region of south-western Asia Minor (modern south-west Turkey), part of the Roman province of Asia; noted for its Greek cities including Halicarnassus, Mylasa, and Cnidus. In Fam. 2.11 Cicero jokes that the panthers of his Cilician province are emigrating to Caria to escape capture.
A fashionable residential district on the western slope of the Esquiline in Rome, where Quintus Cicero owned the house being rebuilt in 56 BC. Pompey's own house stood here.
Phoenician city-state in North Africa; principal Mediterranean rival of Rome; destroyed in 146 BC by Scipio Aemilianus.
Harbour on the north-east coast of the island of Corcyra (Corfu), the usual departure point for the crossing to the heel of Italy.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Greek coastal city in southern Caria, near the border with Lycia, in the Roman province of Asia. Among the cities indebted to the publican-banker Cluvius in 51--50 BC; pleaded judicial deposit against the running of interest.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Ceos (Keos), a Cycladic island west of Delos touched by Cicero on his Aegean crossing in July 51 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

The Kerameikos, a district of ancient Athens (the potters' quarter and chief cemetery) lying between the Dipylon gate and the Academy. Cicero cites it teasingly as a Greek counterpart to his Tusculan villa (Att. 1.10.1).
A spur or slope of the Palatine hill at Rome, an aristocratic residential quarter. Milo's house, which Clodius attempted to storm and burn in November 57 BC, stood there (Att. 4.3.2).

The Thracian Chersonese (modern Gallipoli peninsula, Turkey), a strategically vital land-bridge between Europe and Asia; in 43 BC it was the scene of Dolabella's attempted military moves against Brutus's forces.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

The province of southern Asia Minor, organised in the 90s BC and reorganised by Pompey after the pirate war (67 BC); in 51–50 BC Cicero's own province. Already named in In Verrem 2.1, it became the chief setting of his Ad Familiares of that pair of years.
A hill-town in Picenum founded and fortified by Labienus at his own expense; reported held for Pompey at the opening of the civil war in January 49 BC, though it soon went over to Caesar.

An open public space in the southern Campus Martius at Rome, outside the pomerium, used for markets, games, and public meetings; Pompey was brought to a contio there in 61 BC because he could not enter the city without forfeiting his triumph (Att. 1.14.1).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town in Cisalpine Gaul on the Via Aemilia east of Bononia. In Fam. 12.5 Hirtius is stationed there with his army in the Mutina campaign of early 43 BC.

A small town of the Aequi in central Italy (Cliternia, in the upper Aniene/Sabine country), here named in Fam. 9.22 for its cheeses as a contrast to those of Lanuvium in Cicero's joke about decent and indecent words.

Dorian Greek city on the south-west tip of Caria, civitas libera under Rome; celebrated for its sanctuary of Aphrodite housing Praxiteles's Cnidian Venus. Cicero names the Cnidians in the Verrines (2.4) for the artworks Verres extracted from their city.
An ancient Ionian city of the province of Asia, north of Ephesus. In Fam. 13.69 an unnamed Colophonian is the adversary of Cicero's friend Mithres in a dispute over an estate.
A Phrygian city on Cicero's route through his assize-district, where he was received with honour in 51 BC.

Comana in Cappadocia, a temple-state of the goddess Ma whose chief priest held quasi-royal power; the incumbent priest threatened to defend himself by arms during the disturbances of 51 BC until Cicero engineered his withdrawal (Fam. 15.4).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A small kingdom of northern Syria on the upper Euphrates, ruled by Antiochus I; in 51 BC reported as the route by which Parthian forces were said to be advancing into the Roman province.
A stronghold on Mount Amanus, in the sector held by the legate C. Pomptinus, taken after stubborn fighting in Cicero's Amanus campaign of 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).
Comum (Novum Comum) in Transpadane Gaul; the consul M. Claudius Marcellus had one of its magistrates flogged in 51 BC to deny the validity of Caesar's grant of Roman citizenship to the colony.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Island off the north-western coast of Greece (modern Corfu), on the sea route between the Greek mainland and Brundisium; a staging-post for travellers between Greece and Italy. T. Antistius fell ill and died there while attempting to comply with Caesar's order to return to Rome (13.29).
(stub --- to be enriched)

The chief town of the Paeligni in central Italy, on the Via Valeria, where L. Domitius Ahenobarbus tried to hold out for Pompey. Its siege and surrender to Caesar in February 49 BC — followed by Caesar's celebrated clemency to the captured commanders — was the decisive turn of the war's Italian phase.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A harbour town on the coast of Cilicia (Corycus), with a closed/defensible port. In Fam. 12.13 the Dolabellan admiral L. Figulus shut himself up there.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A promontory on the north coast of Cyprus (the headland of Crommyon), used as the dateline of Fam. 12.13. Cassius Parmensis wrote from the fleet there in June 43 BC.
Greek city on the Bruttian coast of southern Italy. Cicero names it, with Thurii, as an embarkation point should Puteoli prove impossible.
A town on the Isère in the territory of the Allobroges (modern Grenoble). Plancus made it his base after retreating across the river in June 43 BC, and dated Fam. 10.23 from there.
Greek city of Campania on the bay of Naples; site of Cicero's Cumae villa, much used as a writing retreat in the 50s.

A town in south-eastern Cappadocia below Mount Taurus, near the Cilician border. Cicero pitched camp there for three days in 51 BC, dealing with King Ariobarzanes and watching Syria and Armenia (Fam. 15.2).
The large island in the eastern Mediterranean. In 43 BC the republican fleets under Lentulus Spinther the younger and the Cassian squadrons used it as a base in the campaign against Dolabella.
A district of northern Syria bordering Cicero's province, the first region overrun when the Parthians under Pacorus crossed the Euphrates in 51 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The mountainous Illyrian region on the eastern Adriatic coast, home of the Dalmatae, where Vatinius campaigned in 45 BC; a refuge into which Cicero's runaway slave Dionysius might have fled.
(stub --- to be enriched)
City in Lycaonia, in the southern interior of Asia Minor, seat of the dynast Antipater (Antipater of Derbe), Cicero's guest-friend. Mentioned in Fam. 13.73 (mid-40s BC).
A town in Liguria (modern Tortona) on the road from Cisalpine Gaul toward the coast; Decimus Brutus's camp there in early May 43 BC as he pursued Antony.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A city of Phrygia in the province of Asia whose people were among the Ciceros' bitterest enemies until Cicero won over their leader Hermippus (Q.fr 1.2).

The sanctuary of Zeus in Epirus, home to the oldest Greek oracle, whose prophecies were read from the rustling of a sacred oak. Cicero jokes that Terentia's wooded estate rivals Epirus, lacking only the Dodonaean oak.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Adriatic port city of Epirus, terminus of the Via Egnatia; Cicero's residence in late 58 BC and the city he sailed from on returning from exile.
Town in Attica, seat of the Eleusinian Mysteries of Demeter and Persephone; Appius Claudius Pulcher was building a propylaeum (monumental gateway) to its sanctuary in 50 BC.

The 'Free Cilicians', autonomous highland tribes of Mount Amanus who had never been subject to any king; Cicero campaigned against them in 51 BC, taking their town Pindenissum. Staged as type 'place' (a populus) with categories including 'people' per the session convention.

City and territory in the north-western Peloponnese, famous as the home of the Olympic sanctuary and games. In the Roman period Elis was part of the province of Achaea and lay under the jurisdiction of the proconsul. Roman negotiatores (merchants and businessmen) were active there, as attested by M. Mindius (Fam. 13.26).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

A city on the eastern coast of the Argolid in the Peloponnese, famous for its sanctuary of Asclepius. In Ad Fam. 4.12 Servius Sulpicius Rufus sails from Epidaurus to the Piraeus, where he meets the doomed Marcellus.
A town of Cilicia near Mount Amanus where a praetorian cohort of Cicero's was stationed on garrison duty; Cicero encamped beside it before his night-march up Amanus in October 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).

Region of north-western Greece, lying to the west of Macedonia and north of Achaea; noted for its connections to the Molossian kingdom and the oracle at Dodona. T. Pomponius Atticus owned extensive and profitable estates there, which underpinned a large part of his fortune.
A Roman colony of Cisalpine Gaul (modern Ivrea) at the foot of the Alps, guarding the approach to the Aosta valley; Decimus Brutus dates Fam. 11.20 from here in May 43 BC.
A town in the Hirpini of Samnite Italy on the road from Beneventum toward Brundisium; a posting-station from which Atticus had dispatched letters.
A town described by Cicero as the 'capital' of Mount Amanus (more a city than a village), stormed and taken in his Amanus campaign of October 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).
(stub --- to be enriched)
The region of west-central Italy north of Rome; in May 43 BC Decimus Brutus reckons it a possible refuge for Antony because it was then without an army.

The great river of Mesopotamia marking the frontier between Roman Syria and the Parthian empire. Its crossing by Parthian forces in 51 BC is the central event of Cicero's Cilician dispatches (Fam. 15.1–15.3).
The chief river of Laconia, flowing past Sparta. Cicero uses "sitting by the Eurotas" as shorthand for idle retirement in Greece, weighed against a provincial command.
A town in the Liri valley (modern Ceprano, Lazio) on the Via Latina, close to Aquinum; named alongside Aquinum in Fam. 9.24 as a site of plots against Cicero in early 43 BC.
A small shrine of the old Roman goddess Furrina near Quintus's Laterium estate, used as a roadside landmark.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Coastal town in southern Latium; site of one of Cicero's villas, and the place of his murder in December 43 BC.

A market town and posting-station on the Appian Way about 43 miles south of Rome, at the edge of the Pomptine Marshes; founded by the censor Appius Claudius Caecus. A regular stage on Cicero's journeys to and from Latium (Att 2.10).
A town in Cisalpine Gaul on the Via Aemilia (modern Imola). In Fam. 12.5 the young Caesar (Octavian) is stationed there with his army during the Mutina campaign.
A way-station on the Via Aemilia east of Mutina in Cisalpine Gaul. Site of the battle of 14 April 43 BC in which the consular armies of Pansa and Hirtius checked Antony (Fam. 10.30).

A coastal town in Narbonese Gaul (modern Fréjus) founded by Julius Caesar, on the route between Italy and the Spanish provinces. A focal point of the converging armies of Antony, Lepidus and Plancus in May 43 BC.
The civic and judicial heart of Rome; site of the Rostra, the senatorial Curia, the basilicas where Cicero pleads.
A posting-station on the road through Narbonese Gaul, about twenty-four miles west of Forum Iulii (Fréjus), between it and Aquae Sextiae (Aix). Lepidus encamped there to await Plancus in May 43 BC.

A Latin colony in the Liris valley of Latium, destroyed by Rome in 125 BC after its revolt; its territory (the ager Fregellanus) was redistributed and absorbed by neighbouring Fabrateria Nova. The landholding C. Valgius Hippianus bought lay in this Fregellan territory (Fam. 13.76).
The strait between Sicily and the Italian mainland (Strait of Messina), crossed by travellers from Sicily; Clodius claimed to have come 'from the Strait' to Rome in seven days.
A town of the Hernici in Latium on the Via Latina; Cicero owned a farm (the Frusinate estate) in its territory, whose buy-back and solvency he worries over from Brundisium (Att. 11.4, 11.13).
An estate among Quintus's holdings that he wished to keep, discussed alongside questions of water rights and resale value.
A well-watered property bought for Quintus from Fufidius at Arpinum for 101,000 sesterces in 54 BC.
An estate (formerly belonging to a Manilius) among Quintus's holdings near Arpinum, with a villa under construction by the contractor Diphilus.
Wealthy Phoenician-founded port city in Further Spain (modern Cadiz), home town of the Cornelii Balbi; a base of Pollio's operations in 43 BC.

The Roman province (Cisalpine, Transalpine, or both depending on context). In Pro Quinctio: the partnership's pasture land and Naevius's auction site; in the Caesarian letters: the theatre of the Gallic War.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The region of Cisalpine Gaul north of the river Po. In Fam. 12.5 Cicero notes that Cassius's Transpadane clients were strongly attached to the republican cause.
The lands of the Germani beyond the Rhine; named at 49 BC among the far theatres from which forces and rumours bore on the war.

A raised platform beside the Comitium in the Roman Forum where foreign envoys stood to watch Senate proceedings. In 57 BC Clodius's gangs raised their shout from here and the adjacent steps.
A small barren island of the Cyclades passed by Cicero on his Aegean crossing in July 51 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A river near Velia in Lucania (modern Alento), famous in antiquity; Cicero calls it a noble river and urges Trebatius not to leave his ancestral property beside it (Fam. 7.20). Not to be confused with Halaesa in Sicily (place:halaesa).

Place or estate at issue in the Egnatius–Aquilius matter referenced in Att. 4.12; otherwise unknown.
