(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Sicilian hill-town of the Ceres cult, in the centre of the island; site of the rape of Proserpine in myth, and of Verres's stripping of the temple of Ceres in In Verrem II.4.

Greek city in Caria, in the Roman province of Asia. Among the cities indebted to the publican-banker Cluvius in 51--50 BC. Probably Heraclea Salbace, the inland Carian city, distinct from the homonymous cities of Lucania and Sicily.
Greek federate city in Lucania; the city in whose roll Archias was enrolled, the basis of his claim to Roman citizenship under the lex Plautia Papiria.
A town in Macedonia, most likely Heraclea Lyncestis (modern Bitola, North Macedonia), where Brutus planned to rendezvous with young Marcus Cicero as the army concentrated for the eastern campaign.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

Town on the bay of Naples (later buried by Vesuvius in AD 79). In Fam. 9.25 the 'Herculanean estate' jointly owned by M. and Q. Fadius, advertised for forced sale, is the object of the brothers' dispute.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A major town of Further Spain on the Baetis (modern Seville); home of an auctioneer's tout whom Balbus threw to the beasts.
The two Roman Spanish provinces; assigned jointly to Pompey by the lex Trebonia of February 55 BC for his five-year proconsulship.
Suburban estate of Furius Crassipes outside Rome; a frequent dinner venue for Cicero in the mid-50s.
Town (modern Otranto) on the heel of Italy in Calabria, the nearest Italian landfall from Corcyra; Cicero makes for it on his crossing of November 50 BC.
Mountain near Athens, by metonymy a sign of Athenian (Academic, Stoic) philosophical learning.
A town of Lydia in the province of Asia, south of Sardis, home of Attalus (Q.fr 1.2).
The chief town of Lycaonia in the interior of Asia Minor (modern Konya). Cicero's army was encamped near it in late August 51 BC when Antiochus of Commagene's envoys reached him (Fam. 15.3).
Ilium (Troy), on the coast of the Troad near the Hellespont in the province of Asia. At Att. 3.8 the freedman Phaethon's ship is blown back from here, delaying his crossing into Macedonia.
The region along the eastern Adriatic coast, part of Caesar's command; reckoned by Cicero among the theatres and provinces in play in 49 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Interamna Lirenas (or Interamna Nahars), a town of central Italy on the road north from Rome; cited in Cicero's mocking timetable of Clodius's nocturnal journeys.

A Ligurian coastal town and its people (the Intimilii), in the western Alps near modern Ventimiglia. In early 49 BC the townsfolk rose in arms after the murder of a local notable, drawing Caelius and his cohorts up through the snowbound passes.
The Greek-settled coastal region of western Asia Minor, the most celebrated part of the province of Asia; Samos was reckoned its noblest city.
A left-bank tributary of the Rhone in Transalpine Gaul (modern Isère). Plancus bridged it in a single day in 43 BC and later broke the bridges to cover his retreat from Lepidus and Antony.
A mountainous district of southern Asia Minor on the Taurus, attached to Cicero's province; he marched through it en route from Lycaonia to Cappadocia in 51 BC (Fam. 15.2).

Coastal town at the north-eastern corner of the Mediterranean (modern Iskenderun Bay, Turkey), famous as the site of Alexander the Great's decisive victory over Darius III in 333 BC. Cicero was hailed as imperator near Issus after his Amanus campaign (Fam. 2.10).
The narrow neck of land joining the Peloponnese to mainland Greece, site of the Isthmian Games near Corinth; Cicero recalls a statue of A. Postumius Albinus standing there (Att. 13.32).
The Italian peninsula; in Cicero's letters of the civil war years synonymous with the homeland Pompey's army left behind in 49 BC.
Island in the Ionian Sea, kingdom of Odysseus; the figure of beloved homeland in Homer.
A small Etruscan coastal port (later Livorno/Leghorn), one of the two harbours from which Pompey planned to embark for Sardinia in April 56 BC.
A locality (a 'pool' or basin) in Rome by which stood the Liciniana house that Cicero rented for Quintus in 56 BC while Quintus's own house in the Carinae was being rebuilt.

A lake in the Sabine country near Reate, drained by Manius Curius Dentatus's channel into the Nar (over the falls now called the Cascata delle Marmore); the drainage dried the Rosian plain and pitted Reate against Interamna (Att. 4.15.5).
(stub --- to be enriched)

Latin town on the Via Appia in the Alban Hills, famed for its cult of Juno Sospita and for its produce. The 'colei Lanuvini' (Lanuvine cheeses) appear in Fam. 9.22 as an example of a word whose decency depends on context.
Laodicea ad Lycum in Phrygia, chief city of the largest assize-district of Cicero's province; he held court there and made it his base before and after the Amanus campaign.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Laodicea ad Mare, a port on the Syrian coast (modern Latakia). Here Dolabella took refuge and was besieged by C. Cassius Longinus in 43 BC. Not to be confused with the inland Phrygian Laodicea in Asia.
Town in Samnium (modern Larino), famous as the home of the defendant in Pro Cluentio.

The principal city of Thessaly in northern Greece, lying on the river Peneios. A major Hellenistic and Roman-era centre; its leading citizens had dealings with Roman magistrates visiting or governing the Greek provinces. Larissa lay within (or on the border of) the province of Macedonia, but its notables naturally sought the favour of the proconsul of adjacent Achaea.
An estate of Quintus Cicero in the territory of Arpinum, near the Via Latina, where Cicero stayed during his April 56 BC tour of the family properties.
The region of west-central Italy south-east of Rome, homeland of the Latins; in its extended sense (Latium adiectum) reaching toward Campania. It contained Rome and the old Latin towns.
A locality near Formiae on the coast of Latium, evidently the site of a shrine of the goddess Laverna; here Pompey overtook Cicero on 25 December 50 BC for their long private conference.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
An island and town off the coast of Acarnania in western Greece, a stage on Cicero's homeward route from Cilicia in 50 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town and estate-district on the Campanian coast north of Cumae, among Cicero's haunts during his irresolute spring of 49 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Etruscan town on the southern road into Cisalpine Gaul; the site of the triumvirs' summit conference of April 56 BC, after which Cicero accepted political silence on Caesar's continuation in Gaul.
A town in Apulia, southern Italy. In Fam. 15.15 Cicero recalls letters Cassius had sent him from Luceria (early 49 BC), where Pompeian forces concentrated at the start of the civil war.
Coastal lagoon between Cumae and Puteoli, famed for its oyster beds and luxury villas.

The cave-shrine at the foot of the Palatine in Rome, sacred to Faunus/Lupercus and the legendary den of the she-wolf who suckled Romulus and Remus. Cicero uses it as a byword for prime, expensive Roman real estate when comparing it to Velia (Fam. 7.20).
The western region of the Iberian peninsula (roughly modern Portugal and western Spain) within Pollio's province of Further Spain; he had wintered his legions there in 43 BC.

A region of the south-central Asia Minor interior, between Phrygia and Cappadocia, attached to Cicero's province of Cilicia; on its borders with Cappadocia he received the warning of the Parthian crossing (Fam. 15.1–15.2).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

The southeastern promontory of the Peloponnese (modern Cape Maleas), proverbially dangerous to navigation; the route a ship would round to sail from Greece toward Italy. In Ad Fam. 4.12 Marcellus intended to sail past Malea on his way home to Italy when he was murdered.
The Greek city on the south coast of Gaul (modern Marseille); in 49 BC it shut its gates to Caesar and was besieged, a focus of the war news Cicero followed.

A Greek city on the isthmus between Attica and the Peloponnese, formerly prosperous but decayed by the first century BC. Servius Sulpicius Rufus names it among the ruined cities of his Saronic voyage in his consolation to Cicero (Ad Fam. 4.5).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
An urban deme of Athens, west of the Acropolis, where the house of Epicurus stood; the 'ruins at Melite' are the property at the heart of the Patro/Memmius affair.
The land between the Tigris and the Euphrates. Cicero names it among the eastern refuges (with Egypt and Arabia Felix) that Pompey was rumoured to be considering.
City on the strait between Sicily and Italy; Verres's collaborators' city, and the site of the cross facing Italy on which Cicero's Roman citizen Publius Gavius was hung.
A town of Phrygia in the conventus region of Cicero's Cilician command, where a vote to commend Appius to the Senate was raised.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town of southern Latium on the Via Appia near the mouth of the river Liris. Cicero wrote Fam. 14.14 from here in late January 49 BC while tracking the early movements of the civil war.
A promontory and harbour at the north-western end of the Bay of Naples in Campania, a fashionable villa district; Cicero promises Atticus a topographical sketch of it and Puteoli at Att. 1.13.5.
Volcanic massif south-east of Rome with many luxury villas; site of Pompey's Alban estate.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A city of Cilicia Pedias on the river Pyramus; Cicero was camped in its territory in October 51 BC as he advanced toward Mount Amanus.

Mutina (modern Modena), a fortified town in Cisalpine Gaul where Decimus Brutus was besieged by Antony in 43 BC. The two battles fought to relieve it (Forum Gallorum and Mutina) cost both consuls their lives and broke Antony's siege.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Principal inland city of Caria, in the Roman province of Asia, an old Hekatomnid centre. Among the cities indebted to the publican-banker Cluvius in 51--50 BC; Cicero relied on the local magnate Euthydemus to send the city's legal representatives to Rome.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Coastal city of Aeolis on the western shore of Asia Minor, in the Roman province of Asia. Cicero records receiving a letter from L. Mescinius there (Fam. 5.20).
A region of north-western Asia Minor within the province of Asia, here noted for the brigandage Quintus suppressed; its people the Mysians were a byword for low origin.
Principal city of Lesbos; another conventional place of cultivated Roman exile.
A sulphurous tributary of the Tiber in Umbria; the Lacus Velinus was drained into it through Manius Curius Dentatus's mountain cut, the cause of the Reate-Interamna dispute (Att. 4.15.5).
Roman colony in Transalpine Gaul (founded 118 BC); in Pro Quinctio, the proposed site for an auction Quinctius was dissuaded from holding.
A staging-post in Lucania, southern Italy, on the road south toward Brundisium; a stage on Cicero's flight into exile in April 58 BC.

Roman colony and trading town in southern Dalmatia (Illyricum), on the river Naro (modern Neretva). It lay within the command of P. Sulpicius Rufus; Cicero's runaway slave Dionysius was seen there (Fam. 13.77).
Greek-foundation city of Campania; home of L. Papirius Paetus and a haunt of Cicero's literary set in the 50s and 40s.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The grove and sanctuary of Diana at Aricia (Lake Nemi), south of Rome; Caesar was building a costly villa there in 50 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Principal inland city of Bithynia, on the eastern shore of Lake Ascanius. In 51--50 BC owed eight million sesterces to the estate of T. Pinnius; Cicero, as guardian of Pinnius's son, asked P. Silius the propraetor to expedite payment.
A town in inland Campania, east of Mount Vesuvius; Cicero asks whether an estate in the territory of Pompeii or Nola is for sale (Att. 13.8).

Colony in Cisalpine Gaul (modern Como) refounded by Julius Caesar in 59 BC with a substantial injection of new settlers and grants of Latin or Roman status. Caesar's citizenship grants to Comensians were disputed by contemporaries (cf. consul Marcellus in 51 BC).
Greek city in inland Caria, in the Roman province of Asia, on the Maeander valley. A hereditary client-city of Tiberius Claudius Nero in 51--50 BC, commended by him through Cicero to P. Silius.

The encircling outer Ocean of ancient geography, here the open sea around Britain (the Atlantic/English Channel) which a Roman crossing to Britain had to brave. At Fam. 7.10.2 Cicero teases that Trebatius, a keen swimmer, refused to swim in it.
A port town on the northeast coast of Sardinia, from which Quintus, then serving in the province, sent letters to Cicero in 56 BC.
The Panhellenic sanctuary of Zeus in Elis, site of the Olympic Games; Cicero proposes it as a possible dramatic setting for a philosophical dialogue on the model of Dicaearchus (Att. 13.30).
(stub --- to be enriched)
A harbour on the coast of Epirus (modern Saranda, Albania), opposite Corcyra; Cicero jokes about the favourable 'Onchesmite' wind that sped him to Brundisium.
(stub --- to be enriched)

The port at the mouth of the Tiber serving Rome, c. 25 km south-west of the city; proposed as the more convenient landing for Caesar's return from Africa in 46 BC (Fam. 9.6). Traditionally founded by king Ancus Marcius.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The Palatine Hill at Rome, site of Cicero's house and its attached Greek-style wrestling-school (palaestra). The collapsing wall of Att. 2.4/2.6/2.7 bordered this property.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A city on the southwest coast of Cyprus, famed for its cult of Aphrodite. Cicero commends its people especially to the quaestor C. Sextilius Rufus in Fam. 13.48.
A house at Velia owned by Trebatius (formerly of a Papirius), notable for a lotus-tree said to hold even strangers captive (Fam. 7.20).

A Greek city on the Hellespont (the Propontis coast of the Troad), in the Roman province of Asia. Its civic community (Pariana civitas) had decreed and granted land rights to L. Genucilius Curvus (Fam. 13.53).
Town in Cisalpine Gaul on the Via Aemilia, near Mutina; reported sacked in the aftermath of the Mutina battles of April 43 BC.
The temple of Athena on the Athenian Acropolis; Cicero recalls an artistic device of Atticus's that he had seen displayed there, pairing the tyrant-slayers Ahala and Brutus (Att. 13.40).

The Arsacid empire east of the Euphrates; Rome's principal eastern adversary. After the destruction of Crassus's army at Carrhae in 53 BC, Parthian incursions into Syria and Cilicia were a recurring concern of the proconsular correspondence (Fam. 15.1--15.4, 15.9).

Major harbour city of Achaea (modern Patras, Greece), on the southern shore of the Gulf of Corinth; the main transit port between Italy and mainland Greece. In 46 BC it lay within the province of Achaea governed by Servius Sulpicius Rufus. M'. Curius operated his business there; Cicero used Curius's house during the civil war.
The old royal capital of Macedon, on the road inland from the coast toward Thessalonica. At Att. 3.8 the freedman Phaethon catches up with Cicero here.

The southern peninsula of mainland Greece, in which Patrae (where Curius the banker had settled) lies; in the Roman period part of the province of Achaea. Cicero contrasts its provincial dullness with the refinement of Rome in Fam. 7.28.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

An important city in Galatia (north-central Asia Minor), the great cult-centre of Cybele (the Great Mother); Cicero's freedmen Diogenes and Philo passed through it en route to the tetrarch Adiatorix (Fam. 2.12, 50 BC).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town in Phrygia (Asia Minor), on the road through the province; the place where the businessman L. Oppius conducted his trade (Fam. 13.43).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

"Acquired Phrygia," the northeastern district of Phrygia (in the province of Asia) attached to Cicero's command of Cilicia; envoys from its communities approached Cicero about excessive embassy allowances (Fam. 3.10.6).
The region on the Adriatic coast of central Italy, a Pompeian stronghold from which Pompey could levy supporters. In 56 BC a large band was expected thence to back Milo against Clodius's gangs.

A fortified hill town of the Free Cilicians (Eleutherocilices) on Mount Amanus, harbouring runaways and awaiting the Parthians; besieged for fifty-seven days and captured by Cicero in 51 BC, the action for which he claimed a supplicatio (Fam. 15.4).
A fortified hill-town of the Free Cilicians (Eleutherocilices) on Mount Amanus that Cicero besieged and stormed during the Saturnalia of 51 BC; its people are the Pindenissites.

The principal harbour of Athens, some 10 km southwest of the city. In May 45 BC M. Claudius Marcellus (cos. 51) was murdered near the Piraeus by his companion P. Magius Cilo, as reported by Servius Sulpicius Rufus in Ad Fam. 4.12.
The Etruscan coastal city of Pisa, the alternative harbour from which Pompey planned to embark for Sardinia in April 56 BC.
Town on the Adriatic coast of Umbria (modern Pesaro), one of the places Caesar seized in his advance into Italy in January 49 BC.
A public reservoir or bathing-basin in the southern part of Rome near the Aventine and the Via Appia, which gave its name to the surrounding district; the flood of 54 BC reached it.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Town in Cisalpine Gaul, modern Piacenza; an Insubrian colony of the second century BC. Piso's maternal grandfather's town.

A town in Liguria (modern Pollenzo, in Piedmont) commanding a route through the Apennine foothills; in May 43 BC Decimus Brutus raced to occupy it ahead of Antony, reaching it one hour before Antony's cavalry under Trebellius.
Town on the bay of Naples, on the slope of Vesuvius; site of one of Cicero's villas, the Pompeianum, where he stayed before joining Pompey in 49 BC.
The first permanent stone theatre at Rome, built by Pompey in the Campus Martius and dedicated in 55 BC; the games of Fam. 7.1 inaugurated it.
The Pomptine Marshes (Pomptinus ager) in southern Latium, crossed by the Via Appia between Tarracina and the Alban hills; a stage on Cicero's route up to Rome.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A gate in the Servian Wall of Rome at the start of the Via Appia, the point from which travellers from the south entered the city. Cicero passed through it on his triumphal return from exile in September 57 BC (Att. 4.1.5).
A portico at Rome built by Q. Lutatius Catulus (cos. 78 BC), among the public works Cicero attended to on Quintus's reminder in 54 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A coastal city near Egypt (Ptolemais of Phoenicia/Acre, or another Ptolemais) where Cicero proposes Lentulus station Ptolemy Auletes while advancing on Alexandria with his fleet and army (Fam 1.7.4).
The chief Roman port for the eastern trade, on the bay of Naples just north-west of the Lucrine lake; a centre of commerce and gossip.
A river of Cilicia (modern Ceyhan) in southern Asia Minor; Cicero was encamped beside it (Fam. 3.11.1) during his proconsulship of Cilicia in 50 BC.

The mountain range dividing Gaul from Spain, whose passes the Pompeian legate L. Afranius held against Caesar's advance in 49 BC. Cicero hopes for news that Afranius has beaten Caesar's officer Trebonius there.
City of Cisalpine Gaul near the Adriatic coast, one of Caesar's bases during his proconsulship; Plancus was there in 51 BC. A key staging-point on the eve of the civil war.
A Sabine town northeast of Rome, in a fertile upland valley; in 54 BC the Reatines engaged Cicero to plead their water-dispute against neighbouring Interamna over the Velinus drainage (Att. 4.15.5).
A town on the via Aemilia in Cisalpine Gaul (modern Reggio Emilia), west of Mutina; Decimus Brutus encamped there at the end of April 43 BC while setting out after Antony.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The river Rhone in Transalpine Gaul; Lepidus broke camp at its confluence (with the Argenteus) to march against the Antonii in 43 BC.
Aegean island; rhetorical and philosophical centre; conventional place of polite Roman exile in the Greek east.
The capital. Almost every speech is delivered here; almost every letter is sent from or to here.
The speaker's platform in the Forum Romanum, faced with the prows (rostra) of captured warships. In Fam. 12.3 Antony sets up there a statue inscribed to Caesar 'to a parent most well-deserving.'
The 'Sacred Way', the principal processional street of Rome running through the Forum up toward the Palatine. Clodius's gang ambushed Cicero as he came down it in November 57 BC (Att. 4.3.2).
The chief city of Cyprus, whose council borrowed from M. Brutus's agents at 48 per cent; Cicero's refusal to enforce the bond against the Salaminians is the crux of the Scaptius affair.

The mountain defile of the Sierra Morena near the town of Castulo, the chief land route between Further Spain and the rest of the empire; in 43 BC made impassable to couriers by brigands and rival patrols (Fam. 10.31).

A town of the Ambiani in Gallia Belgica (modern Amiens) that served as Caesar's army headquarters and winter quarters in 54-53 BC, where Trebatius was stationed. Cicero turns its name into a running joke for the back-of-beyond.
The mountainous region of central-southern Italy east of Campania; Antony was reported heading there in May 44 BC (Att. 14.20).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Ancient Lydian capital, the chief inland city of the Roman province of Asia, on the road between Pergamum and Ephesus. The seat of one of the conventus of the province.
A locality (the small Latin town, or a place of that name in the Arpinum district) toward which the Laterium road ran past Furina's shrine.
Sicilian city in the west; site of the bronze Diana whose theft by Verres is one of the famous narrative passages of In Verrem II.4.

The fortified Syrian port of Antioch, founded by Seleucus I in the coastal district of Pieria; the point of departure for a freedman of C. Trebonius who brought Cicero news from the East (Att. 11.20). Distinct from Seleucia-on-the-Tigris.
The great Hellenistic city on the Tigris, a chief seat of Parthian power; Caelius names its storming, alongside the capture of "Arsaces," as the epitome of an eastern conquest (Fam. 8.14).
The chamber where the Senate met; on the north side of the Forum.
A scenic spot of pools and falls near Reate ('Seven Waters'), to which Cicero's host Axius took him during the Velinus arbitration of 54 BC (Att. 4.15.5).
A stronghold on Mount Amanus taken by Cicero's forces during the Amanus campaign of 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).
(stub --- to be enriched)
First Roman province (from 241 BC); subject of In Verrem; its cities supplied much of Rome's grain.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Side (Latin Sida), a port city of Pamphylia, described in Fam. 12.15 as the easternmost district of Lentulus Spinther's province of Asia. The pursuit of Dolabella's fleet reached this far.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A pine forest on the Campanian coast between Cumae and the mouth of the Volturnus, near the bay of Naples. Cicero passes through it in Fam. 9.23 on the way to visit Paetus.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town on the coast of Latium/Campania on the Via Appia, near the Campanian border. Cicero owned a small lodging (deversoriolum) there which Cornificius had slighted by passing it by.
A port on the Apulian coast below the Garganus promontory. Caesar posted a legion there, with others at Brundisium and Tarentum, to close the seaward exits of Italy in 49 BC.
A major Greek city on the coast of the province of Asia (modern Izmir); home of Nicias, and the place where Quintus had two Mysians sewn into a sack as a show of severity (Q.fr 1.2).
A district and estate near Lanuvium, south of Rome, where Clodia Metelli held property. Cicero awaits a Clodian conversation she will report on her return from Solonium (Att 2.9.1).
Doric city-state of the Peloponnese; with its dual kingship and gerousia, the prime classical example of the mixed constitution.
Coastal town south of Pompeii, looking onto the Stabian gulf; M. Marius's villa had a window cut to its view.
The territory of the Ligurian Statielli around Aquae Statiellae (modern Acqui Terme); Decimus Brutus's camp there in early May 43 BC during the pursuit of Antony toward the Alps.
A Roman municipium in Latium Adiectum (modern Sessa Aurunca, Campania), in the region once inhabited by the Aurunci; its citizens held full Roman citizenship.

A town of the Paeligni in central Italy (modern Sulmona), held for Pompey early in 49 BC; its commander C. Atius opened the gates to Antony despite a garrison of five cohorts (Att. 8.4). Later famous as the birthplace of Ovid.
The promontory and deme at the southern tip of Attica, site of the temple of Poseidon; Cicero cites it in a grammatical debate over whether Attic demes count as towns.
The Sybota islets and anchorage off the coast of Epirus opposite southern Corcyra, where Cicero broke his voyage and feasted on Atticus's provisions in 51 BC.
A town of Phrygia in the interior of Asia Minor, seat of one of the assize-districts (conventus) attached to Cicero's province; he held court there for three days in 51 BC (Fam. 15.4).
Capital of Roman Sicily; centre of Verres's depredations; site of the Achradina executions and the Latomiae quarry-prison in In Verrem II.5.
Greek colony in eastern Sicily; under Dionysius I and II, the seat of the most famous classical tyranny.
Roman province organised by Pompey in 64 BC; assigned to Crassus by the lex Trebonia of February 55 BC for his five-year proconsulship.
Syros (Syra), a central Cycladic island on Cicero's Aegean route between Gyaros and Delos in July 51 BC.
A money-changer's table or bank in the Forum; the spot where Naevius staged his witness-testifying that Quinctius had not appeared.
A spot in the Roman Forum beside the Curia, named for a Valerian painting there, used as a banking and notarial station. Terentia was hauled there from the shrine of Vesta in 58 BC.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A site (the textually disputed 'castra taricheis/Tadolicis') in or near Syria where C. Cassius Longinus encamped in March 43 BC; the dateline of Fam. 12.11. Location uncertain.

Coastal town in southern Latium on the Via Appia (ancient Anxur, modern Terracina), a convenient overnight stop on the road south from Rome. In Fam. 7.23 Cicero says he would rather have spent the money on a lodging there than on the statues.
The chief city of Cilicia. In Fam. 12.13 the Tarsians are reproached as 'basest of allies' for summoning Dolabella.
(stub --- to be enriched)
The Taurus mountain range running across southern Asia Minor, the great natural barrier behind which lay Cilicia; 'beyond the Taurus' denotes the allied dynasts of the interior (Fam. 15.1–15.2).

The chief town of the Sidicini in northern Campania, on the Via Latina north of Capua; in January 49 BC Pompey and the consuls gathered there, and L. Caesar brought Caesar's terms to Pompey at Teanum. Distinct from Teanum Apulum.

A people of Mount Amanus, neighbours of the inhabitants of Pindenissum and (says Cicero) their equals in wickedness and audacity; they gave Cicero hostages after the fall of Pindenissum in 51 BC. Staged as type 'place' (a populus) with categories including 'people' per the session convention; identity and exact location uncertain.
The celebrated wooded gorge of the river Peneus in Thessaly, byword for idyllic beauty; Cicero jokingly calls the Reatines' valley 'their own Tempe' (Att. 4.15.5).

entities.canonical_name: Temple of Apollo (in the Circus Flaminius)
entities.summary: The temple of Apollo just outside the pomerium near the Circus Flaminius, where the Senate could meet to allow generals holding imperium (such as Pompey) to attend without crossing into the city. The setting of the February 56 BC Senate sessions on the riots.
(stub --- to be enriched)
Temple in the Forum, used in Cicero's day for public business and as a checking point for treasury account-balances; mentioned in Pro Quinctio §17.
Temple at the foot of the Capitoline beside the Forum; site of the Senate meeting of 5 December 63 BC at which Cicero delivered the Fourth Catilinarian.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
Temple at the foot of the Palatine, near the start of the Sacra Via; site of the Senate meeting of 8 November 63 BC at which Cicero delivered the First Catilinarian.
The temple of Mars outside the Porta Capena on the Via Appia at Rome, near the first milestone; Cicero uses it as a landmark for the flood of 54 BC.

The temple of the goddess Salus (Health/Welfare) on the Quirinal at Rome, dedicated in 302 BC; it stood next to Atticus's house. Its dedication day coincided with Tullia's birthday and the founding of the colony of Brundisium (Att. 4.1.4).
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)

The large plain region of central Greece (north of Boeotia, south of Macedonia), through which lay the main overland route from Epirus to Macedonia; young Marcus Cicero led cavalry through Thessaly in May 43 BC to join Brutus.
Capital of Macedonia; Cicero's residence for most of his exile in 58 BC, in the household of the quaestor Plancius.
A town on the gulf of Tarentum in Lucania, southern Italy, on the site of the old Greek colony of Sybaris; a stage on Cicero's flight toward Brundisium and exile in April 58 BC.
A river of Epirus (modern Kalamas) near Atticus's Buthrotum estate; the young Quintus Cicero insisted on going to see it on the homeward journey.
An inland town of Acarnania in western Greece where Cicero paused briefly on his homeward journey in 50 BC.

An ancient Latin town in the hills east of Rome (modern Tivoli), a fashionable site for the villas of the Roman elite. Reported in early 49 BC as one place Domitius Ahenobarbus might make for after his release at Corfinium.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A city of Caria in the province of Asia, on Cicero's route east; he reached it on 27 July 51 BC.
A town in Campania (Trebula Balliensis or Mutuesca); Matius wrote to Cicero from its territory while going out to meet Caesar at the start of the civil war.
A posting-station ('Three Taverns') on the Via Appia south-east of Rome near Forum Appii, a customary point for meeting travellers and exchanging letters (Att. 1.13.1).
(stub --- to be enriched)
Underground state prison near the Forum; place of execution by strangling for state criminals; site of the killing of Lentulus, Cethegus, and the other Catilinarians on the night of 5 December 63 BC.
Hill town near Rome; site of Cicero's favourite villa and the setting of the Tusculan Disputations.
A site in northern Syria where the Parthian prince Pacorus pitched camp after crossing the Euphrates in 51 BC, according to Tarcondimotus's dispatch to Cicero (Fam. 15.1). Location uncertain.
(stub --- to be enriched)
(stub --- to be enriched)
The great Phoenician port city on the coast of Syria. In Q.fr 2.11 a Tyrian embassy is given a hearing in the Senate, opposed by the Syrian tax-farmers.

A nearly deserted small town in the Pomptine marshes of Latium, proverbial for desolation; Cicero jokes that its 'constituents' are the croaking frogs of the marsh (Fam. 7.18.3) and worries over the 'people of Ulubrae' should Trebatius forsake public life (Fam. 7.12.2).
(stub --- to be enriched)

A coastal town in Liguria on the via Aemilia Scauri (near modern Vado Ligure); the point where Ventidius's column crossed the Apennines to join Antony in May 43 BC. Distinct from Vada Volaterrana in Etruria.
Coastal location in Etruria; the place where Quinctius was sighted by Publicius travelling to Gaul on 29 January 83 BC.

Ancient Etruscan city about 16 km north of Rome, captured by Camillus in 396 BC; its territory (ager Veientanus) was prime farmland. In Fam. 9.17 (46 BC) Caesar's commissioners are surveying the Veientine and Capenate land for veteran allotments, dangerously near Cicero's Tusculan villa.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A town in northern Campania/Samnium near Arpinum, famous for its olive oil; Cicero summoned the workman Cillo from there.
A town in Apulia on the Via Appia, birthplace of the poet Horace; Cicero passed through its territory returning home in October 47 BC. He writes Fam. 14.20 from the Venusian district.
A town of Cisalpine Gaul (modern Vercelli) in the upper Po valley, near the site of Marius's victory over the Cimbri in 101 BC; Decimus Brutus dates Fam. 11.19 from here in May 43 BC.
The trunk road of Cisalpine Gaul, running from Ariminum through Forum Gallorum and Mutina to Placentia. The Forum Gallorum fighting was strung out along its line (Fam. 10.30).
The great Roman road from Rome southward to Capua and beyond, the most prestigious highway of Italy; Cicero invokes it as the standard of orderly travel against the Asian roads.

The great northern highway from Rome to Ariminum on the Adriatic, built by C. Flaminius (censor 220 BC). At Att 1.1.2 Thermus's curatorship of its repair gives him political visibility useful to his consular canvass.
A road running down the Adriatic side of Italy through Apulia toward Brundisium. Cicero reports six cohorts from Alba going over to Curius by this route.
(stub --- to be enriched)
A local road in the Arpinum district by which Cicero travelled from the Manilian estate to the Fufidian property.
(stub --- to be enriched)

A town of Cisalpine Gaul (modern Vicenza) in the Veneto; its people (the Vicetini) had a dispute over home-born slaves that Decimus Brutus commends to Cicero's protection in the Senate. First appearance Fam. 11.19.
Principal city of the Allobroges in Transalpine Gaul (modern Vienne, France, on the Rhone); a major staging point for Plancus's army as it crossed the Rhone in 43 BC.

A public building on the Campus Martius used for the census, the levy, and the reception of foreign embassies. Cicero plans to join it to the marble Saepta he and Caesar's agents are building for the tribal elections.
Etruscan town; site of Sulla's camp during the closing campaign of the civil war (81 BC).

Cicero's dialogue on Academic (sceptical) epistemology, composed 45 BC and dedicated to M. Terentius Varro, who appears as an interlocutor; surviving in two partial recensions (Academica Priora/Posteriora).
A Roman tragedy (Ennius's Andromacha aechmalotis) staged at the games of 54 BC, in which the diminutive actor Antiphon performed (Att. 4.15.6).
Aristotle's treatise on dialectical commonplaces (topoi), the model on which Cicero based his own Topica for Trebatius (Fam. 7.19).

entities.short_name: On the Best Kind of Oratory
aliases: On the Best Kind of Oratory
entities.summary: Cicero's short essay on the best style of oratory, written as a preface to his (lost) translations of Demosthenes' and Aeschines' speeches On the Crown. He mentions it to Cornificius as a recent composition on which they may differ.
Cicero's lost autobiographical hexameter poem in three books on his consulship, exile and recall, completed ca. 55 BC; the speech of Jupiter mentioned in Q.fr. 2.7 closed its third book.

Cicero's speech in defence of King Deiotarus of Galatia, delivered before Caesar (who heard the case in his own house) in late 45 BC; Cicero here sends Dolabella a copy at his request, deprecating it as a slight piece (Fam. 9.12).
Lost Roman tragedy by Lucius Accius, on the murder of Agamemnon; revived at Pompey's games of 55 BC, with six hundred mules in the procession.

Cicero's prose memoir (commentarius / Greek hypomnema) of his consulship of 63 BC, composed first in Greek and sent to Atticus in 60 BC, with a projected Latin version; a self-promoting account of the suppression of Catiline. Distinct from the verse De Consulatu Suo.

Xenophon's quasi-historical prose work on the upbringing and reign of Cyrus the Great, idealizing him as the model just ruler and commander. Cicero says in Fam. 9.25 that he had worn the book to shreds with reading and now puts its lessons into practice as a provincial commander.

Cicero's lost Latin epic poem in three books on his own consulship, the "third poem" promised to Atticus in 60 BC; the source of the much-mocked line cedant arma togae. Distinct from the prose commentarius and from the later De Temporibus Suis.

Cicero's dialogue on the ideal orator (55 BC); its second book contains the long treatment of wit and jesting (de ridiculis) put in the mouth of the orator M. Antonius. Cicero cites it self-referentially in Fam. 7.32.

Cicero's dialogue on the ideal state (54–51 BC), in which the elder Scipio Aemilianus is the principal speaker and the surviving fragments include the portrait of the ideal statesman (rector/moderator rei publicae). Self-cited at Att. 8.11, where Cicero measures Pompey against the work's ideal.

The deliberative orations of Demosthenes urging Athens to resist Philip of Macedon, the model of the politically engaged speech. Cicero invokes them as the pattern for his own collected 'consular' speeches.
A Latin tragedy on the Electra myth written by Quintus Cicero in 54 BC, one of four he composed in sixteen days; Cicero asks him to send it.
(stub --- to be enriched)

Lost Roman tragedy (probably Naevius's), on the fall of Troy by the wooden horse; revived at Pompey's games of 55 BC, with three thousand mixing-bowls (creterrarum tria milia) in its triumphal pageant.
A literary work titled Erigona that Quintus had written (a tragedy, on the myth of Erigone) and sent to Cicero via Oppius; Cicero awaited it eagerly.

Comedy by Terence (161 BC); Cicero quotes the parasite Gnatho's advice to the soldier (lines 440-445) to illustrate his tit-for-tat motive in defending Vatinius. One of the most popular Roman comedies.

A lost political pamphlet by A. Caecina, written in exile after the Pompeian defeat; it attacked Caesar or lamented the state of the republic and was considered so dangerous that Caecina's son feared circulating it. It is repeatedly referenced in Fam. 6.5-6.8 and in Caesar's mild character as rendered in 6.6.8.

The collection of oracular verses kept by the quindecimviri at Rome and consulted in crises. In 56 BC a verse forbidding the senate to restore an Egyptian king 'with a multitude' (i.e. an army) was produced to block an armed restoration of Ptolemy Auletes, the 'religious scruple' Cicero reports.
A comedy by Terence; Cicero quotes a line from it ("a captive from Sunium") in a grammatical argument about whether Greek place-names are towns.
A treatise by the physician Nico on excessive eating, which Cicero playfully carried off from Sex. Fadius at Velia (Fam. 7.20).

Cicero's self-assembled corpus of twelve orations from his consular year (63 BC), gathered on the model of Demosthenes' Philippics. The umbrella collection; the individual speeches are listed at Att. 2.1.3.

Cicero's rhetorical treatise of 46 BC, dedicated to M. Junius Brutus, setting out the ideal orator's qualities and defending Cicero's own periodic Attic style against the fashionable Atticists. Referenced by Caecina in Fam. 6.7.4.
Platonic dialogue on love and rhetoric, set under a plane-tree by the Ilissus stream outside Athens. The model for the Tusculan setting of De Oratore.
Tragedy of the Greek hero Philoctetes; Sophocles's survives, Accius's and Pacuvius's are lost. Cicero's allusion in Q.fr. 2.8 is to the figure rather than to a specific dramatic version.

Plato's dialogue on justice and the ideal state, which Cicero takes as the structural model for his own De Republica. He cites its opening (Socrates at Cephalus's house in the Piraeus) to justify a dramaturgical choice.

Cicero's youthful verse rendering of the weather-signs section of Aratus's Phaenomena, part of his Aratea. He promises Atticus a copy alongside the consular speeches. FLAG: possible crossref to the Aratea.
A Latin tragedy on the Troilus myth written by Quintus Cicero in 54 BC, among the four he composed in sixteen days; Cicero asks him to send it.
A veteran Roman legion with a long history in Caesar's armies; recalled by Plancus's efforts along with other units, but feared to be susceptible to Antonian agitation in 43 BC.

One of two veteran legions that deserted Antony for the Republican cause in late 44 BC. It formed the shock element of the consular army and bore the brunt of the fighting at Forum Gallorum (Fam. 10.30).
One of the two veteran legions (with the Martian) that deserted Mark Antony for the Republican cause in late 44 BC, declaring Antony a public enemy. It fought in the Mutina campaign of 43 BC.
One of the two legions Mark Antony led out at the battle of Forum Gallorum on 14 April 43 BC (Fam. 10.30).
One of Caesar's legions in Gaul; in 51 BC rumour reported it had taken a beating during the campaign against the Bellovaci. One of the units that later followed Caesar across the Rubicon.
Asinius Pollio's most battle-hardened legion in Further Spain in 43 BC; he resisted pressure to surrender it because it was the core of his strength for the commonwealth (Fam. 10.31).
The Fourteenth Legion under Brutus's command in Macedonia in 43 BC; it was the unit in which C. Antonius fomented a mutiny through treachery, as reported in Ad Brutum 1.2.
One of the two legions Mark Antony led out at Forum Gallorum on 14 April 43 BC; it was routed by the Martian Legion on the Republican right wing (Fam. 10.30).
